name: Student Affairs at Vanderbilt
description:
Student Affairs serves a central role in student learning and development at Vanderbilt, advancing the University’s mission of teaching, research, and service by fostering academic and social networks through which students develop as intentional learners and global citizens. Student Affairs creates opportunities to involve students, faculty and staff in diverse learning communities and provides a critical support structure for enriching the overall student experience. We achieve these ends through strategic alignment, both internally and in concert with other university departments.

The Purposes of Student Affairs

Our core purposes are to:

  • Create a transformative college experience that dissolves the traditional barriers between in and out of classroom learning.
  • Enhance academic performance and intellectual excellence by building effective learning communities that emphasize the active engagement of students, faculty and staff.
  • Serve as a structural support system that fosters well being, security, safety, accessibility, capability, and encouragement, as well as provide the student with resources for belonging, recognition, contribution, and respect.
  • Promote healthy lives and balanced minds to ensure the well being of the Vanderbilt community and establish the foundation for individual learning and personal development.
  • Forge collaborative partnerships to achieve a seamless learning environment that links, aligns, and integrates programmatic and academic endeavors.
  • Expand opportunities for experiential learning, civic engagement, cultural enrichment, leadership development, and service learning.
  • Conduct ongoing assessments upon which to generate policies and programs that meet the changing needs and interests of students and to increase our capacity for doing so.
  • Provide intellectual challenge and increase the overall sense of community by generating dialog around issues of tolerance, justice, moral responsibility, and human dignity.
  • Espouse, embody and impart the values of the Community Creed within our residential community.
summary: Student Affairs serves a central role in student learning and development at Vanderbilt, advancing the University's mission of teaching, research, and service.

organizationType.name: Student Affairs Department, Office, or Program

name: Alpha Epsilon Pi
description:
Alpha Epsilon Pi is a fraternity with rich tradition and brotherhood. Founded at New York University in 1913, AEPi now extends to 175 chapters worldwide with a global membership of over 9,000 undergraduates and 102,000 lifetime members. Many brothers, such as Wolf Blitzer, journalist, and Mark Zuckerberg, founder of Facebook, have used their experiences in their school AEPi chapters to accomplish great things throughout America and the world.


The Tau chapter was founded at Vanderbilt University in 1929, and has always been an active part of the Vanderbilt Greek community. Our brothers place a very high value on grades and athletics. Most important to our brotherhood, however, are the friendships that we have built and the way that AEPi Tau has made all of our lives as students incredibly enjoyable and fulfilling. With a fresh start and a founding father class in 2021, we continue to pass on the esteemed tradition and pride of being a brother of Alpha Epsilon Pi.
summary: With a strong social presence, numerous individuals involved in student government and athletics, Alpha Epsilon Pi continues to make a huge impact in the Vanderbilt Community.

name: The Kappa Theta Chapter of Alpha Phi Alpha Fraternity, Inc.
description: Since its founding on December 4, 1906, Alpha Phi Alpha Fraternity, Inc. has supplied voice and vision to the struggle of African-Americans and people of color around the world. While continuing to stress academic excellence among its members, Alpha also recognized the need to help correct the educational, economic, political, and social injustices faced by African-Americans.  Alpha Phi Alpha has long stood at the forefront of the African-American community's fight for civil rights through leaders such as: W.E.B. DuBois, Adam Clayton Powell, Jr., Edward Brooke, Martin Luther King, Jr., Thurgood Marshall, Andrew Young, William Gray, Paul Robeson, and many others.
summary: Alpha Phi Alpha Fraternity, Inc is the first Inter-Collegiate Black Greek Letter fraternity dedicated to developing leaders, promoting brotherhood and academic excellence, while providing service and advocacy for our communities.

name: Alpha Tau Omega
description: The Beta Pi chapter of the Alpha Tau Omega Fraternity was founded in 1889 and has since developed a strong presence with over 70 current members, many of whom are involved in leadership positions in organizations throughout the Vanderbilt community.
summary: The Beta Pi chapter of the Alpha Tau Omega Fraternity was founded in 1889 and has since developed a strong presence with over 90 current members, many of whom are involved in leadership positions in organizations throughout the Vanderbilt community.

name: Beta Theta Pi
summary: The mission of Beta Theta Pi is to develop men of principle for a principled life. We try and achieve this through our five core principles: mutual assistance, cultivation of the intellect, trust, integrity, and responsible conduct.

name: Kappa Alpha Order
description:
Kappa Alpha Order is a men's fraternity founded to give members a more well-rounded college experience while encouraging students to be as successful as possible. The Chi Chapter was first established on 04/09/1883 at Vanderbilt University. This chapter is located in Nashville, TN. Members of KA place an intentional focus on leadership, scholarship, community service, and friendship.The philosophy of Kappa Alpha Order is that the acquisition of knowledge and the development of character are the great goals of life. Through fellowship and brotherhood, KA seeks to teach young men to be leaders; to be brave, self-reliant, and to continually strive to better themselves.
 
Kappa Alpha Order does not discriminate on the basis of race, creed, color, ethnicity, national origin, religion, sexual orientation, height, weight, physical or mental ability, veteran status, military obligations, and marital status.
summary: Welcome to Kappa Alpha Order. Our Order was founded on the knightly ideals of reverence, gentility, service, leadership, knowledge, perseverance and excellence.

name: Kappa Alpha Psi Fraternity, Inc.
description: The Nu Rho Chapter of Kappa Alpha Psi Fraternity, Inc. was chartered on February 25, 1989, with brothers having cross-pledged at Tennessee State University prior to this chartering. The Chapter remains dedicated to the Fraternity's commitment to achievement, and works hard to demonstrate this dedication in all that its members do.
summary: Kappa Alpha Psi was founded on the campus of Indiana University on January 5, 1911. The Fraternity's fundamental purpose is achievement, aspiring to raise the sights of collegians and stimulate them to accomplishments higher than they might have imagined

name: Kappa Sigma
description:
National Website: kappasigma.org

Since its founding at the University of Virginia in 1869, Kappa Sigma has initiated more than 245,000 men on college campuses throughout North America.  Today, the Fraternity has over 175,000 living alumni and more than 15,000 undergraduate members in 282 active chapters.  

Our proud history of producing campus leaders who go on to succeed in business, government, the armed services, sports, entertainment, and the arts has led to Kappa Sigma’s continuous and unparalleled growth across the continent. 

 At Vanderbilt, the Kappa Chapter of Kappa Sigma has somewhat of a unique history. Before moving on campus in 2008, we relied on the strength of our brotherhood to survive as an off-campus Fraternity. This commitment to each other and to our Fraternity lives on in the Kappa Chapter of Kappa Sigma.  Our members hail from all parts of the world, creating an incredibly diverse brotherhood.  Our members strive to live up to Kappa Sigma’s principles of Fellowship, Leadership, Scholarship, and Service by staying actively involved in Vanderbilt Student Government, Vanderbilt athletics, and many different student organizations.  Our annual philanthropic events include our fundraising drive for the Mary Parrish Center and various events that benefit the Military Heroes Campaign.  Our Fraternity hosts various social functions, including mixers with sororities, brotherhood events, and tailgates.
summary: This is the AnchorLink page for the Kappa Chapter of Kappa Sigma Fraternity.

name: Lambda Chi Alpha
description: Warren A. Cole founded Lambda Chi Alpha in 1909 at Boston University where he established the Alpha Chapter. Lambda Chi grew quickly, and in merging with Theta Kappa Nu, became one of the largest fraternities nationally. Today, there are 250,000+ living alumni and 190+ chapters. Here at Vanderbilt, the Gamma-Delta Chapter of Lambda Chi was established in 1922. Yearly, the brothers host a philanthropy event known as Watermelon Bust, benefitting food justice organizations in the Nashville area. In addition to this, brothers continually engage in community service and dedicate themselves to scholastic achievement. With the open mottos "Vir Quisque Vir" (Every Man a Man), "Per Crucem Crescens" (The Cross Through the Crescent), and "Kalepa ta Kalaa" (Nought Without Labor), Lambda Chi Alpha focuses on recruiting students who hold themselves to the the ideals of Loyalty, Duty, Respect, Service and Stewardship, Integrity, and Personal Courage.
summary: The Gamma-Delta Chapter of Lambda Chi Alpha was founded at Vanderbilt University in 1922, producing numerous men who have held themselves to the values of loyalty, duty, respect, service and stewardship, honor, integrity, and personal courage.

name: Phi Beta Sigma Fraternity Incorporated
description:
Phi Beta Sigma Fraternity was founded at Howard University in Washington, D.C. on January 9, 1914 by three young African-American male students. The Founders, Honorable A. Langston Taylor, Honorable Leonard F. Morse, and Honorable Charles I. Brown, wanted to organize a Greek letter fraternity that would truly exemplify the ideals of brotherhood, scholarship, and service.

The Founders deeply wished to create an organization that viewed itself as "a part of" the general community rather than "apart from" the general community. They believed that each potential member should be judged by his own merits, rather than his family background or affluence...without regard to race, nationality, skin tone or texture of hair. They desired for their fraternity to exist as part of an even greater brotherhood which would be devoted to the "inclusive we" rather than the "exclusive we".

From its inception, the Founders also conceived Phi Beta Sigma as a mechanism to deliver services to the general community. Rather than gaining skills to be utilized exclusively for themselves and their immediate families, they held a deep conviction that they should return their newly acquired skills to the communities from which they had come. This deep conviction was mirrored in the Fraternity's motto, "Culture For Service and Service For Humanity".

Today, Phi Beta Sigma has blossomed into an international organization of leaders. No longer a single entity, members of the Fraternity have been instrumental in the establishment of the Phi Beta Sigma National Foundation, the Phi Beta Sigma Federal Credit Union and The Sigma Beta Club Foundation. Zeta Phi Beta Sorority, founded in 1920 with the assistance of Phi Beta Sigma, is the sister organization of the Fraternity.

The Alpha Gamma Alpha Chapter of Phi Beta Sigma Fraternity was chartered on October 7th, 1994. Since its inception, the goal of the chapter has been to bring innovative, creative, and informative programming to the Vanderbilt community as well as setting the standard for the ideal fraternity man. A member of the National Pan Hellenic Council, Phi Beta Sigma is one of the nine historically black fraternities and sororities, eight of which are present on Vanderbilt's Campus. While our historical focus has been the aiding in the development of the African American community, all are welcome and invited to attend our programming and anyone meeting the requirements for membership is encouraged to apply.
summary: A member of the Vanderbilt Community since October 7, 1994, the Alpha Gamma Alpha Chapter of Phi Beta Sigma Fraternity aims to exemplify the ideals of Brotherhood, Scholarship, and Service and fulfill our motto: Culture for Service, Service for Humanity

name: Phi Delta Theta
description:
Originally one of the first fraternities on Vanderbilt’s campus in 1876, with 2,460 members within our alumni ranks, we continue to build on our legacy by creating a positive culture that is founded in strong brotherhood as we collectively pursue moral, academic, and intrapersonal excellence. 

As the Tennessee Alpha chapter, we have the unique opportunity to play a role in our chapter's philanthropy efforts. Which includes the LiveLikeLou Foundation’s $3.5 million Whole New Ballgame for ALS Research campaign. The campaign teams up with Vanderbilt University Medical Center (VUMC) and the Vanderbilt Baseball Team to establish new, pre-clinical, and innovative science for Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) and fund the region’s most significant investment in ALS discovery.
summary: The Tennessee Alpha chapter of Phi Delta Theta.

name: Phi Kappa Psi
description:
Join the Family, Join the Movement
 
--------------------------------------------------
Our fraternity is more than just a social organization, it's a family united by a shared purpose. We are dedicated to fostering a culture of inclusivity, where every member feels welcome, valued, and supported. We believe that diversity and acceptance are crucial for personal growth and positive impact on society. We strive to create an environment where everyone can be their true selves and have the opportunity to reach their full potential.
-
Our fraternity is also committed to promoting brotherhood, which is the foundation of our organization. We believe that brotherhood is about more than just friendship, it's about a deep sense of belonging and mutual support. We strive to create a sense of family among our members, and we believe that we can achieve great things through brotherhood.
-
Finally, our fraternity is dedicated to serving our community. We believe that we are responsible for giving back to the world around us, and we take pride in the various service projects and volunteer opportunities we undertake. We believe that by serving others, we can make a real difference in the lives of those around us.

              - Statement of Purpose (2023)
summary: Join the Family, Join the Movement

name: Sigma Alpha Epsilon
description: The Sigma Alpha Epsilon fraternity has been a model of success since the date of its conception in 1856. The fraternity’s inaugural chapter was the Alabama Mu chapter at the University of Alabama at Tuscaloosa. A group of intuitive Vanderbilt students were quickly attracted to the standard of excellence portrayed by the fraternity, thus leading them to found the second Sigma Alpha Epsilon chapter at Vanderbilt University in 1857. While originally constricted to the Southern states, the Sigma Alpha Epsilon fraternity inevitably extended its influence to the North, and is now considered a major organization in forty-nine states with more than two hundred fifty chapters. In just the last year, more than two thousand new initiates were invited to join among the ranks of over 300,000 active members. The Vanderbilt chapter, Tennessee Nu, strives to live up to and continue the precedent set by the organization’s commemorated past. Our fraternity stresses the importance of academics, athletics, and involvement in the community in order to mold well-balanced young men. Our chapter of Sigma Alpha Epsilon boasts not only the highest grade point average of any Greek organization at Vanderbilt, but of any Sigma Alpha Epsilon chapter in the country. In addition to our standard of academic excellence, our fraternity is actively involved in serving Nashville’s vibrant community. This includes cleaning parks, feeding the homeless, and conducting fund-raisers in order to preserve the well-being of our fraternity, University, and the city of Nashville. First and foremost, our fraternity is dedicated to fostering a group of young men and turning them into true gentlemen. Membership of the Sigma Alpha Epsilon fraternity does not end at graduation; it is a life-long commitment. Our goal as a fraternity is to prepare young individuals to prosper in our society and contribute to the tradition of excellence being currently upheld by the many alumni.
summary: The mission of Sigma Alpha Epsilon is to promote the highest standards of friendship, scholarship, and service for our members based upon the ideals set forth by our Founders and as specifically enunciated in our creed "The True Gentleman".

name: Sigma Nu
description: Sigma Nu Fraternity was founded at VMI in 1869. Sigma Chapter at Vanderbilt was founded in 1886. Our fraternity seeks to recruit men who embody our values of Love, Honor, and Truth.
summary: Sigma Nu Fraternity was founded at VMI in 1869. Sigma Chapter at Vanderbilt was founded in 1886. Our fraternity seeks to recruit men who embody our values of Love, Honor, and Truth.

name: Zeta Beta Tau
description: An IFC Fraternity committed to the four pillars of its Credo-Brotherly Love, Intellectual Awareness, Integrity, and Social Responsibility-as well as its motto, "a Powerhouse of Excellence."
summary: An IFC Fraternity committed to the four pillars of its Credo-Brotherly Love, Intellectual Awareness, Integrity, and Social Responsibility-as well as its motto, "a Powerhouse of Excellence."

name: Alpha Chi Omega
description: Alpha Chi Omega is a diverse group of women who value leadership, service, academic excellence, and sisterhood. Alpha Chi Omega supports domestic violence awareness nationally and our local efforts benefit the Mary Parrish Center, a transitional housing center for women leaving abusive situations. Alpha Chis love to support each other and have fun while staying true to our motto: "Together let us seek the heights!"
summary: Alpha Chi Omega is a diverse group of women who value leadership, service, academic excellence, and sisterhood. Alpha Chi Omega supports domestic violence awareness nationally and our local efforts benefit the Mary Parrish Center in Nashville.

name: Alpha Delta Pi
description: Be the First. Founded in 1851, Alpha Delta Pi was the first secret society established for women. ADPi sisters are everywhere on Vanderbilt’s campus, holding leadership positions in a wide variety of clubs and activities such as VUcept, Tour Guides, Dance Marathon, Next Steps, the Association of Latin American Students, and Vanderbilt Student Volunteers for Science. They pride themselves on their national philanthropy, the Ronald McDonald House Charities (RMHC), which houses the families of patients at Monroe Carell, Jr. Children’s Hospital. Sisters venture to the chapter house to make meals for the RMHC and into the Nashville Community to work with Pawster Nashville or BELL Gardens. During the fall, the ADPi philanthropy event raises thousands of dollars for the RMHC, as does ADPiHop in the spring. ADPi sisters love to hang out with each other, whether this means doing homework on the back porch, taking workout classes, or doing service together. Alpha Delta Pi’s open motto, “We Live for Each Other,” exemplifies their incredible sisterhood that they love sharing with their new sisters.
summary: Be the First. Founded in 1851, Alpha Delta Pi was the first secret society established for women.

name: Alpha Kappa Alpha Sorority, Inc.
description: Our purpose is to cultivate and encourage high scholastic and ethical standards, promote unity and friendship among college women, study and help alleviate problems concerning girls and women and to be SUPREME in service to ALL mankind
summary: Our purpose is to cultivate and encourage high scholastic and ethical standards, promote unity and friendship among college women, study and help alleviate problems concerning girls and women and to be SUPREME in service to ALL mankind

name: Chi Omega
description:
Our sisters demonstrate their passion for service through involvement with our national philanthropy, the Make-a-Wish foundation. We host fundraisers and dedicate service hours to the organization and the wonderful work they do for children with critical illnesses. Since 2020, we have been working with Thistle Farms, a Nashville nonprofit that provides stable housing and employment for female survivors of trafficking, prostitution, and addiction. Our chapter is grateful to have this means of supporting our local community in addition to our national philanthropic endeavors!

 

Membership in Chi Omega provides a support system that allows our sisters to be the best versions of themselves. By hosting resumé workshops, highlighting a weekly “smarty pants” in chapter, and promoting scholarship applications, Chi O bolsters women’s academic performance. Moreover, our chapter is home to women in important leadership roles across all corners of campus, with members in leadership positions in VUcept, Women in Computing, pre-professional fraternities, Dance Marathon, club sports, and more. Sisters shine in so many ways around campus, and our members love being there to cheer them on!

 

Chi Omega provides a support system for our sisters through the ups and downs of their college career. We’re happy to be there for each other in the fun, lighthearted moments when we dress up for date parties, eat family-style dinners together every Monday, and hang out with our big/little sisters. We’ve also worked hard to make Sigma Epsilon an inclusive community by appointing the Director of Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion to our executive board, engaging in difficult but meaningful conversations, and seeking opportunities to share our individual identities in an effort to educate each other, thus working towards a space where each member feels both accepted and appreciated. 

 

Chi Omega proudly calls itself an organization of both “sisters of purpose” and “sisters on purpose”. Vanderbilt’s chapter is no exception to this rule, as we’re proud to celebrate our shared values and deep-rooted sisterhood!
summary: The Sigma Epsilon Chapter of Chi Omega.

name: Delta Sigma Theta Sorority, Inc
summary: Delta Sigma Theta Sorority, Incorporated is an organization of college educated women committed to the constructive development of its members, and to public service, with a primary focus on the Black community.

name: Kappa Alpha Theta
description:
The Alpha Eta chapter of Kappa Alpha Theta was the first sorority established at Vanderbilt in 1904. Our members actively participate in all parts of campus life - in the classroom, on the stage, in student government, in special interest groups, service organizations, professional fraternities, and so much more. As Thetas, we value intellectual curiosity, leadership potential, commitment to service, and personal excellence. We support each other as we strive to be leading women. 

 

Alpha Eta is an award-winning chapter. Most recently, our chapter achieved Golden Kite status, the highest honor bestowed on college chapters by Kappa Alpha Theta headquarters. In addition, we received two Chancellor Heard Greek Awards in 2022: the Cultivating Member Engagement Award and the Social Action Award. 

 

Throughout the year, our chapter is involved in a number of philanthropy and service projects. Our signature philanthropy event, Theta BBQ, takes place each fall.The event raises money for Kappa Alpha Theta’s national philanthropy, CASA (Court Appointed Special Advocates). With a lively performance by our members and great food, it is our most anticipated event of the year! Also during the fall we celebrate Theta Day of Service. We love being able to join Nashville CASA for their Trunk-or-Treat Halloween event for children. One of our favorite activities is to invite our friends and neighbors to Theta Catered Lunches on our front porch where we share a meal and raise money for CASA! 

 

Our diversity, equity, and inclusion initiatives serve to promote learning, understanding, and affirmation. We publish a calendar of DEI initiatives and dialogues around campus to promote educational and multi-cultural events for our members to attend each semester. We also connect with other campus groups to learn about tough topics such as systemic racism in the criminal-legal system, discrimination, harassment in the workplace, sexual assault, women’s rights, and more.

 

Finally, Thetas love spending time with one another! We celebrate our sisterhood with programming like weekly chapter dinners and smaller family dinners, Big/Little mentors, networking seminars with alumnae, weekly Smart Cookie and Supportive Sister recognition, and exciting semi-formals. We also create a supportive environment for our members to achieve their personal and academic goals. 

 

From Bid Day to graduation and beyond, Kappa Alpha Theta provides a home for college women at Vanderbilt, and a family for life.
summary: Theta's rich history and strong bonds of sisterhood have made it an enduring member of the Greek community at Vanderbilt. Throughout the year, Theta is very involved in a number of professional and service activities on and around the Vanderbilt campus.

name: Kappa Delta
summary: Kappa Delta is a national sorority with more than 230,000 members. We are committed to fostering a sisterhood of confidence, community involvement, and genuine friendship.

name: Kappa Kappa Gamma
description: With 134 collegiate chapters and 300 alumnae associations, Kappa Kappa Gamma’s membership includes thousands of women, all of whom embrace Kappa’s values of goodness, truth, and loyalty. At Vanderbilt, Kappa Kappa Gamma is the home away from home for members who hail from over twenty states and five countries, bonded together by friendship, leadership, and scholarship. Kappa demonstrates a strong commitment to philanthropy and awareness of the needs of others through our fundraising events such as Pie a Kappa, which raises money to buy books for underprivileged children. Our members are diverse in interest and ability and are involved in a range of organizations that include Vanderbilt Student Government, VUCept, Dance Marathon, Varsity Athletics, and various cultural organizations.  Our members’ commitment to sisterhood and philanthropy is lived out through several events during the year that include movie nights, group work-out classes, parents’ weekend, and our annual philanthropy event Kappa Kappa Gala.
summary: With 134 collegiate chapters and 300 alumnae associations, Kappa Kappa Gamma's membership includes thousands of women, all of whom embrace Kappa's values of goodness, truth, and loyalty.

name: Pi Beta Phi
description: With a clear commitment to service and a culture that develops the whole person, Pi Beta Phi Fraternity is a values-based organization in which individual development is a key component of the mission and vision of the organization. Pi Beta Phi attracts women who possess a desire to be a part of a dynamic organization promoting  growth in all areas of life.
summary: The mission of Pi Beta Phi Fraternity for women is to promote friendship, develop women of intellect and integrity, cultivate leadership potential and enrich lives through community service.

name: Sigma Gamma Rho Sorority, Inc.
summary: Sigma Gamma Rho Sorority, Inc. was foundeded on November 12, 1922 at Butler University in Indianapolis, IN, by seven schoolteachers. Sigma Gamma Rho addresses concerns that impact society educationally, civically and economically.

name: Delta Delta Delta
description:
Hello and welcome to the Delta Gamma chapter of Tri Delta! Thank you for taking the time to explore our Anchorlink page and find out what life as a Tri Delta is all about. 

Tri Delta at Vanderbilt is an community of women with shared values where you can be yourself and belong to something bigger. We are brave, bold, and kind. We are passionate about serving others, committed to becoming the best version of ourselves and dedicated to helping women live, learn, and lead with purpose for a lifetime.

 

As a part of the Vanderbilt community for over one hundred years, our chapter is proud to have maintained one of Greek Life’s top GPAs and top fundraising totals, while having a great time doing it all! We look forward to another successful year, and can’t wait to continue to expand and improve.

Service and philanthropy are an important part of our sorority experience. Along with our annual dance show, Delta Underground, which benefits Monroe Carell Jr. Children's Hospital at Vanderbilt, our chapter also supports Tri Delta’s national philanthropy, St. Jude Children’s Research Hospital. Our spring philanthropy efforts are dedicated to this cause, and that combined with other fundraising efforts have raised over $170,000 in the last two years! Each spring, new members take a trip to Memphis, TN to tour the St. Jude Hospital and interact with patients and their families. Vanderbilt Tri Delta has been consistently honored as one of the top fundraisers among Tri Delta chapters across the country, and we plan to continue to raise our fundraising total this year.

Beyond our involvement through service, Tri Delta maintains a strong presence on campus throughout the year. At the 2019 Vanderbilt Greek Awards, we were awarded "Chapter of the Year". One of our proudest accomplishments was the $16,000 we raised for the Leukemia and Lymphoma Society during Sigma Chi Derby Days! With members on the varsity dance, cheerleading, soccer, golf and lacrosse teams as well as student leaders in almost every major student organization, it’s hard to miss Tri Deltas on campus.

We are proud to live our values every day and look forward to the next hundred years on Vanderbilt's campus!
summary: Founded in 1911, Tri Delta at Vanderbilt is a community of women who are brave, bold, and kind. Our members live out our motto "let us steadfastly love one another," everyday through our commitment to sisterhood, philanthropy, and living with purpose.

name: Zeta Phi Beta Sorority Inc.
description: Zeta Phi Beta Sorority, Incorporated was founded January 16, 1920 on the campus of Howard University in Washington D.C. by our five illustrious founders. We were founded on the principles of scholarship, service, sisterly love, and Finer Womanhood. The sorority's motto states that we are a community conscious, action-oriented organization. The Omega Pi chapter was chartered on Vanderbilt's campus by five dynamic women on March 18, 2000.
summary: To foster the ideas of service, charity, scholarship, civil and cultural endeavors, sisterhood and finer womanhood. These ideals are reflected in the sorority's national program for which its members and auxiliary groups provide voluntary service.

name: American Institute of Chemical Engineers
description: Since 1946, the Vanderbilt Student Chapter of the American Institute of Chemical Engineers has provided a platform of professional development opportunities for Vanderbilt University's undergraduate chemical engineering students.
summary: Since 1946, the Vanderbilt Student Chapter of the American Institute of Chemical Engineers has provided a platform of professional development opportunities for Vanderbilt University's undergraduate chemical engineering students.

name: American Society of Mechanical Engineers
description: The Vanderbilt University Chapter of the American Society of Mechanical Engineers (ASME) strives for excellence in all facets of the School of Mechanical Engineering for all of its constituents, mainly the student body. Being the only professional mechanical engineering society on campus, we are committed to helping our members and mechanical engineering students realize their potential and prepare them to be successful in a professional environment.
summary: Vanderbilt ASME strives to provide meaningful activities, resources, and connections for undergraduate Mechanical engineers.

name: Vanderbilt Association of Student Anthropologists
description: The Vanderbilt Association of Student Anthropologists is an interconnected network of undergraduate students who are interested in anthropology and anthropological research. VASA works to engage the Vanderbilt community and further education on the applications of anthropology. VASA also encourages and supports both personal and professional development within the field of anthropology.
summary: Vanderbilt Association of Student Anthropologists encourages anthropological conversation between students and faculty in a casual community, and provides opportunities to gain real-world anthropological experience.

name: American Society of Civil Engineers
description: ASCE engages students in weekly meetings, conference projects, and community outreach events to learn about the variety of work and innovation in the civil engineering field.  Weekly meetings bring in professional speakers from all walks of civil engineering, including transportation, sustainability, structural, environmental, water resources, and more.  Each year, we compete in the annual ASCE Mid-South Symposium against over 10 other schools in a variety of competitions, giving the chance for hands on experience in their intended career field.  Finally, we organize community outreach events to spread the good word about civil engineering.
summary: ASCE engages students in weekly meetings, conference projects, and community outreach events to learn about the variety of work and innovation in the civil engineering field.

name: Association of Biology Students
description: The Association of Biology Students aims to provide the undergraduate student population with a means of exploring future biomedical education and career options as well as promote awareness of educational opportunities relating to scientific research and its importance in the community. We also strive to enhance communication between students and faculty in the biological sciences as well as foster a community among students of the biological sciences.
summary: Our organization brings together students from across the field of biology to provide resources and opportunities to members and the community. We provide academic and career guidance to students interested in medicine, biology, and scientific research.

name: Biomedical Engineering Society
description:
This organization promotes the academic and professional development of biomedical engineering students by:

- promoting student research awareness

- identifying job opportunities and options

- aiding fellow students in technical elective selection

- promoting student-faculty interaction

- facilitating outreach locally at Vanderbilt, to the scientific community, as well as to alumni
summary: Vanderbilt Chapter of the National Biomedical Engineering Society

name: Chemical Biology Association of Students (CBAS)
description:
We are an organization that strives to promote professional and social relationships between students, post-doctoral fellows, and faculty in the chemical biology discipline at VU and VUMC.

Please email cbas@vanderbilt.edu if you'd like to be added to the CBAS listserv for up-to-date news and announcements!

__________________________________________________

 

NEWS AND UPCOMING EVENTS:

Thank you for a fantastic Fall semester of seminars!

Interested in presenting your research at CBAS seminar? Email cbas@vanderbilt.edu!

summary: We are an organization that strives to promote professional and social relationships between students, faculty, and post-doctoral fellows in the chemical biology discipline.

name: Chemical Engineering Graduate Student Association
description: The purpose of ChEGSA is to enhance the experience of graduate students within the department by engendering camaraderie among the graduate students and increasing student involvement in the department through student/faculty interactions.
summary: The purpose of ChEGSA is to enhance the experience of graduate students within the department by engendering camaraderie among the graduate students and increasing student involvement in the department through student/faculty interactions.

name: Neuroscience Majors Club
description:
Our mission is to foster a community among interdisciplinary Neuroscience majors and minors in the College of Arts and Sciences.  The Neuroscience Majors Club (hereafter referred to as Neuromajors Club) represents several hundred students currently enrolled in the interdisciplinary program, advocating for the addition of new courses (through resources in both the Psychology and Biology departments), petitioning for courses to be converted to NSC courses for the purposes of graduate school applications, and assisting in the search for research opportunities.  Our primary goal is to be the voice of those who choose to pursue a Neuroscience degree and to continue developing a rigorous academic major.  Our secondary goal is to assist Neuroscience program graduates gain recognition and acceptance from medical and graduate schools.  

We have a mentoring program that pairs older students in the Neuroscience degree program with students who are new to the program in order to help them pick classes, find research opportunities, discuss options for summer internships, etc.  Our aims for the coming year include preparing neuroscience majors with crucial knowledge on how to succeed at Vanderbilt and prepare them for graduate school, increase the neuroscience majors community and network, and reach out to the greater Nashville community and later promote the development of general body meetings that will enrich the experience of students in the department.
summary: Our mission is to foster a community among interdisciplinary Neuroscience majors and minors in the College of Arts and Sciences.  Our goal is to be the voice of those who choose to pursue a Neuroscience degree and to continue developing a rigorous major.

name: Society of Asian Scientists and Engineers
description:
SASE is dedicated to the advancement of Asian heritage scientists and engineers in education and employment so that they can achieve their full career potential.
 
Mission
 
SASE's mission is to:
- Prepare Asian heritage scientists and engineers for success in the global business world.
- Promote diversity and tolerance on campuses and in the workplace.
- Provide opportunities for its members to make contributions to their local communities.
 
Company Overview
 
The Society of Asian Scientists and Engineers (SASE) was founded as non-profit organization by Procter & Gamble employees in November 2007.

SASE is dedicated to the advancement of Asian heritage scientists and engineers in education and employment so that they can achieve their full career potential. In addition to professional development, SASE also encourages members to contribute to the enhancement of the communities in which they live.

 

Corporate Sponsors

Sponsors Include: 

Lead Sponsors:
First Energy
Shell

Organization Advisory Council:
Lockheed Martin
Booz Allen
Proctor & Gamble
Actalent

Full List of Sponsors: 2023 National Conference Sponsors and Supporters

 

SASE can be found on 106 collegiate campuses: Collegiate Chapters                                                              

SASE National website: SASE
summary: SASE is dedicated to the advancement of Asian heritage scientists and engineers in education and employment so that they can achieve their full career potential.

name: Society of Physics Students
description:
This club is for anyone with an interest in Physics!

Weekly meetings (with food) to discuss current research and physics topics. Occasional lectures and fun trips!
summary: The Vanderbilt University Chapter of the Society of Physics Students.

name: Society of Women Engineers
description: The mission of SWE is to empower women to achieve their full potential in careers as engineers and leaders; expand the image of the engineering and technology professions as a positive force in improving the quality of life, and demonstrate the value of diversity and inclusion. At Vanderbilt, we host many professional, service, and bonding events in order to provide a supportive community for women engineers.
summary: Empower women to achieve their full potential in careers as engineers and leaders; expand the image of the engineering and technology professions as a positive force in improving the quality of life, and demonstrate the value of diversity and inclusion.

name: Theta Tau
description: Theta Tau is a co-ed professional engineering fraternity built on three pillars: brotherhood, service, and professional development. By fostering a tight-knit community of engineering students, engaging in meaningful service projects, and providing opportunities for career growth, the organization aims to develop well-rounded engineers who excel both academically and professionally, while making a positive impact on society.
summary: Theta Tau is a co-ed professional engineering fraternity that emphasizes brotherhood, service, and professional development to cultivate well-rounded engineers dedicated to making a positive impact on society.

name: Vanderbilt History of Art Society
description: The HART Society is an undergraduate organization open to all interested students. Activities include: visits to local galleries and exhibitions; informational meetings for potential HART majors and minors; networking opportunities with HART alums; and workshops to guide students through the process of applying for internships and graduate school.
summary: The HART Society is an undergraduate organization open to all interested students. Activities include: visits to local galleries and exhibitions; informational meetings for potential HART majors and minors; and networking opportunities with HART alums.

name: Vanderbilt Math Club
description: Vanderbilt Math Club provides a place for those interested in mathematics to get to know each other on a more informal level outside of the classroom. Students can also find mentoring and learn about undergraduate opportunities.
summary: Vanderbilt Math Club provides a place for those interested in mathematics to get to know each other on a more informal level outside of the classroom. Students can also find mentoring and learn about undergraduate opportunities.

name: Undergraduate Honor Council
description: Since the first classes began at Vanderbilt, the Honor System has served to strengthen the academic integrity of the university. The Honor Council was formed to help enforce and protect the tradition of the Honor Code. Today, the Honor Council serves two simultaneous aims: to enforce and protect the Honor Code and to inform members of the Vanderbilt community about the Honor System. (http://studentorgs.vanderbilt.edu/HonorCouncil/)
summary: The Undergraduate Honor Council is run by students and supported by faculty advisers. The Executive Board and the general body, consisting of around 90 members, is entirely composed of students.

name: Vanderbilt Bar Association
description: VBA is the official student government organization of the Vanderbilt University Law School. VBA is responsible for things such as funding our various student organizations, organizing social and community service events, and serving as the channel of communication between the student body and the University. The organization's objectives are to foster a spirit of community and cooperation among students, faculty and staff, and to advance the aims and purposes of the student body at the law school.
summary: The VBA is composed of all students of Vanderbilt Law School. The organization's objectives are to foster a spirit of community and cooperation among students, faculty and staff, and to advance the aims and purposes of the student body at the law school.

name: Vanderbilt Student Government
description:
Vanderbilt Student Government (VSG) represents the interests of Vanderbilt undergraduates to university administrators, faculty, staff, and others in the Nashville and Tennessee communities. Our goal is to empower students to pursue their passions and feel at home in their college environment. In addition to lobbying administrators on the issues that matter most to students, VSG provides a wide range of student services, hosts a variety of events to enrich undergraduate life on campus, and partners with student organizations and university departments to sponsor unique programs that serve the student body as a whole.

Mission

Vanderbilt Student Government’s mission is to enrich and improve the undergraduate experience through proactive advocacy on behalf of students, collaborative partnerships with administrators and faculty, and relevant, innovative services.

Values

Tradition

We are committed to preserving and honoring Vanderbilt’s rich history while fostering new traditions.

Community

We are dedicated to engaging and supporting an increasingly diverse undergraduate community.

Equality

We are devoted to ensuring that all Vanderbilt students have equal opportunities to pursue a fulfilling undergraduate experience.

Integrity

We will be ethical, responsible and trustworthy student representatives who seek to reflect the Vanderbilt Honor Code in all of our actions.

Leadership

We will be passionate and effective student leaders who work to accomplish the goals of Vanderbilt Student Government.

Goals

To develop and implement relevant policies which address the interests and concerns of undergraduates:

  • We anticipate student needs and are proactive rather than reactive.
  • We involve students outside of Vanderbilt Student Government in the creation and implementation of policy.

To establish and maintain positive working relationships with university administrators and faculty:

  • We meet regularly with administrators and faculty to discuss current issues and initiatives relevant to undergraduates and the university.
  • Administrators and faculty consult us prior to university policy changes that impact the undergraduate experience.
  • We serve as respected representatives on university-wide committees.

To foster an environment that empowers, challenges, and develops student leaders:

  • We have elections that are competitive and where voter turnout is strong for all classes of undergraduates.
  • Our members are active in organizations beyond Vanderbilt Student Government.

To create avenues which encourage active engagement between students and student leaders:

  • Undergraduates engage our members in order to change and improve the quality of their undergraduate experience.
  • We are respected and valued by undergraduates and regarded as a leading student organization on campus.

To provide meaningful programming and services for undergraduate students independently or in cooperation with other student organizations on campus:

  • We include undergraduates outside of our organization in the development of campus programming.
  • Student organizations view Vanderbilt Student Government as a valuable partner in the promotion, staffing and funding of their events.
  • We have a deep and lasting relationship with the Residential Life Staff in the creation of programming for undergraduates.

To judiciously allocate student activity fees to student organizations:

  • Our process for allocating funds is well planned and easily navigable for student organizations.
  • We are recognized by undergraduates and student organizations for the fairness, thoroughness, and effectiveness of the activity fees process.
summary: Vanderbilt Student Government’s mission is to enrich and improve the undergraduate experience through proactive advocacy on behalf of students, collaborative partnerships with administrators and faculty, and relevant, innovative services.

name: Honor Council at Vanderbilt Law School
description:
The VLS Honor Council administers and regulates the VLS Honor Code which governs the academic conduct of students at Vanderbilt Law School. 

 

FREQUENTLY ASKED QUESTIONS

Submit Your Own Question Anonymously (further instructions provided therein) 

Q: If I am formally charged with an Honor Code violation will I have the full protection of legal counsel at Honor Council proceedings?

A.    No. The Honor Code does not provide for formal legal representation at Honor Council proceedings. Students are allowed to retain outside legal counsel but such an attorney is not allowed to be present at proceedings or to speak for the accused. Students facing formal charges are given the opportunity to choose an “Advisor” from the student body. Upon request, the Honor Council President will appoint an Honor Council member so serve as advisor. Article IV of the Code lists the sanctions a Trial Panel may impose, and the Honor Council Procedures provide a detailed outline of how allegations are handled from beginning to end.

Q: Can hearsay evidence be used against me at Honor Council proceedings?

A.    Yes. The Honor Code does not restrict the use of hearsay in Honor Council proceedings. The jury would be instructed, however, to consider the hearsay nature of the evidence in determining its credibility.

Q: My Torts exam is in a week and I am not sure if it is open book, closed book or a take home. Can I bring my E&E? What is the best way to figure out the rules? What if I am no longer able to contact my Professor?

A.    The best way to determine the official rules for an exam is to contact your professor directly. Email and office hours are a great way to both get to know your teachers and figure out the rules for an exam. If exam season is in full swing and students are no longer able to contact professors directly, contact the registrar’s office for clarification of the exam rules. If they do not know the answer to your question, they can discuss the issue with the appropriate faculty member.

Q: What exactly happens when you have to go before the Honor Council?

A. Students can be asked to come before the Honor Council under various circumstances. If a member of the law school community accuses a student of an Honor Code violation, the Accused student will be notified, and an investigation will ensue. There are several preliminary proceedings that take place before an Accused student is placed on Trial for violating the Honor Code and possibly subjected to Sanctions. Please refer to Article V of the Honor Code for a complete discussion of the Trial Procedures. Students may also be asked to cooperate in Honor Council proceedings as witnesses to a potential violation.

Q: What are common Honor Code violations?

A. The Honor Code is fairly expansive and can cover a broad range of activities. All students should be familiar with Article III of the Honor Code, which gives a non-exclusive list of violations. Usually, Honor Code violations fall under Plagiarism, Unauthorized Collaboration or Unauthorized Materials.

Q: Who do I talk to if I think I accidentally violated the honor code? Or if I think a friend has violated it?

A. As members of the student body, we have an obligation to one another to ensure that we are all operating on a level playing field and that the highest scholastic standards are being upheld. What may initially appear as a minor violation may ultimately have a serious impact on a lot of people. If you feel that you or someone else has violated the Honor Code, you should bring it to the attention of an Honor Council member as soon as possible. The Honor Council is in the best position to move swiftly to ensure that any negative impact is minimized.

Q: Can people be brought before the Honor Council for non-class related infractions? DUIs? Drug charges?

A. While the majority of Honor Code violations involve class-related behavior, the Honor Code does cover other activities. For example, it is an Honor Code violation to knowingly mislead an employer, which could occur during on-campus interview season. Criminal offenses, such as those mentioned above, are not typically covered by the Honor Code. Refer to Article III for a non-exclusive list of Honor Code Violations.

Q: Is it an Honor Code violation to use someone else’s outline for finals?

A: It depends on the rules of you instructor. A professor may or may not allow you to use another person’s outline on an exam, and a violation of your instructor’s policy would be an Honor Code violation. You should clarify your instructor’s position with regard to outlines before the Exam Period begins.

Submit Your Own Question Anonymously (further instructions provided therein)
summary: The VULS Honor Code regulates the academic conduct of students at Vanderbilt University Law School. The Code is regulated and administered by the VULS student body through the VULS Honor Council.

name: VUcept
description:
What is VUcept?

VUcept is a peer mentor organization, supported by Residential Colleges. VUceptors facilitate the cultural, social, and intellectual growth of Vanderbilt students within the living and learning community of The Ingram Commons through CommonVU Orientation, Vanderbilt Visions, and beyond. 

 

What is Vanderbilt Visions?

Vanderbilt Visions allows new students to discover college life together.  All first-year students are assigned to one of 90+ groups, led by a student and faculty VUceptor pair.  These groups of 16-18 first-year students first meet on Move-In Weekend, immediately forging strong connections and support networks that often last through graduation. Over the course of the fall semester, Visions groups meet on a weekly basis in order to discuss topics such as the first-year transition, diversity, equity, stress, and resilience at Vanderbilt.

 

Who are the VUceptors?

Faculty and student VUceptors are chosen because they are dedicated to (1) assisting and learning from first-year students, (2) introducing the newest Commodores to Vanderbilt’s traditions, and (3) fostering a brave and inclusive Vanderbilt community. Applying complimentary skills, faculty and student VUceptors model a dynamic and meaningful professor-student interaction and help to create a mutually beneficial learning environment for everyone.
summary: VUcept is a peer mentor organization that facilitates the personal and academic growth of Vanderbilt students within the living and learning community of The Ingram Commons through CommonVU Orientation, Vanderbilt Visions, and beyond.

name: Dores for Israel (DFI)
description:
Dores for Israel is Vanderbilt's Israel advocacy group on campus. DFI promotes a positive impact on the Israeli climate on campus through organizing educational, political and social programs.

DFI encourage discussions on Israel in an open and intellectual settings while drawing our red lines on illegitimate criticism of Israel: Delegitimization of Israel's right to exist, Demonization of Israel, and subjecting Israel to Double standards

DFI supports peace and a two-state solution that will be agreed by the Israelis and the Palestinians.
summary: Dores for Israel is Vanderbilt's Israel advocacy group on campus. DFI promotes a positive impact on the Israeli climate on campus through organizing educational, political and social programs.

name: The National Association for the Advancement of Colored People
description:
The Vanderbilt NAACP Chapter works diligently to implement programs and events that cater to our organization's national initiatives in order to seek justice and awareness of issues that affect communities of color.

We are organized into three committees in addition to the executive board: Political Activism, Social Justice, and Community Service, which each address issues relevant to the campus community and the greater Nashville area with a primary focus on marginalized groups.
summary: The mission of the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People is to ensure the political, educational, social, and economic equality of rights of all persons and to eliminate race-based discrimination.

name: Vanderbilt Students Demand Action
description: Vanderbilt Students Demand Action is part of a group of college and high-school students working together nationwide to end gun violence. Students Demand Action has teamed up with Everytown for Gun Safety, a movement of Americans working to build safer communities throughout the country. Our issues include addressing gun violence trends, domestic violence, illegal gun trafficking, suicide, the gun lobby, background checks, and unintentional gun deaths.
summary: Students Demand Action is part of a movement of Americans working together to end gun violence and build safer communities throughout the country. Our issues include gun violence trends, domestic violence, illegal gun trafficking, suicide, and more.

name: Sarratt Art Studios
description: The Sarratt Art Studios offers the Vanderbilt community the opportunity to take non-credit art classes to learn a new form of art or to continue an interest in art without the pressures associated with academic courses. The Sarratt Art Studios create a social environment that is friendly and supportive, and is comprised of students, Vanderbilt faculty, staff and Nashville community members.  The studio facilities are open daily and on weekends to students enrolled in our classes so they may have a place to practice their skills apart from their academic schedules, and pursue their interest in the arts. The Art Studios provide information regarding upcoming art events within the greater Nashville community. This year's Art Studio Committee has actively been working to develop new relationships with fellow student organizations, academic departments, and other programs throughout the campus community. We have also donated our time and space by offering free art activities for a multitude of campus wide events.  We are a space for Vanderbilt students (both in our classes and outside of them) to explore their creative interests and use art to relax in an often stressful academic environment.
summary: Sarratt Art Studios provides undergraduate & graduate students, faculty, staff, alumni, family members, and Nashville community members with a variety of non-credit art classes and opportunities to use the facilities offered in the Sarratt Student Center

organizationType.name: Student Affairs Department, Office, or Program

name: VAnime
description:
VAnime is the Vanderbilt student's one-stop spot for anime and manga. Our mission is to build and foster a community for those who enjoy anime, manga, and related aspects of Japanese culture. We shall do so by hosting a diverse range of activities, which include games, discussions, events, and collaborations with other organizations.
 
We hold weekly meetings.
summary: VAnime is the Vanderbilt student's one-stop spot for anime and manga. Each week, we hold a large array of events to create a community for those who enjoy anime, manga, and related aspects of Japanese culture.

name: Vanderbilt Business Review
description: The Vanderbilt Business Review is a publication whose members write on a variety of topics that interest them, including the macroeconomy, startups, technology, and on-campus news. As the only undergraduate business publication, we service the Vanderbilt community by providing business coverage with a student focus.
summary: The Vanderbilt Business Review is publication whose members write on a variety of topics that interest them. As the only undergraduate business publication, we service the Vanderbilt community by providing business coverage with a student focus.

name: Owen Global Business Association
description:
The Global Business Association strives to serve as the official link between Owen and the world of global business where we live and work. The mission of the Global Business Association is to

  1. Provide tangible value to one’s Owen experience through initiatives aimed at increasing awareness about globally focused academic and career environments
  2. Ease international student transition to a new culture by providing a sense of community and additional programming to support recruiting efforts

These initiatives include a fun, enriching speaker series aimed at promoting the importance of global awareness in business, international dinners, sessions on how to network as international students, the Owen Bridges program and much more.
summary: The Global Business Association (GBA) strives to link Owen to the world of global business where we live and work. Our vision is to provide tangible value to your Owen experience by increasing awareness about globally focused business environments.

name: Human and Organizational Performance Association
description: The Owen Human and Organizational Performance Association (HOPA) is a student-run organization focused on providing support and opportunities to those interested in HR or Human Capital Consulting roles, but open to anyone who wants to learn more about the field.  The mission of HOPA is to foster and support the professional development of prospective, current and alumni members of the HOP concentration. We strive to provide our members with the tools and knowledge necessary to succeed once we leave the structured environment of Owen. Throughout the year, we offer opportunities to learn and network through the HOP Symposium, Recruiting workshops, Lunch & Learns, and social events with like-minded students and alum.
summary: Our mission at HOPA is to foster and support the professional development of prospective, current and alumni members of the HOP concentration at the Owen Graduate School of Management.

name: Operations Management Club, Owen Graduate School of Management
description: As a professional club our mission is to enhance class learning and professional development for those interested in operations and analytics. Our goal is to support students in their learning and understanding of the vast opportunities in the field of operations and analytics through events and programs like lunch and learns, recruiting information sessions, career outlook discourses and fun industry tours.
summary: The Operations Management Club at Owen is a club that enhances in-class learning and professional development by organizing lunch and learn panels with industry leaders, recruiting treks and tours to companies across multiple industries.

name: Owen Armed Forces Club
description:
The Armed Forces Club seeks to provide a networking platform for current students who have served or are currently serving in the armed forces of their respective countries. We help members access veterans and alumni in corporate leadership positions throughout the country and abroad. We serve as a resource to prospective veteran students to assist with and answer questions about the Owen application process. Our events include guest speakers, socials, networking events, and the Owen Veterans Day celebration.

Note: Anchor Link membership is for current students only. If you are a prospective student and need assistance, please email us at armedforcesclub@vanderbilt.edu.
summary: The Owen Armed Forces Club represents all Owen Graduate School of Management students who have an interest in leadership and/or the military and provides a networking platform for students, alumni, and prospective candidates.

name: Owen Christian Business Association
description: The Owen Christian Business Association (CBA) exists to foster fellowship and spaces for students to learn and discuss the intersection of faith and business. This club provides opportunities for spiritual exploration and growth through guest speakers, regular club meetings, community service projects, and special events. We not only aim to create a tight-knit community here at Vanderbilt but to prepare students to lead as Christians in the business world.
summary: The Owen CBA exists to provide a forum for students to actively pursue faith, fellowship, and friendship through belief in Jesus Christ. It provides a platform to learn and discuss the intersection of faith and business among students at Owen.

name: Owen Strategy and Consulting Club
description:
The Owen Strategy and Consulting Club (OSCC) is a student-run organization for those with interests in consulting. Our goal is to help students gain a better understanding of the work culture, required skills, and career opportunities in consulting related fields. The club also facilitates networking opportunities and prepares its members for careers in consulting through workshops and case practice sessions. Events like the Consulting Symposium, case prep sessions, mock interviews, guest talks, treks, and workshops with alumni provide an invaluable set of opportunities that members can leverage.

Why join OSCC?

The consulting industry is growing and intensely competitive. The OSCC, with its vast resources of members, faculty, and alumni, combined with the efforts of the career center, will help the members pursue their dream careers successfully. Structured case analysis, problem solving, decision-making, and interaction with top firms in the industry are extremely useful skills and experiences for all MBA students who will step out of Owen as future leaders of the industry.
summary: The Owen Strategy and Consulting Club is targeted toward Owen students with interests in strategy and consulting ranging from consulting firms, internal consulting, and leadership development programs.

name: Owen Culinary Society
description: Owen student club
summary: Owen Culinary Society brings students together who have a passion for cooking and eating! We explore Nashville and the surrounding areas to discover new places on the food scene, and we cook to show off our skills to the Owen Community.

name: Owen Finance Club
description: The OFC provides a forum for students to actively pursue finance-related interests including: (1) education and training, (2) career development and (3) personal network development in an educational-friendly environment that fosters a rich exchange of learning opportunities.
summary: The OFC provides a forum for students to actively pursue finance-related interests including education and training, career development and personal network development in an educational-friendly environment.

name: Owen Partners Association
description:
Because pursuing a business school education at Vanderbilt is such a significant joint decision, we feel it is important to support and include all who are involved. Partners are always welcome at Owen events and gatherings, but OPA is an additional opportunity for partners to connect with each other outside of the school.

OPA organizes social events throughout the school year. These events allow partners to get to know each other, make friends, and learn about Nashville. All partners and families are encouraged to participate in these events, as well as other Owen social events and clubs.

It is an exciting time for you and your significant other, and we are thrilled to have every partner as a part of the Owen family.

Additional information can be found on the Owen website.
summary: The Owen Partners Association (OPA) is the official club that helps connect and integrate spouses, partners, and families to the Owen and Vanderbilt communities.

name: Owen Women's Business Association
description:
The Owen Women's Business Association (WBA) strives to create an inclusive and empowering environment for all women within the Vanderbilt University Owen Graduate School of Management. The WBA promotes and supports women in all Owen programs by fostering community, encouraging personal development, and providing opportunities for professional development.

The WBAʼs resources and events are designed to be inclusive and accessible to all students at Owen who would like to participate. Additionally, membership is automatically extended to all women of Owen, and the WBA purposely does not require an application or interview to join in order to promote an inclusive environment and remove any barriers that may limit one’s access to joining the organization. The WBA promotes diversity, equity, and inclusion in all programming through a variety of educational workshops, mentorship opportunities, lunch and learns, community service events, partnerships, and social events.
summary: The Owen Women's Business Association (WBA) strives to create an inclusive and empowering environment for all women. We support the women of Owen through professional networking events and provide opportunities for personal and professional development.

name: Owen Real Estate Club
description:
The Real Estate Club is organized to:

  • Foster understanding of the role of real estate,
  • Share knowledge of careers in real estate,
  • Provide opportunities for students to exhibit knowledge and skills to employers,
  • Encourage and facilitate development of relationships among Owen students and real estate companies in the Nashville community and across the country.

 

 To achieve each of these operational objectives, the Real Estate Club will host informational meetings, sponsor guest speakers, and encourage students to get involved with real estate projects with companies in the greater Nashville community.
summary: In general, the Owen Vanderbilt Real Estate Club is intended to be a student group focused on objectives determined by its members.

name: Owen Marketing Association
summary: The OMA's goal is to further develop the marketing skills of all members by extending learning outside the classroom, collaborating with faculty and alumni, and increasing the number of tools which are vital to receiving a top-notch job or internship.

name: African Student Union
summary: Our mission is to promote cultural awareness about the African diaspora within the organization and the Vanderbilt community. As a group we strive to foster unity among students at Vanderbilt by embracing the diversity that exists in our community.

name: Asian American Student Association
description:
The Asian American Student Association’s purpose is to promote awareness of Asian and Asian-American cultures and issues within the University, the local community, and beyond, in order to foster a campus community more inclusive to AAPI identities. Founded in 1986, AASA strives to establish a sustaining culture of activism regarding Asian and Asian-American identity, moving beyond established stereotypes and providing a platform for students to celebrate their heritage and find their voice for change.

There are four major focuses of our programming: 

1. Recruitment & Social: including Freshman & Transfer Mixers, Family Systems & Reveal, and Social events

2. Asian Pacific Islander Desi American (APIDA) Heritage Month: celebrated throughout October; usually a series of events including our signature Night Market and Taste of Asia 

3. Advocacy: year-long event series of dialogues and workshops, collaborating with other cultural organizations, to promote awareness of issues and topics across the Asian diaspora

4. Lunar New Year Festival (LNYF): largest cultural showcase on campus celebrating cultures in the Asian & Asian American Diaspora with cultural dances and performances
summary: Our purpose is to promote and provide a cultural and political awareness of Asian and Asian American identity and diversity within the University, local community, and beyond.

name: Asian Pacific American Law Student Association
description: APALSA aims to provide cultural, social, and academic support to students of Asian heritage and friends of the Asian legal community at Vanderbilt.
summary: APALSA aims to provide cultural, social, and academic support to students of Asian heritage and friends of the Asian legal community at Vanderbilt.

name: BhangraDores
description: The BhangraDores is Vanderbilt's premier competitive, co-ed Bhangra dance team. The purpose of the Vanderbilt University BhangraDores (BD) is to promote cultural awareness and aesthetic appreciation of the Punjabi folk dance form that is Bhangra. The BhangraDores commit multiple hours to practices and meetings which culminate in on-campus performances and performances in the broader Nashville community. Additionally, BD competes in the national Bhangra circuit and has seen much success at competitions across the country in the past.
summary: Vanderbilt's Bhangra team

name: Black Student Association
description:
The purpose of the BSA is to:

 

Provide support for our membership through academic programming and extra-curricular activities;

 

To expose the greater Vanderbilt community to Black culture with cultural programming;

 

Increase the Black presence within the Vanderbilt Community through leadership development and through involvement in outside student organizations;

 

Provide students an avenue where they might positively contribute to the Vanderbilt and Nashville community through service and service based learning, and;

 

Support and promote the recognition of the significant contributions of different ethnic and cultural heritages.
summary: The Black Student Association is dedicated to the social and academic uplift of Black students with a focus on educating campus about issues of diversity and inclusion, mainly those affecting Black and African American students.

name: Caribbean Students Association
description: The goals of the Caribbean Students Association (CSA) are to promote pride and awareness of the Caribbean culture amongst its members and the Vanderbilt Community at large; to provide strong links among the Caribbean Students and the Faculty at Vanderbilt; and to keep the Vanderbilt Community informed on events and issues that affect the Caribbean. All of these goals shall be accomplished through a variety of extra-curricular activities aimed at fulfilling intellectual, social, and cultural exchange.
summary: The mission of CSA is two-pronged: to promote cultural pride, and to act as a medium of public dissemination of cultural, political and historical information about the Caribbean nations to Vanderbilt University

name: International Law Society
description:
The International Law Society is open to all students at the Law School, whether or not they are concentrating in transnational studies. Organized in 1966, ILS is intended to foster interest in transnational legal studies, and to provide an opportunity for all students to inquire into transnational problems outside the formal classroom atmosphere.

 

Everyone is encouraged to join the International Law Students Association, information for which can be found under the "News" tab.
summary: The International Law Society is intended to foster interest in transnational legal studies, and to provide an opportunity for all students to inquire into transnational problems outside the formal classroom atmosphere.

name: Vanderbilt Japanese Cultural Association
description: The Vanderbilt Japanese Cultural Association is committed to providing the Vanderbilt community with genuine experiences of Japanese culture. JCA arranges panel discussions on the future of US-Japan relations and hosts cultural events such as food fairs and traditional Japanese festivals. The executive board gathers weekly to organize and manage these events, occasionally collaborating with other cultural clubs.
summary: The Vanderbilt Japanese Cultural Association is dedicated to sharing authentic Japanese culture and cultural encounters with the Vanderbilt community through cultural events and discussions on the future of US-Japan relations.

name: KOREAN GRADUATE STUDENTS AND SCHOLARS ASSOCIATION
summary: This is an organization for Korean graduate students and scholars at Vanderbilt University to interact and participate in various social events.

name: Korean Undergraduate Student Association
description: Vanderbilt Korean Undergraduate Student Association is an organization where members promote the diversity of the Vanderbilt campus by promoting and sharing the cultural and ethnic features of Korean and Korean American students.
summary: Vanderbilt Korean Undergraduate Student Association is an organization where members promote the diversity of the Vanderbilt campus by promoting and sharing the cultural and ethnic features of Korean and Korean American students.

name: La Alianza at Vanderbilt Law School
shortName: La Alianza at Vanderbilt Law School
description: La Alianza is the Latinx/Hispanic law student Association at Vanderbilt Law School. La Alianza works to increase the presence of Latinx/Hispanic students and culture at Vanderbilt Law and to provide a support system for our current students. La Alianza also strives to address the growing interest of law students in the legal issues affecting Latinx people in the United States, legal issues in Latin America, and the relationship between Latin America and the United States. Our goals are to provide social and cultural activities, to implement charitable and educational programs, promote leadership, and serve as a voice for the Latinx/Hispanic community at Vanderbilt Law. We are focused on supporting, mentoring, and organizing Latinx/Hispanic law students and other students in order to help them thrive in the law school community by supporting their interests and future goals. We welcome all law students to participate in La Alianza!
summary: La Alianza is a platform to offer support and a voice for Latinx/Hispanic students at Vanderbilt Law School. La Alianza offers an opportunity to support law students interested in exploring legal issues facing Latinx people in the U.S. and Latin America.

name: Vanderbilt Lambda Association
description:
Lambda, officially recognized by Vanderbilt University as the Vanderbilt Lambda Association, is intended to reach out to and serve the needs of lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, queer, questioning, asexual, aromantic, and otherwise gender- or sexuality-variant (LGBTQ+) members of Vanderbilt’s community. The organization strives to recognize the diversity of the Vanderbilt community by dispelling stereotypes and misconceptions while addressing issues of discrimination and inequity. It is also a goal of Lambda to encourage the support and involvement of all people in the Vanderbilt community. The organization addresses the needs of all students in the process of discovering identity, functioning as both a support group for those who are struggling with issues of identity and as a vehicle by which students can be vocal in the fight for equality. The organization is committed to involving and connecting members of the LGBTQ+ community with resources both on and outside of campus.
 
As the University’s Gender and Sexuality Alliance, the Vanderbilt Lambda Association takes great pride in being a leading promoter in the awareness of diversity among the student body. Lambda offers a space on campus for students of all backgrounds, cultures, races, beliefs, sexualities, gender identities, and gender expressions to respectfully exchange ideas and to experience the variety of belief systems Vanderbilt has to offer.
