By instilling these traits in young people, Alpha Phi Omega will not only help them to reach out to their communities while in college, but also equip them with the values that will make them the future leaders and citizens of this nation.
summary: Alpha Phi Omega is a national, co-ed service fraternity that has set the standard for college campus-based volunteerism since 1925. We strive to help each individual member develop leadership skills and experience meaningful friendship and service.

name: Alternative Spring Break
description:
Visit us at our website here!  
Stay up to date by following our instagram @vanderbiltasb

Vanderbilt Alternative Spring Break is a student-run service organization that empowers students from all corners of Vanderbilt’s campus in an ongoing process of education, service, and reflection. Leading up to the week of service, each site meets to learn history, relevance, and important context regarding its theme. Participants are equipped with principles of social justice in order to engage with a specific issue, from healthcare equity to species conservation. This experience has the potential to transform participants’ views of themselves, their Vanderbilt community, and the world. 

 

OUR MISSION

Vanderbilt Alternative Spring Break brings together students and empowers them to progress towards active citizenship through education, service, and reflection. As an organization, we encourage members to reexamine the past, present, and future role social justice may play in their lives, communities, and personal relationships.
summary: ASB is Vanderbilt's largest student-run organization and the premier alternative break program in the nation. For more info, visit our website: my.vanderbilt.edu/vandyasb/!

name: American Red Cross at Vanderbilt
description: American Red Cross at Vanderbilt is committed to extending the mission of American Red Cross to Vanderbilt and the Nashville community as a whole. We work closely with the Nashville Chapter of the Red Cross to provide training opportunities and sponsor events, including the multiple blood drives we sponsor on campus each year. We aim to coordinate Vanderbilt's role in disaster relief services as well as ensure that students are prepared to prevent and respond to emergencies. Additionally, we work closely with other service organizations on campus and in the community throughout the year through co-sponsorships and other activities.
summary: American Red Cross at Vanderbilt is committed to extending the mission of ARC to Vanderbilt and the Nashville community by working in cooperation with the local ARC Chapter and other organizations to offer valuable educational and service opportunities.

name: Best Buddies
description: Best Buddies is a global volunteer movement dedicated for forming one-to-one connections within communities of individuals with intellectual and developmental disabilities (IDD). Student peer buddies are matched with buddies who have IDD for one-on-one friendships. Best Buddies also hosts monthly group events that have included holiday parties, trivia, and karaoke. Complete your Membership Application to join today!
summary: Best Buddies is an organization dedicated to establishing a global volunteer movement that creates opportunities for social friendships, integrated employment, and leadership development for people with/out intellectual and developmental disabilities.

name: Vanderbilt Global Brigades
description:
Global Brigades is an international non-profit that uniquely implements a holistic model to meet a community’s health and economic goals. Our model systematically builds community ownership and collaboratively executes programs with the end goal of sustainably transitioning to a relationship of impact monitoring. Our mission is to empower volunteers and under-resourced communities to resolve global health and economic disparities. Our vision is to improve equality of life by igniting the largest student-led social responsibility movement on the planet. www.globalbrigades.org

 

What sets Global Brigades apart from other programs? 

1. Funded through Student Movement

Global Brigades funds all of its programs, leadership development and administrative costs through the fundraising efforts of more than 500 Global Brigades University Chapters around the world. This grass-roots fundraising effort gives us the autonomy to develop programs to the needs of communities.

 

2. Community-Led Sustainability

All of our projects include community member involvement, formation of committees and long term funding streams for future sustainability. We believe that the empowerment of local leaders is the only way that programs will continue to be maintained and grow once Global Brigades transitions its partnership with a community to follow-up.

 

3. Full-Time Local Teams

Global Brigades employs more than 100 full-time local staff members and over 300 temporary local staff members year-round. Our local staff members are the driving force behind program development, brigade coordination, community follow-up and impact measurement. We believe that programs should be locally led.

 

4. Government Collaboration

We believe in working with local governments instead of against or in replacement of. Our projects could not be possible without the support of local governments. The local governments are involved with each of our projects in varying degrees such as offering support in funding, transportation of supplies, equipment, and/or vocalizing support.
summary: Global Brigades is the world’s largest student-led global health and sustainable development organization. Our mission is to empower volunteers and under-resourced communities to resolve global health and economic disparities. www.globalbrigades.org

name: InterAxon
description:
Vandy Interaxon is a service organization at Vanderbilt University, established Fall 2011. We visit Nashville-area middle schools weekly in order to expose students to the study of the brain. Through fun, interactive lessons, we teach middle-school basic neural concepts, including the anatomy of a neuron, functions of different brain regions, and disorders of the nervous system.

Our goal is to introduce students to the significance of the nervous system in everyday life and to foster an interest in the study of neuroscience.

Please contact us for more information if you are a student interested in joining, or a school official interested in our visits!
summary: InterAxon is a STEM service-based organization, centering around neuroscience and cognitive sciences. Our goal is to introduce local Nashville middle school students to the significance of the nervous system in everyday life.

name: Moneythink
description: Moneythink is a national student movement aimed at rejuvenating urban America by mentoring the next generation of America's small business leaders. We believe that by selecting, training, and deploying talented college students to mentor urban high school students in financial literacy, life skills, and entrepreneurship, we can create a generation of financially responsible consumers and entrepreneurial problem solvers. Moneythink accepts new members for both the fall and spring semesters, so please contact us if you are interested in supporting our work.
summary: Vanderbilt Moneythink is a mentorship program that partners with Nashville high schools to educate students on topics ranging from financial literacy to pre-professional activities.

name: Vanderbilt Operation Smile
description: Vanderbilt Operation Smile strives to build awareness, raise funds and educate others regarding cleft lip and cleft palate treatment within the University, the local community, and beyond. Operation Smile aims to create solutions that deliver safe surgery to people where it’s needed most. Students will be able to support this mission through fundraising and service projects that focus on the four pillars of leadership, service, advocacy, and education. Vanderbilt Operation Smile aims to foster community between members of the Vanderbilt community and others who share an interest in serving others who suffer from cleft lip and palate.
summary: Vanderbilt Operation Smile strives to build awareness, raise funds and educate others regarding cleft lip and cleft palate conditions. We raise money for our Smile Fund, partner with VUMC, host philanthropic events, and educate the community.

name: Begin with Books
description:
Join Begin with Books to become a virtual literacy tutor for elementary students in Metro Nashville Public Schools through the Y-Literacy program. No experience with tutoring necessary. Training and materials are provided. Tutoring times are flexible with your schedule. 

Alongside tutoring, Begin with Books serves a purpose of instilling a lifelong love of reading and literature in young people and encouraging literacy in people of all ages. We seek to educate ourselves on current barriers to literacy in the Nashville area and beyond.

Begin with Books hosts reading and book-related events such as book drives, on-campus literacy awareness events, and events with literary partners across Nashville.

Begin with Books is open to all Vanderbilt students. If you would like to join, please send us a message through Anchor Link or email Sophia Earle (sophia.g.earle@vanderbilt.edu). Thank you!
summary: Begin with Books seeks to instill a lifelong love of reading and literature in young people through becoming virtual literacy tutors for students in Metro Nashville Public Schools.

name: Relay For Life
description:
Vanderbilt Relay for Life is a street fair-style celebration of life and hope that one day we will find a cure for cancer. Take a moment to connect with the Commodore and greater Nashville community and honor our cancer survivors and caregivers. No matter who you are, there's a place for you at Vandy Relay. All proceeds directly benefit the American Cancer Society (ACS). Each dollar you raise will help save lives. How far will you go to make a difference in the fight against cancer? Your reasons for walking at Vanderbilt Relay For Life are as unique and special as the story that motivates you. Vanderbilt Relay for Life's ultimate goal is to raise cancer awareness on campus.

 

The 2026 Relay for Life will occur November 8th, 2026, from 12 PM - 7PM in the SLC Ballroom. Groups of students, staff, families, or friends form Relay teams and fundraise throughout the entire calendar year culminating their efforts at the Relay event where there will be food, activities, music, speakers, and lots of fun for everyone. Remember, people make the teams, teams make the event, and the event changes everyone! This is your opportunity to not only honor cancer survivors and remember people we have lost, but also to raise funds and awareness to help save lives. Because of your support, we are creating a world where cancer can't claim another year of anyone's life. You are helping create a world with less cancer and more birthdays!
 
Did you know? Vanderbilt Relay for Life is one of America's top collegiate fundraising chapters!! Additionally, every year, there are over 4 million Relay participants and over 5,000 Relay For Life events, which take place in more than 32 countries around the world. Not only has Relay for Life inspired millions, but it has also raised over $4 billion for ACS!

Visit relayforlife.org/vandy for more information on ways to get involved and for the link to our fundraising page on the American Cancer Society website. You can also follow us @vandyrelay on Instagram for all exciting updates and to see how our fundraising makes an impact for ACS!
summary: Relay for Life's mission is to celebrate those who have survived cancer, to remember those who have lost their lives to cancer, and to fight back for a cure. Relay for Life is an all-day, life-changing event that raises cancer awareness on campus.

name: Commodore CARES Coalition
description: The Commodore CARES Coalition is a joint effort between Nashville CARES and Vanderbilt University students. We focus on getting students to volunteer, we encourage a positive view of sex on campus, and teach about HIV/AIDS prevention and safe sex for everyone. We also work on activism and we teach students about laws related to sex and the rights of people with HIV/AIDS. Our goal is to create an informed, welcoming, and active campus community at Vanderbilt University, where students are involved in health education and stand up for important issues.
summary: Our mission is to empower students through volunteerism, to advocate for sex ed in schools and for those who live with HIV/AIDS, to promote sex positivity, and to educate on HIV/AIDS prevention, and safe sex practices for all sexualities

name: Vanderbilt Prison Project
description:
Together for Justice. 

Founded in 1972, Vanderbilt Prison Project is a service-based organization that aims to educate, equip, and inspire the Vanderbilt student body to work with the Nashville community for progress in the criminal justice system, and to serve, advocate for, and uplift those impacted by it.  

 

GroupMe Link: https://groupme.com/join_group/108004421/DJx1jll7
summary: A service-based organization that aims to educate, equip, and inspire the Vanderbilt student body to work with the Nashville community for progress in the criminal justice system, and to serve, advocate for, and uplift those impacted by it.

name: Vanderbilt Student Volunteers for Science
description:
Vanderbilt Student Volunteers for Science (VSVS) is a service organization composed of undergraduate, graduate, and medical students who are committed to bringing inquiry-based, hands-on science lessons to middle school students. Four times per semester, groups of 2-5 volunteers visit 5th-8th grade classrooms in the Nashville area to teach science lessons that align with the students' curriculum. VSVS also runs outreach events at local schools and STEM organizations, science lessons at the Vanderbilt Children's Hospital, and a rural outreach program. VSVS provides Vanderbilt students with the opportunity to be role models and teach science while also helping its members recognize their responsibility to the community and the importance of service. View our calendar here.

 

 

 

To sign up as a member/volunteer, click the join request on this page and fill out our application here: https://forms.office.com/r/EW5PL01uJQ. We accept new members for the first two weeks of every Fall and Spring semester!

 

 

 

Note: "Protection of Minors" (POM) and "Chemical & Physical Safety Training for VSVS" compliance trainings in Oracle are required for all VSVS members! Please take these courses ASAP if you plan to participate.
summary: VSVS is dedicated to delivering engaging, hands-on science experiments to 5th-8th grade students across Nashville and rural Tennessee. Volunteers teach lessons in classrooms 4 times per semester--no science or teaching experience is necessary to join!

name: Vanderbilt Pre-Medical Society
description: The Vanderbilt Pre-Medical Society (formerly Alpha Epsilon Delta, AED) is the student branch of the Health Professions Advisory Office (HPAO) and serves to maximize the Vanderbilt pre-medical experience by connecting the student body with resources and opportunities. Such efforts include hosting speakers and representatives from medical schools, organizing community service opportunities throughout Nashville, facilitating medical student mentoring, planning social events, sponsoring an international Spring Break service trip, sending a weekly newsletter (This Week in VPS, or “TWIV”), and collaborating with the HPAO and Vanderbilt University Medical Center to support a robust shadowing program and MHS course.
summary: The Vanderbilt Pre-Medical Society (formerly Alpha Epsilon Delta, AED) is the student branch of the Health Professions Advisory Office (HPAO) and serves to maximize the Vanderbilt pre-medical experience by connecting the student body with resources.

name: Vanderbilt Tour Guides
description:
Our recruitment season is coming up! We restrict Anchor Link access to our members, but we highly encourage anyone interested to follow the Tour Guide instagram @vandyguides or use this link: https://vl.clubfair.io/tourguides/interestform to join our email list! 

 

Tour Guides is an organization which seeks to introduce prospective students to Vanderbilt University from a uniquely student perspective. Working in conjunction with the Office of Undergraduate Admissions, this organization helps recruit a broad range of prospective students through daily tours of campus, informative student panels, and various targeted programs throughout the year. Tour Guides are committed to providing a unique, balanced perspective on life as a Vanderbilt student to visiting prospective students. Tour Guides also love our 'Dores and strive to foster a spirit of community and dedication to Vanderbilt within the Tour Guides organization!
summary: Tour Guides is a student-run organization that collaborates with the Office of Undergraduate Admissions to provide an honest student perspective to prospective students to interested in Vanderbilt University.

name: Vanderbilt Emergency Medical Society
description: Vanderbilt Emergency Medical Society (VEMS) was founded in March 2007 by several Vanderbilt students with a passion for pre-hospital care. Many of our members are EMR/EMT certified and participate in ride-alongs with Vanderbilt LifeFlight or volunteer at local events with LifeFlight Event Medicine. Other members can gain valuable clinical skills through our Basic Life Support certification course taught on campus by students who have trained as certified instructors as well as our skills sessions, suturing clinics, and Narcan trainings. All of our members share excitement for learning about medicine and a commitment to serving the Nashville community.
summary: Vanderbilt Emergency Medical Society (VEMS) is comprised of students interested in pre-hospital care and serving the community. Our members gain clinical exposure through volunteer work at Vanderbilt and ride-along opportunities with LifeFlight .

name: Designing World Health
description: We are a non-profit organization with a mission of inspiring, educating, and empowering the engineering community to improve healthcare delivery in communities in need. With this professional expertise, we assemble biomedical instrumentation kits, such as ECG simulators and optical heart rate monitors, that are then shipped to developing countries to improve healthcare infrastructure.  We also participate in national design competitions to encourage the development of technical solutions that contribute to improving medical techniques and global healthcare.
summary: We are an organization with a mission of inspiring, educating, and empowering the community to improve healthcare delivery in communities in need through design competitions and volunteering.

name: Vanderbilt Business Healthcare Association - Owen
description:
The mission of the Vanderbilt Business Healthcare Association (VBHA) is to offer members unique opportunities to learn, lead, and succeed in preparing for the challenges of today’s highly competitive healthcare industry.

The VBHA hopes to foster student understanding of the healthcare business, share knowledge of careers in the healthcare industry, provide opportunities for students to exhibit knowledge and skills to employers, and encourage and facilitate the development of relationships among Owen students and alumni.
summary: The purpose of the Vanderbilt Business Healthcare Association at Owen is to prepare its members for careers as emerging healthcare (HC) leaders and to foster a better understanding of the HC industry by providing educational and networking opportunities.

name: Naval Reserve Officer Training Corps Vanderbilt University
description:
NROTC UNIT (NROTCU) Vanderbilt University is located on the corner of Edgehill and 19th Avenue South in downtown Nashville, TN across the street from the Vanderbilt Peabody campus. This unit is the host university for Tennessee State University and Belmont University NROTC students.

NROTC emphasizes academic excellence and professional development in preparing our midshipmen for the challenges and demands they will face as junior officers in the fleet. Through a rigorous curriculum consisting of naval science classes, weekly professional labs, and summer training with fleet units around the world, midshipmen acquire the skills and knowledge required to become an Ensign in the United States Navy or Second Lieutenant in the United States Marine Corps. With vast support from various organizations and campus resources, the midshipmen at Vanderbilt University are well equipped to succeed.

Midshipmen at Vanderbilt are heavily involved in many activities including color guard, drill team, Semper Fi and Trident societies, community service, Greek Life, and various club and intramural sports such as basketball, soccer, flag football, and swimming.

NROTCU Vanderbilt University consists of about 50 midshipmen. Approximately 25% of the midshipmen are female, and 30% of the midshipmen are Marine Option students.

The NROTC Program at Vanderbilt

Challenging academic courses and experience-building events prepare midshipmen for the opportunity to serve their country, receive an education, and begin their career in the United States Navy and United States Marine Corps. NROTC develops the ablest leaders possible by building upon the academic strength of Vanderbilt and providing essential military and leadership education.

Students participate in the NROTC unit in either the scholarship program, the college program, or the naval science program. Scholarship students take the prescribed naval science course each semester, participate weekly in naval science lab, and engage in a four-week summer training program after each academic year. The college program is identical to the scholarship program except that students only participate in summer training upon completion of their junior academic year. Any Vanderbilt student may take any or all of the naval science courses without participating in naval science lab or summer training.

Scholarship students receive full tuition, fees, textbooks, uniforms, and a monthly stipend of $250 in their freshman year, increasing by $50 each subsequent year. Vanderbilt also provides scholarship students with a $6,000 per year stipend toward room and board. College program students are provided with uniforms, textbooks for naval science courses, and, upon commencement of their junior year, a monthly stipend of $350 and $400 their senior year.

Naval Science courses cover naval engineering systems, sea power in history, naval weapons systems, navigation, naval operations, leadership, and management. Each class meets three hours per week. In addition, a two and one-half hour naval science lab is conducted each Thursday afternoon.
summary: NROTC emphasizes academic excellence and professional development in preparing our midshipmen for the challenges and demands they will face as junior officers in the fleet.

name: Pre-Dental Society Vanderbilt University
description: The Vanderbilt University Pre-Dental Society is devoted to providing educational support, shadowing opportunities, and volunteer opportunities for all pre-dental students on campus.  We serve as a resource for students seeking more information about the varied aspects of the dental profession by providing access to influential members of the dental community, access to the knowledge accrued by pre-dental students on campus, as well as published resources.  In addition, the PDSVU partners with the Health Professions Advisory Office to improve on-campus offerings to pre-dental students.  By encouraging dentists, periodontists, orthodontists, endodontists, and oral and maxillofacial surgeons of tomorrow to work together today, the Pre-Dental Society, Vanderbilt University contributes to the development of a collective spirit of cooperation necessary in the medical professions.
summary: The Vanderbilt University Pre-Dental Society is devoted to providing educational support, shadowing opportunities, and volunteer opportunities for all pre-dental students on campus.

name: Pre-Nursing Society
description:
The Pre-Nursing Society is the only Vanderbilt Student Organization that is dedicated to undergraduate students who have an interest in nursing! The organization holds monthly meetings and community service events. 

The major goals of this organization are to explore the field of nursing, discover the pre-nursing options at Vanderbilt, and increase the understanding of the role of an advanced practice nurse. This will be accomplished through:

  • Participation in research, conferences, panel discussions, community service and other pertinent events. 
  • Exposure to clinical settings (shadowing). Students are responsible for arranging their shadowing experience in consultation with Brandee Madden and Vicki Harris, Pre-nursing advisors.
  • Networking opportunities in the nursing community, and 
  • Liaison activities between undergraduate and graduate nursing students.

Be sure to follow our instagram! page: https://www.instagram.com/vandypns/ for more information!
summary: The Pre-Nursing Society is a pre-professional organization that aims to unite pre-nursing students and create a community through educational opportunities, community service, and career guidance.

name: Vanderbilt Secular Student Alliance
description: Vanderbilt SSA hosts educational and social gatherings for students who subscribe to or are interested in a secular worldview. We aim to provide an active community and safe space for nonreligious students from all walks of life through our programming, which includes talks and events promoting the ideals of scientific and critical inquiry, as well as discussion of a variety of religious and philosophical topics. We hope to reform public opinion of secular individuals in our area and beyond.
summary: The Secular Student Alliance empowers secular students to proudly express their identity, build welcoming communities, promote secular values, and set a course for lifelong activism.

name: Seniors Give Back
description: During our time at Vanderbilt, each of us has become involved with something special at Vanderbilt. Seniors Give Back is an opportunity to give to any area of campus that’s been meaningful to you. Whether it’s your favorite student organization, department, or Greek organization—you decide where to leave your mark.
summary: The Seniors Give Back campaign promotes seniors to leave their mark on Vanderbilt by making a gift to any area across campus. The campaign brings seniors together as a class, allowing them to give back through fundraising and class-focused events.

name: Student Alumni Board
description:
The VU Student Alumni Board, also known as SAB, consists of 4 boards - one for each class. These students work as ambassadors for the Alumni Association, creating and implementing events throughout the year. The mission of SAB is to engage students with VU alumni to create meaningful community and a lifelong relationship with Vanderbilt.

 

Follow us on Instagram to be in the loop about upcoming events!
summary: Building class community and connecting with VU alumni.

name: Vanderbilt Investment Club
summary: The Vanderbilt Investment Club (VIC) aims to serve as an educational organization for undergraduates looking to develop experience in investing and financial markets by actively managing a $200,000 equity portfolio.

name: Students Promoting Environmental Awareness and Responsibility
description:
Students Promoting Environmental Awareness and Responsibility (SPEAR) is a student-run environmental organization at Vanderbilt University, located in Nashville, Tennessee, USA.

Mission

SPEAR will increase environmental awareness and promote more environmentally sustainable habits and infrastructure within the Vanderbilt and Nashville community.

  1. SPEAR will strive to redouble our efforts on campus while maintaining a presence in the Nashville community because SPEAR’s primary commitment is to the University.
  2. SPEAR will make an attempt to work closely with the administration on all projects so as to maximize available resources in the quest for sustainability
summary: SPEAR is a student group dedicated to advancing both Vanderbilt and its students in an environmentally-friendly manner. Our interests range from simple acts of recycling to large-scale infrastructure projects on campus.

name: Vanderbilt Magic: The Gathering
description:
For Vanderbilt students, it can be tough to find the resources and transportation needed to play in Magic: The Gathering events. VandyMTG seeks to eliminate that barrier and provide a space for anyone on campus who wants to play Magic. We run weekly Magic: The Gathering events including Commander play nights, booster drafts, cube drafts, and more! The club is open to students of all experience levels from total beginners to long-time veterans.

Our spring interest form!

Join our discord server!
summary: Vanderbilt Magic: The Gathering (or VandyMTG) is an organization that seeks to foster a community of Magic: The Gathering players of all experience levels on campus. Magic is a strategic trading card game played by millions of people worldwide.

name: Vanderbilt International Relations Association
description:
Vanderbilt International Relations Association (formerly Vanderbilt Model U.N), or VIRA, is a multifaceted organization aimed at bringing together students interested in international affairs and global issues through two main branches: Model UN Travel Team and VUMUN. Across these two branches and our regular social meetings, VIRA promotes international affairs through competition, philanthropy, community engagement, written publication, and open discussion!

Programs Offered By VIRA

Vanderbilt Model UN Travel Team

Travel Team competes at MUN conferences across the country with each student taking on the role of a diplomat, delegate, or nation to work with others in resolving simulated major international crises of past, present, and future. Participants research their positions, investigate global issues, and debate with others in attempting to develop solutions to a wide array of topics. Cities that we have traveled to include NYC, Chicago, Boston, Montreal, Atlanta, and more.

For all questions relating to the Model UN Travel Team, contact Maddie Amberg at madeleine.g.amberg@vanderbilt.edu!

VUMUN

In addition to our competitive Travel Team, VIRA also hosts a high-school level Model UN conferences in the Fall. VUMUN brings over 600 high school students from across the country to Vanderbilt for three days each Fall, presenting over 20 committees staffed by over 100 Vanderbilt students. All are welcome!

For all questions relating to VUMUN, contact Shravya Vassiredy at shravya.vasireddy@vanderbilt.edu!
summary: VIRA Mission: To provide Vanderbilt students with a forum to discuss and engage in international topics; to improve speaking and debate skills of its members; and to represent Vanderbilt in national and international Model United Nations competitions.

name: American Medical Student Association at Vanderbilt
description:
AMSA at Vanderbilt:

The Vanderbilt chapter is dedicated to fulfilling the national mission and goals of the American Medical Student Association. We are a cohort of pre-health students who are committed to academics, service, community, and public health awareness. We believe in equal opportunity and thus are dedicated to providing all students with any resources they need to successfully navigate being a pre-health student at Vanderbilt. Furthermore, we strive to embrace the diversity within Vanderbilt’s pre-med body and will work to cultivate friendship and collaboration in hopes of creating a more united pre-medical community.

We strongly believe that it takes more than medical school to become a great physician, so in addition to providing students with academic guidance and resources, we are committed to creating opportunities for students to learn about and discuss health and medicine from a non-academic approach; this means engaging members in discussions about the social, moral, and ethical obligations of the profession of medicine, as well as improving students’ awareness about the cultural, social, and environmental determinants that affect individuals’ [particularly minorities'] health.

Our Mission: To create visionary future physicians through learning, meaningful public service, activism and community.


National AMSA Description (from national website)
The American Medical Student Association (AMSA), with a half-century history of medical student activism, is the oldest and largest independent association of physicians-in-training in the United States.

Today, AMSA is a student-governed, national organization committed to representing the concerns of physicians-in-training. AMSA members are medical students, premedical students, interns, residents and practicing physicians. Founded in 1950, AMSA continues its commitment to improving medical training and the nation's health.

How is AMSA different from other organizations? When you join AMSA, you become part of a vital force of future physicians who believe that patients and health professionals are partners in the management of health care and that access to high-quality health care is a right and not a privilege. At AMSA, activism is a way of life. Student idealism is transformed into meaningful public service, innovation and institutional change.
summary: We empower students to make the most out of their pre-health career here through mentorship,community-building,meaningful conversation,and education. We are future physicians who believe that access to high-quality health care is a right, not a privilege

name: Vanderbilt Pre-Veterinary Medicine Society
description:
Vanderbilt University's Pre-Veterinary Medicine Society is a student organization that strives to promote and stimulate interest in veterinary medicine by gathering students so that they may support one another in scholarship, fellowship and leadership.  VVMS offers opportunities for members to shadow local veterinarians, observe surgeries, visit shelters, zoos, and rescue centers, and gain advice from guest speakers ranging from veterinarians to veterinary school admissions directors.  VVMS supports Vanderbilt's pre-veterinary students through the long, difficult process of applying to veterinary school by offering advice, guidance, and volunteer opportunities.

VVMS aims to:

  • Describe the wide range of interdisciplinary opportunities in veterinary medicine.
  • Familiarize interested students with information concerning the veterinary application process and requirements.
  • Provide information on various veterinary medical fields and careers.
  • Create opportunities for hands-on experience and travel in veterinary medicine.
  • Gather students of similar interests so that they may support each other in scholarship, fellowship, and leadership.
summary: VVMS is an organization dedicated to promoting interest in veterinary medicine and supporting its members through their pre-veterinary undergraduate career and the veterinary medicine application process.

name: Vanderbilt Quizbowl
description: The purpose of Vanderbilt Quizbowl is to promote and celebrate knowledge in all its forms through teamwork amongst ourselves and competition with other universities across the country; to provide a community for student trivia-lovers and offer the opportunity for competition and learning within the quizbowl format; to expand our own breadth of knowledge from the scientific and the literary to pop culture and current events.
summary: The purpose of Vanderbilt Quizbowl is to promote and celebrate knowledge in all its forms through teamwork amongst ourselves and competition with other universities across the country.

name: Wilderness Skills 101 (WilSkills)
description: Wilderness Skills is Vanderbilt University's premier outdoor experience. The entirely student-run course is designed to integrate academic study with practical experience in the wilderness. It utilizes both the classroom and the environment, combining lectures, films, and discussions with trips to the surrounding areas. The trips are designed to teach students the basic techniques of backpacking, caving, rock climbing, and whitewater paddling. The course is offered each Fall and Spring semester and is open to all current Vanderbilt students, both undergraduate and graduate.  Go to our website to learn more and have a more aesthetically pleasing viewing experience!
summary: Wilderness Skills is Vanderbilt University's premier outdoor experience.  The entirely student-run course consists of lectures on outdoor topics and weekend trips.  Trip activities include backpacking, rock climbing, caving, and whitewater paddling.

name: Badminton Club
description:
Hey everyone, welcome to the Badminton Club! We welcome badminton enthusiasts of all levels and organize weekly open play. Club open play sessions will take place on Wednesdays and Saturdays from 5:00PM - 7:00PM. Club team practices will take place on Tuesdays from 5:30PM - 7:30PM and Sundays from 5:00PM - 7:00PM. Please note that all courts are reserved for team members during the team practices but you are welcome to come play any other time as long as the courts are available! Rackets and birdies are provided!

For any questions pertaining to badminton, feel free to contact darren.r.wang@vanderbilt.edu! Please join our GroupMe Here!!
summary: The Badminton Club serves as a community for all badminton players regardless of level or experience. The club hosts tournaments and events for the Vanderbilt community to enjoy.

name: Club Baseball
description: The Vanderbilt Club Baseball team was founded in 2006 as an effort for those who want to continue to play a competitive level of baseball at the collegiate level with less time commitment than varsity sports. The team is comprised of current undergraduate and graduate students at Vanderbilt University. The club is an entirely student-run and self-coached organization. The team practices 3 times per week at the Vanderbilt Student Recreation's club fields. We generally travel for two series each semester across the southeast.
summary: We strive to provide an opportunity for undergraduate and graduate students of Vanderbilt University to continue playing competitive baseball without the level of commitment that comes with varsity athletics.

name: Vanderbilt Cricket Club
description: Cricket is played by more than 16 countries at the International level. The United States is also actively involved in the International Cricket Council’s (ICC) trophy. However, although popular around the world, cricket is relatively unknown in the US. Vanderbilt, with its diverse and open-minded students and faculty, provides a great opportunity to have an active cricket-playing community. Every year, we recruit several new members, hold cricket workshops, and host practice sessions to familiarize those new to the game. While we believe in enjoying the game, the club’s bigger objective is to participate in cricket tournaments involving cricket clubs from other universities and in the Nashville area. We wish to host cricket tournaments -- putting Vanderbilt on the University cricket map. We encourage anyone wanting to play or learn about cricket to join us.
summary:
Hit it out of the park, or get bowled over!
We play taped tennis ball cricket and have a fun and competitive team. We host and actively participate in Nashville area tournaments (Nashville Premier League). We also promote cricket at Vanderbilt.

name: Vanderbilt University Fencing Club
description:
Practice is held every Tuesday and Thursday evenings at the Rec Center in Studio B. All skill levels are welcome, and practices are open to epee, foil, and saber fencers. (Any changes to our practice schedule will be updated through our Instagram or GroupMe).

We accept new members at any point during the school year! If you have any questions please fill out the contact form, reach out through our Instagram/Linktree or email logan.s.walsh@vanderbilt.edu
summary: The club was formed to provide an arena in which members of the Vanderbilt community may learn to fence in a recreational and competitive setting with the objective of providing an open learning atmosphere to all skill levels.

name: Club Field Hockey
summary: Vanderbilt University's Club Field Hockey team is open to both women and men. We enjoy weekly practices and tournaments both at home and on the road!

name: Ice Hockey
description:
Established in 1976, Vanderbilt Club Ice Hockey competes at a high level in the CHF, ACHA, and CHS. We’re a fully student-run team of undergrad and grad players traveling across the South with pride, intensity, and a culture rooted in camaraderie, development, and Dore-style grit.


Season & Team Logistics

  • 25‑game season spanning the fall and spring semesters, including southern rivalries, out-of-state showdowns, and nationals–highlighted by our first-ever Nationals win in March 2024

  • Practices every Wednesday at 8:45 PM, honing skills and teamwork consistently each week.

  • Home rink: Ford Ice Center Bellevue (formerly Ford Ice Center Antioch), complete with team-managed transportation and a dedicated locker room—so your gear stays put all season.

This structure empowers players to focus on hockey, toughness, and achieving excellence together—on and off the ice.
summary: To provide students who love hockey with the opportunity to represent the University in the Collegiate Hockey Federation and College Hockey South in a fun and competitive culture that is fully governed by the club members.

name: Vanderbilt Shotokan Karate Club
description:
The Vanderbilt Shotokan Karate Club seeks to be a means for students, faculty and staff to learn, practice, test, and compete in Shotokan Karate. The club aims to provide a supportive and inclusive environment for individuals interested in martial arts, fostering physical fitness, mental discipline, and camaraderie.

 

Members agree to adhere to the dojo-kun, or “school rules”, which are as follows: 
to seek the perfection of character
to be faithful 
to endeavor
to respect others
to refrain from violent behavior

 

 

Members are encouraged to attend the Shotokan Karate class in studio A at the Vanderbilt Wellness Center Mondays and Wednesdays from 5:30-6:30 for beginners and 6:30-7:30 for advanced.

Sign up for class here!
summary: The Vanderbilt Shotokan Karate Club promotes the practice, understanding, and appreciation of Shotokan Karate among the members of the Vanderbilt community. All are welcome regardless of experience level.

name: Vanderbilt Law Soccer Club
description: VLSC is a law school organization designed to promote and coordinate soccer within the law school. Included in this effort, VLSC organizes weekly pickup games and facilitates interested students in playing during the intramural soccer season. People of all skill levels are encouraged to join.
summary: VLSC is designed to promote and coordinate pickup and intramural soccer within the law school and with other graduate schools. Individuals of all skill levels are welcome!

name: Men's Club Lacrosse
description: As the premiere Men's Lacrosse team on campus, we spend about 4 hours per week practicing, participate in 2 fall tournaments, host the legendary Halloween scrimmage, and will have anywhere between 10-14 games during the spring season, with around 3-5 away weekends.
summary:
VFL. Burn the boats. 

Fill in your info here: https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/171KSJWB5f8ghiPDuLuBirWaSwuPCD02krExXQeZKgbs/edit?usp=sharing

name: Rowing Team
description:
The Vanderbilt Rowing team was established in 1985. Decades later, they are a highly competitive club team, having won multiple National and Conference Regional Championships under Assistant Coach Emmanuel Pagan, the 2023 All-South ACRA Men's Coach of the Year and Head Coach Jon Miller, the 2023 ACRA All-South Women's Coach of the Year and the 2024 ACRA All-American Women's Coach of the Year. 

Vanderbilt Rowing competes both in the fall and in the spring, traveling both within Tennessee and out of state multiple times each semester. They currently race all boat sizes, from a single up to an eight using their modern and well-maintained fleet of boats. Their varsity team trains intensely 6 days a week on the water when the weather allows and on Concept2 PM5 ergs, bikes and swingulators during winter training. 

The team's tenets are dedication and grit. As a club team, they train athletes with rowing experience and without. They are looking for athletes with a team-first mentality, and a hunger for speed. Email vandyrowing@gmail.com if you are interested in being a part of something great.
summary: Home to many National Champions and ACRA All-Americans, Vanderbilt Rowing is a highly competitive team on campus. They train 6 days a week and represent Vanderbilt at regattas across the country. One does not need prior experience in rowing to try-out.

name: Vanderbilt University Rugby Football Club
description:
MISSION STATEMENT

The Vanderbilt University Rugby Football Club (VURFC) is dedicated to fostering a competitive, inclusive, and team-oriented environment where players of all backgrounds can develop their skills, character, and lifelong connections. Founded in 1970, VURFC upholds a rich tradition of sportsmanship, discipline, and camaraderie, striving to represent Vanderbilt University with integrity and passion.

Our mission is to provide student-athletes with the opportunity to grow both on and off the field through teamwork, resilience, and leadership. We embrace a culture of inclusivity—anyone who shows up is given the chance to practice, play, and contribute to the success of the team.

Through competition, travel, and a strong sense of brotherhood, we honor the legacy of those who built this club before us while ensuring its continued growth for future generations. Vanderbilt Rugby is more than a team; it is a lifelong community dedicated to excellence in the sport and beyond.

 

Training: Tuesday and Thursday, 5:30 - 7pm, Rec Field 2 

 

2024 Southeastern Collegiate Rugby Conference Champions 

2018 Dixie League Champions

2019 Alabama Invitational 7s Champions

 
For any further inquiry, email the president.
summary: Vanderbilt University Mens Rugby Football Club

name: Vanderbilt Running Club
description: Running Club is a club sport dedicated to uniting student runners of all levels, experiences, and goals for charitable races, intercollegiate competition, camaraderie, and training. Running Club has optional daily practices that are open to all members of the Vanderbilt community, meeting at 4:30pm every day. Official members are informed of races in the Nashville area, as well as NIRCA competitions against running clubs from other universities. Running Club sends a team to the annual Chattanooga to Nashville Ragnar Relay and also hosts an annual 5K to benefit the Monroe Carell Jr. Children's Hospital.
summary: The Running Club is a student organization dedicated to uniting student runners of all levels, experiences, and goals for charitable races, intercollegiate competition, camaraderie, and training.

name: Club Sailing
description:
The Vanderbilt Sailing Team competes in the South Atlantic district of the ICSA. We cater to all levels of sailors, from new to dedicated racers. Our facilities are located at Harbor Island Yacht Club where we sail on the Cumberland River. During our season, we hold practices on Sundays, typically focusing on fun as well as racing technique. Harbor Island also has other sailing opportunities available, with active Lightning and V-15 fleets. Our team has many opportunities for learning, such as our weekly chalk talks and a number of experienced sailors, many with their Level 1 instructor certificates. We are looking for any individuals who wish to join an organization for which rule number 1 is to have fun. 
 
Here is our Mission Statement: 
The Vanderbilt Sailing Team welcomes sailors of all skill levels, including students without prior experience, to engage in competitive and recreational sailing. Through weekly practices, a regional schedule of competitive regattas, and travel opportunities, the Vanderbilt Sailing Team fosters a close community between its members.
summary: The University's oldest club sports team since 1962.

name: Vanderbilt Men's Club Soccer
description:
The purpose of the Vanderbilt Men’s Club Soccer Team is to provide an arena for students to play organized, competitive soccer.

Tryouts are held the first couple weeks of the fall semester outside the Rec Center. Please check our Instagram page for the most up to date information. If you have questions, please attend the student organization fair (also beginning of the fall semester), direct message us on Instagram, or email the club president (charles.g.frech@vanderbilt.edu).

We practice twice a week (Monday & Wednesday nights) and play roughly 5-10 games a semester. Our league consists of teams in Kentucky and Tennessee.
summary: The Vanderbilt Men’s Club Soccer Team consists in providing an arena for select students to play organized, competitive soccer.

name: Squash Club
description: The Vanderbilt Squash Club is an intercollegiate sports club. We travel several times during the year to tournaments around the country, culminating with Squash Club Championships, which is a national tournament in Philadelphia for all squash teams. All skill levels are welcome. We also have informal practices as well as get-togethers outside of squash.
summary: The Vanderbilt Squash Club is a co-ed intercollegiate sports club. We travel during the year to tournaments around the country, culminating with Squash Nationals, which is a national tournament for all squash teams. All skill levels are welcome.

name: Vanderbilt Swim Club
description:
Our purpose is to have organized practices for students, grad students, faculty, staff and recreational members. The Vanderbilt Swim Club meets multiple times a week, and a schedule will be sent out every Sunday night.

All are welcome at every practice time. Practices are a good place for new members to meet people!

The average member attends 2-3 workouts/week. Attendance at practices is completely optional as we are primarily a recreational club built for camaraderie. Members can attend as few or as many practices as they desire. Our practices range in ability level from casual fitness swimmers to former Division I athletes. While we do not offer lessons or instruction, we structure our workouts to accommodate almost all levels of swimmers. Average yardage at any practice varies from 2500 yards to 4000 yards, depending on ability, motivation, and time. Pull buoys and kick boards are provided and available at the pool facility.

We attend swim meets as a collegiate club team. We will travel to various meets around the southeast depending on interest, including Collegiate Club Swim Nationals in March.

If able, we will also host 1-2 meets per school year.
summary: Vanderbilt  Swim Club is an organization including current students, graduates, faculty, and staff who meet as often as possible to swim.  We also participate in meets throughout the year, both as a masters team and a collegiate club.

name: Club Tennis
description:
We are a nationally recognized organization dedicated to fun, competitive tennis. We practice four times a week and play numerous tournaments both on and off campus throughout the year. 

Membership is determined through yearly tryouts. Tryouts for the Fall 2024 semester will occur around the first full week of classes.

Practices can change throughout the year because of court availability, but practice is usually every Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday in the evening and every Sunday afternoon. Attendance at all practices is not necessary.

Please contact Emily Bernstein with any questions.
summary: We are a nationally recognized organization dedicated to fun, competitive tennis. We practice four times a week and play numerous tournaments, both on and off campus throughout the year.

name: Men's Club Ultimate
description: Armada is Vanderbilt's competitive, traveling men's club ultimate frisbee team. We practice 2-3 times per week year-round and have 3-5 tournament weekends per semester. We are open to all experience/skill levels - most of us began playing in college! As an organization, we pride ourselves on our team's cohesive culture. We traditionally carry an open roster for the fall season, trying to give as many players chances to play in tournaments as possible. In the spring, we decide on a travel roster that competes in the more competitive and sanctioned tournaments of the spring season. Regardless of travel roster status, all club members remain an integral part of the team and are encouraged to come to as many practices as they can!
summary: Armada is Vanderbilt's competitive, traveling men's club ultimate frisbee team. We practice 2-3 times per week year-round and have 3-5 tournament weekends per semester. We are open to all experience/skill levels - most of us began playing in college!

name: Vanderbilt Men's Club Volleyball
description: Vanderbilt Club Volleyball is a USAV certified club team located at Vanderbilt University in Nashville, TN. The club has supported both a men's and women's team since 1980. We participate in the Southeast Division II tournaments under the ECVA as well as NCVF nationals. We host several tournaments each year, and play a full schedule of tournaments and matches throughout the year.
summary: Vanderbilt Club Volleyball is a certified club team located at Vanderbilt University that plays in ECVA and NCVF sanctioned events.

name: Women's Club Lacrosse
description:
Vanderbilt Women's Club Lacrosse is a competitive club program comprised of skilled lacrosse players seeking a well-rounded college experience, blending academic, athletic, and social life.

Spring is our primary season, but we train/practice and play in games year-round. We are always looking for competitive and energetic players to join our team!

We joined the Southeastern Women's Lacrosse League in the spring of 2020 and often play against talented club programs including Tennessee, Georgia, Auburn, Ohio State, and many more!

Reach out to our email vanderbiltclublax@gmail.com or our Instagram @vanderbiltclublax if you are interested in joining!!
summary: Vanderbilt Women's Club Lacrosse is a competitive club program comprised of skilled lacrosse players seeking a well-rounded college experience, blending academic, athletic, and social life.

name: Women's Ultimate Frisbee
description: We are a group of women that enjoy playing the sport of ultimate. We aim to be spirited, fun, and competitive. We practice twice a week and attend multiple competitive tournaments per semester! We welcome anyone interested in playing ultimate whether you've been playing for a while or have never played at all. Our strong community goes beyond just the field as we support each other in everything we do. From out of state tournaments to team hikes and brunch, we have it all!
summary: We are a group of women that enjoy playing the sport of ultimate and strive to spread it within the community. We aim to be competitive, inclusive, and fun while providing a support network for our team.  No previous experience needed!

name: Multicultural Leadership Council
description:
What is the MLC?

The Multicultural Leadership Council (MLC) seeks to advocate for and to address issues facing the Vanderbilt student body. The council is comprised of over 50 student organizations. It serves as a steering committee for the student organizations under its umbrella by implementing programs and addressing needs of the cultural minority students and those from historically marginalized communities, as well as educating the Vanderbilt campus on issues of diversity and cultural awareness.

Spaces the MLC uses

Multicultural Community Space (MCS) - a house in the West End neighborhood.

Multicultural Longue in Sarratt Student Center - where SCBC offices are housed.

Link to Current Constitution
summary: The Multicultural Leadership Council serves to ensure integration and cooperation between multicultural organizations and the University, and to ensure integration and cooperation among the different multicultural organizations on campus.

name: Vanderbilt Interfaith Council
description:
The Vanderbilt Interfaith Council is a leadership organization composed of students dedicated to interfaith relations both on campus and in the greater community. Our purpose is to promote understanding and respect for diverse spiritual beliefs by means of communication, dialogue, education, and advocacy. Our membership consists of representatives from each of the registered religious/spiritual organizations on-campus (both undergraduate and graduate) and is open to any interested member of the Vanderbilt community. 
 
Important Forms:
Co-sponsorship and funds request form: https://forms.gle/nqrevs8sevZ4VzNy9
Newsletter submission form: https://forms.gle/XAjPpjWoVJq6EWuX9
summary: Interfaith Council is a leadership organization composed of students dedicated to interfaith relations on-campus and in the greater community.

name: Vanderbilt Programming Board
description: The purpose of VPB is to sponsor, integrate, and provide social, cultural, educational, recreational, and multicultural activities at Vanderbilt University. We will constantly evaluate these programs and assess the needs of the Vanderbilt community to provide a more enjoyable and purposeful experience for all members of the Vanderbilt community. Students who provide leadership in VPB will gain experience in program planning and implementation for both small and large scale activities.
summary: The Vanderbilt Programming Board (VPB) is comprised of the 6 largest programming organizations on campus: Homecoming Committee, Speakers Committee, CityVU, The Music Group, Late Nite Programming, and Vandy Fanatics.

name: Career Center
description:
We support undergraduate students enrolled full-time in Blair School of Music, College of Arts and Science, Peabody College, and School of Engineering.

To learn more about events hosted by the Career Center, visit here.
summary:
GAIN CLARITY ABOUT YOUR FUTURE

Let the Career Center help

name: The Ingram Commons
description:
Residential colleges are a type of on-campus student residence in which the academic experience is integrated into daily life, creating communities and opportunities for learning outside the classroom among a diverse student body. Led by faculty heads who live in residence, Vanderbilt’s residential colleges create an intentional, mutually-beneficial educational experience for our students, faculty and staff. At Vanderbilt, residential colleges are a space where students are supported to curate their sense in self; pursue integrative learning; dialogue across difference; engage in advocacy; foster their leadership potential; and forge global connections.

Driven by the educational experience of the residential college system, the upper-division residential colleges and The Martha Rivers Ingram Commons represent a cohesive and growing network of residences that spark creativity, build community, support student success, and extend educational opportunities beyond the classroom.
summary: The Martha Rivers Ingram Commons begins four transformative years of a Vanderbilt education. First-year students live and learn together in the 10 houses of The Ingram Commons, each guided by a faculty head of house who lives among the students.

name: Commons Center
description:
The Commons Center

The Commons Center is the community crossroads of the Commons living learning community. It has it all: the Dining Hall and great food; a living room with a concert grade grand piano, and the occasional live musical performance; a small rec room with cardio equipment, free weights, and weight machines; meeting and study rooms; and academic support services like the Writing Studio and the CASPAR premajor advising center. Students from across campus come here for a meal, a quiet place to study, an event, or a meeting with a professor. The third floor of the Commons Center is the home of the Department of Political Science, the Center for the Study of Democratic Institutions, the Latin American Public Opinion Project, and the Research on Individuals, Politics, and Society Lab.

The Dining Hall

The dining hall is a meeting place for students from all ten houses. By day, natural light flows through five skylights and expansive floor-to-ceiling windows as students enjoy their meals from the nationally recognized Food Gallery.

The dining hall isn’t limited to breakfast, lunch, and dinner. It has served as venue to dozens of concerts, and is the home of each semester’s formal dance. Beyond it’s role in the social fabric of The Commons, the dining hall is open to students all night, every night as a group collaboration and study space.

 

Exercise Room

The Commons Center exercise room offers students a convenient place for both cardiovascular and anaerobic exercise. The facility features:

  • Treadmills
  • Stationary bikes
  • Elliptical trainers
  • Free weights
  • Weight machines
  • Rowing machine
  • Stairmaster
  • Exercise and medicine balls
  • Exercise mats
  • Ping pong tables

The exercise room is open from early morning until late evening.

The Writing Studio

The Writing Studio fosters conversation, collaboration, and critical reflection on writing. Open to all members of Vanderbilt’s scholarly community, the Studio provides opportunities to receive constructive feedback from trained consultants and to develop strategies for all stages of the writing process.

Located in the academic support space in room 217, the Writing Studio is open for individual appointments Sunday-Thursday from 3:00-10:00 p.m. The Studio also hosts special events throughout the academic year. For more information about the Studio, visitwww.vanderbilt.edu/writing.

The Center for Student Professional Development

The Center for Student Professional Development, formerly the Vanderbilt Career Center, offers walk-in hours from Noon to 2:00 on Tuesdays and Thursdays in room 217 at The Commons Center.  Students can get help with resume writing, finding a job or internship, using DoreWays, exploring options or learning about other Center services. To learn more about the Center, visit www.vanderbilt.edu/career . 

CASPAR

Located in Suite 225 of The Commons Center, CASPAR provides academic advice to all students in the College of Arts and Science before they declare a major. A pre-major adviser helps students understand how to make sense of AP credit, interpret course requirements, select appropriate courses, develop a workable schedule each semester, and make long-term plans for a successful college career and beyond. CASPAR advisors can also help students identify other available campus resources that will encourage academic success at Vanderbilt -- like the Writing Studio just down the hall.

Post Office

The Commons Center is home to a full service USPS branch.

VU Mail Services info is available here.

The Department of Political Science

The 3rd floor of The Commons Center was completed in 2011, and has been occupied since by the Department of Political Science, including the Center for the Study of Democratic Institutions, the Latin American Public Opinion Project, and the Research on Individuals, Politics and Society Lab.  Tour the Department of Political Science here.

Leed Certification

From the lights to the layout, the Commons Center was designed to be extremely eco-friendly and efficient.  The building has been awarded a Gold LEED certification from the U.S. Green Building Council.

For more information about Vanderbilt's sustainability efforts, visit SustainVU.
summary: The Commons Center is the community crossroads of the Commons living learning community.

name: Student Health Center
description: The Student Health Center's mission is to enhance the academic experience of students by providing quality primary healthcare services in a nurturing and cost-effective manner. All of our physicians and nurse practitioners have chosen college health as their area of expertise and interest, and are dedicated to meeting the unique health care needs of the student population. Whether you are sick or injured, managing a chronic health problem, or working to develop a healthier lifestyle, the VU SHC is here to help.
summary: We are here to serve the primary care needs of the Vanderbilt student community. There are no office co-pays for routine visits, and all registered students are eligible for care, regardless of insurance coverage.

name: University Counseling Center
description:
The Vanderbilt University Counseling Center is located at 2015 Terrace Place. It has served the Vanderbilt community since 1949, (known to the community at that time as the University Counseling and Placement Center) when it was located in the “basement” of Kirkland Hall.

The Center offers a range of services to Vanderbilt students including individual and group psychotherapy, psychiatric services, alcohol and drug assessments/counseling, and LD/ADHD assessment. For the Vanderbilt community, we offer outreach, workshops organizational consulting, crisis manegement and more.

The UCC functions also as a training facility. Each academic year, graduate practicum students, pre-doctoral interns, post-doctoral psychology residents and psychiatric residents are chosen to train at the Center. They receive supervision from our staff of licensed psychologists, psychiatrists and other professionals.

To make an appointment or to obtain further information about our services, please contact the Center at 615-322-2571. (If on campus, call 2-2571).
summary: The Center offers a range of services to Vanderbilt students including individual & group psychotherapy, psychiatric services, alcohol and drug assessments/counseling, LD/ADHD assessment, academic skills program, & educational workshops and prevention.

organizationType.name: Student Affairs Department, Office, or Program

name: Development and Alumni Relations
description:
Vanderbilt University is a vibrant center of knowledge and learning, from student scholars and world-class faculty to innovative research facilities, top-ranked hospitals and more. In Development and Alumni Relations, our mission is to engage graduates, families, friends and corporations and foundations in the life of the university; to ensure an active and committed alumni community; and to encourage financial investment in Vanderbilt’s programs and scholarship.

 

Giving to Vanderbilt

  • Opportunity Vanderbilt
  • The Vanderbilt Fund
