summary: Our mission is to engage graduates, families, friends and corporations and foundations in the life of the university; to ensure an active and committed alumni community; and to encourage financial investment in Vanderbilt’s programs and scholarship.

name: Office of Greek Life
description:
Greek Life at Vanderbilt is more than just a social community. It’s a tradition that dates back before the institution’s founding in 1873. The establishment of the third chapter of Delta Kappa Epsilon (Deke) Fraternity in 1847 marked the beginning of Greek Life in Nashville. At the time, the University of Nashville hosted the chapter of Deke, and ten years later Sigma Alpha Epsilon Fraternity joined the community, followed by Phi Kappa Psi in 1870. Following the establishment of Vanderbilt University in 1873, seven more fraternities joined the community: Phi Delta Theta, Kappa Sigma, Chi Phi, Sigma Nu, Delta Tau Delta, Alpha Tau Omega, and Sigma Chi. Eight of these groups remain active at Vanderbilt today.

Sororities joined the Greek community soon after the university began admitting women in 1892. Out of a need for housing and close friendships, these new female students established two local sororities: Phi Kappa Upsilon and Theta Delta Theta. Within ten years, these two groups affiliated with the national organizations of Kappa Alpha Theta and Delta Delta Delta. Since that time 12 more National Panhellenic Conference organizations have established chapters at the institution, of which 9 remain active on campus today.

Historically African American Greek organizations joined the Vanderbilt community in 1971 with the founding of a chapter of Omega Psi Phi Fraternity, Inc. By 1975 Alpha Kappa Alpha Sorority, Inc., Delta Sigma Theta Sorority, Inc., and Alpha Phi Alpha Fraternity, Inc. joined the Greek community. In 1990, these organizations, with the help of the men of Kappa Alpha Psi Fraternity, Inc., formed the Black Greek Council to govern the affairs of these five groups. In 1999, the Council formalized its operations and became a chartered council under the National Pan-Hellenic Council, Inc. During that period of time, Phi Beta Sigma Fraternity, Inc. and Zeta Phi Beta Sorority, Inc. also established chapters at Vanderbilt.

Today, Vanderbilt University is home to approximately 30 organizations focusing on diversity equity and inclusion, creating spaces of belonging, community and civic engagement, sexual misconduct prevention, health and wellness, and personal and professional development.
summary: Vanderbilt University is home to almost 30 fraternities and sororities with about 25% of VU undergraduates belonging to the VU Greek community.

organizationType.name: Student Affairs Department, Office, or Program

name: Student Centers
description:
Mission: Student Centers at Vanderbilt create space for connection and belonging across campus.

For our visitors, you will find that our Student Centers team goes above and beyond to meet your expectations and create memorable experiences for you. We host over 40,000 events a year! From large conferences to student-hosted events, we have the ability to create something that is unique and special.

Vanderbilt Student Centers consists of Alumni Hall, Sarratt Student Center | Rand Hall, Student Life Center, Commons Center, Kissam Center, Community Event Space, Leadership and Service Space, Multicultural Community Space, as well as spaces in E. Bronson Ingram, Zeppos, Rothschild, and Carmichael College.
summary: Student Centers at Vanderbilt is a department within the Office of the Dean of Students in Rand 307. Our professional and student staff manage reservations, operations, and events in Student Centers and auxiliary building

organizationType.name: Student Affairs Department, Office, or Program

name: International Student and Scholar Services
description:
International Student and Scholar Services fosters the education and development of non-immigrant students and scholars to enable them to achieve their academic and professional goals and objectives. ISSS provides advice, counseling, and advocacy regarding immigration, cross-cultural, and personal matters. ISSS supports an environment conducive to international education and intercultural awareness via educational, social, and cross cultural programs.

Vandy ISSS 2023 GroupMe: https://groupme.com/join_group/83871085/rTEikD1o
summary: International Student and Scholar Services serves to be the primary resource for non-immigrant students and scholars at Vanderbilt University.

name: Housing Assignments
summary: We are the office that is responsible for assigning Vanderbilt students to rooms

organizationType.name: Student Affairs Department, Office, or Program

name: Center for Spiritual and Religious Life
description:
The Center for Spiritual and Religious Life seeks to function in an educational capacity, not only for those students and groups who are traditionally religious, but by way of raising ethical questions and issues of value and character among the student body at large. Because we seek to educate the “whole person”, we view ethical and spiritual formation as integral to the University’s overall educational mission and religious life as an important dimension of the so-called hidden curriculum of the University. Therefore we seek to integrate the programs and services offered by the chaplains and professional staff into the larger life of the University community.

Office hours are 8:00 AM to 5:00 PM, Monday-Friday.
summary: The Center for Spiritual and Religious Life serves the students, faculty and staff of Vanderbilt University.

organizationType.name: Student Affairs Department, Office, or Program

name: Arts and Student Activities
description: Arts and Student Activities (ASA) includes the major arts and campus programming initiatives on Vanderbilt’s campus: Vanderbilt Programming Board, Vanderbilt Performing Arts Community, Sarratt Art Studios, and  Sarratt Gallery. Arts and Student Activities mission is to facilitate and promote diverse programming for the Vanderbilt and Nashville Community that cultivates social engagement, cultural enrichment, artistic exploration and intellectual growth.  Arts and Student Activities is a part of the Student Engagement and Leadership Cluster.
summary: The Office of Arts and Student Activities (ASA) includes the major arts and campus programming initiatives on Vanderbilt’s campus.

organizationType.name: Student Affairs Department, Office, or Program

name: Lesbian, Gay, Bisexual, Transgender, Queer and Intersex Life
description:
Located in the K.C. Potter Center, the Office of Lesbian, Gay, Bisexual, Transgender, Queer, and Intersex Life at Vanderbilt University is a cultural center and a place of affirmation for individuals of all identities, and a resource for information and support about gender and sexuality.

LGBTQI+ Life serves all members of the Vanderbilt community — students, faculty, staff, and alumni — by creating educational, cultural, and social opportunities. The office also supports and advises LGBTQI-related campus groups and activities.

Sign up for our email list here
summary: The Office of Lesbian, Gay, Bisexual, Transgender, Queer, and Intersex Life at Vanderbilt University is a cultural center and a place of affirmation for individuals of all identities, and a resource for information and support about gender and sexuality.

organizationType.name: Student Affairs Department, Office, or Program

name: Margaret Cuninggim Women's Center
description:
Mission

 

The Margaret Cuninggim Women’s Center is an affirming space for women and for all members of the Vanderbilt community that actively resists discrimination in all forms, including sexism, by providing resources and educational programming.

 

 

Core Values

  • We believe that progress toward gender equity calls all of us to be champions for change.
  • We work to build community across differences by creating spaces for open dialogue.
  • We strive to foster a community of belonging on Vanderbilt’s campus and to empower all people to have a voice.

 

The Women’s Center is open Monday through Friday, 8:30 a.m. to 8:00 p.m. For more information, please call (615) 322-4843 or email us at womenctr@vanderbilt.edu.
summary: Celebrating women, empowering all.

organizationType.name: Student Affairs Department, Office, or Program

name: Crawford House
description:
Crawford House

Gilbert Gonzales, Faculty Head of Crawford House

Gilbert Gonzales is an associate professor in the Department of Medicine, Health and Society, director of Public Policy Studies and associate director of the LGBTQ+ Policy Lab at Vanderbilt. He teaches courses in health policy, quantitative research methods and LGBTQ+ health. In 2016, he was awarded a Chancellor's award for his research at Vanderbilt.

The House

Crawford provides modern features in a classic building housing 150 first-year students in traditional double rooms. Students live on floors 2–6 of this six story building. The second floor includes an apartment for the Faculty Head of House, a seminar room, and common kitchen for residents. Floors 3–6 each have a study area and three shared bathrooms with showers. In addition, there is a study area, laundry room, and music rooms on the first floor. The building is fully air conditioned. Corridors and common areas are carpeted. Student rooms have tiled floors.
summary: Located on the Martha Rivers Ingram Commons, Crawford House provides modern features in a classic building housing 150 first-year students in traditional double rooms. Students live on floors 2–6 of this six story building.

name: East House
description:
East House

Justin Quarry, Faculty Head of East House

Justin Quarry (he, him, his), BA '03, is principal senior lecturer in the Department of English. An author of both fiction and creative nonfiction, Quarry’s works have appeared in The New York Times, The Guardian, New York Daily News, The Southern Review, New England Review and more. Among other honors, Quarry has received the Normal Prize in Fiction from The Normal School literary magazine, the Robert Olen Butler Prize for short fiction, a grant from the Elizabeth George Foundation, and Individual Artist Fellowships from the Tennessee Arts Commission and the Arkansas Arts Council.

Originally from Jonesboro, AR, Quarry is a first-generation college graduate. After receiving a bachelor's degree in English and American Studies from Vanderbilt, he earned a master of fine arts in creative writing from the University of Virginia. He then lived and wrote in artist colonies around the United States before returning to Vanderbilt as a professor.  Look for him on strolls around campus with his Irish Terrier–the East Beast–Malcolm.    

The House

Located on The Martha Rivers Ingram Commons, East House provides modern features in a classic structure. Approximately 100 students live in East House. The building was completely renovated in 1987–88 and further renovations took place in the summers of 2005 and 2006. There is a study lounge, television lounge, and full-size kitchen on the second floor. The Faculty Head of House lives in an apartment on the second floor. The first floor consists of mechanical rooms, and the office of the Housing Facilities Supervisor.

East House was built in 1924.
summary: Located on The Martha Rivers Ingram Commons, East House provides modern features in a classic structure. Approximately 100 students live in East House.

name: Hank Ingram House
description:
Hank Ingram House

Eric Barth, Faculty Head of Hank Ingram House

Faculty Head of Hank Ingram House Eric Barth is a professor of mechanical engineering and professor of neurological surgery. He teaches courses in automatic controls for robotics and mechatronic systems. His current research focuses on soft robots and MRI-compatible surgical robots. Soft robots are a new class of robots that are rubbery and are excellent at interacting with objects and people. He is investigating the treatment of epilepsy with MRI-compatible robots by replacing open brain surgery with a robotically steered helical needle that is inserted through the cheek while being monitored by the MR scanner. Professor Barth grew up in Los Angeles and studied at UC Berkeley and Georgia Tech. Professor Barth, his wife Helen Bird, and their twins Nola and Kira are excited to join Hank residents in building a community together.

The House

Opened in August 2008, Hank Ingram House is located next to the Dean’s Residence. “Hank’s House” provides modern features in a classic building housing approximately 290 first-year students in traditional double rooms. Students live on floors 2–7 of this seven story building. On the first floor, students will find the laundry, an adjoining study space, and two music practice rooms. The second floor includes an apartment for the Faculty Head of House, two seminar rooms, a study space, and a common kitchen for building residents. Floors 3–6 each have a large study area and six shared bathrooms with showers. The seventh floor has a study area and three shared bathrooms with showers. The building is fully air conditioned. Corridors and common areas are carpeted. Student rooms have tiled floors.
summary: Hank Ingram House is located next to the Dean's Residence on The Martha Rivers Ingram Commons. "Hank House" provides modern features in a classic building housing approximately 290 first-year students in traditional double rooms.

name: Gillette House
description:
Gillette House

Brittany Chase, Faculty Head of Gillette House

Brittany Chase (she, her, hers) is a lecturer in ethnomusicology and assistant dean for nondegree programs at the Blair School of Music. She specializes in American popular music and teaches courses in Hip Hop Culture and the intersections of music, identity and diversity. Professor Chase is a graduate of Vanderbilt’s College of Arts and Science and joined the Vanderbilt faculty in 2016. Professor Chase and her dog Dexter are excited to build community alongside the residents of Gillette House. 

 

The House

Located on the Commons, Gillette House is residence for 220 first-year men and women living separately on floors 2–6. The Faculty Head of House lives in an apartment on the first floor. Also on the first floor is a large recreation room, a laundry, a seminar room, a kitchen and a break room with vending machines. The building has central air-conditioning.

Gillette House was built in 1963 and named for Frank E. Gillette, a trustee of Peabody College. Gillete House was completely renovated during the 2006–2007 academic year.
summary: Located on The Martha Rivers Ingram Commons, Gillette House is residence for 220 first-year men and women living separately on floors 2–6.

name: Memorial House
description:
Memorial House

Celso Castilho, Faculty Head of House 

Celso Castilho (he/him/his) is an associate professor of history, and director of the Center for Latin American, Carribean, and Latinx Studies. He is also currently president of the Conference on Latin American History (CLAH), an affiliate of the American Historical Association, and the largest and oldest society of its kind in the US. His two main areas of research are on the comparative histories of slavery and emancipation in the Atlantic world, and on sport and the Latin American diaspora, on which he is writing a book about Latino culture and politics in the context of the 1994 World Cup.

Celso was born in São Paulo, Brazil, and raised in Los Angeles, California. He earned a B.A. in history at UC Berkeley, an M.A. in Latin American Studies at UCLA, and returned to Berkeley to complete a doctorate in history. You will see him around Memorial with his wife, Jessica, and three children: Luiza, Paulo and Jack. 

 

The House

Located on the Commons, Memorial House provides modern features in a classic structure. Memorial House was modernized and renovated in 1987 and then again in the summers of 2006 and 2007. The building houses approximately 80 first year men and women on separate floors, mostly in traditional double rooms.

On the first floor are two seminar rooms, a study lounge, music practice rooms, and a vending area. Students live on floors 2–5. On the second floor is the apartment for the Faculty Head of House, a study lounge, a common kitchen, and seven student rooms. The third floor includes two study lounges, student rooms, and two common bathrooms with showers. Floors 5 and 6 each have 16 student rooms and two common bathrooms with showers.

Memorial House was built in 1935.
summary: Located on The Martha Rivers Ingram Commons, Memorial House provides modern features in a classic structure. The building houses approximately 80 first year men and women on separate floors, mostly in traditional double rooms.

name: Murray House
description:
Murray House

Renã A. S. Robinson, Faculty Head of Murray House

Faculty Head of Murray House Renã A.S. Robinson (she, her, hers) serves as a professor of chemistry and joined the Vanderbilt faculty in 2017. Robinson earned her Ph.D. from Indiana University and is a Dorothy J. Wingfield Phillips Chair. Her research focuses on Alzheimer’s disease and sepsis and the molecular contributions to racial disparities. She also is a wife, mother of two, and serves various communities in her spare time.

The House

Opened in August 2007, Murray House is located on the Commons. Murray House provides modern features in a classic building housing 150 first-year students in traditional double rooms. Students live on floors 2–6 of this six story building. The second floor includes an apartment for the Faculty Head of House, a seminar room, and common kitchen for building residents. Floors 3–6 each have a study area and three shared bathrooms with showers. In addition, there is a study area, laundry room, and music rooms on the first floor. The building is fully air conditioned. Corridors and common areas are carpeted. Student rooms have tiled floors.
summary: Murray House is located on The Martha Rivers Ingram Commons. Murray House provides modern features in a classic building housing 150 first-year students in traditional double rooms.

name: North House
description:
North House

Andy Finch, Faculty Head of North House

Andy Finch (Ph.D. ’03, M.Ed. ’95) is a professor of the practice in human and organizational development and a core faculty member in human development counseling. Professor Finch (he, him, his) teaches courses in human development, substance use and addiction, and career counseling. His current research focuses on the role of schools as recovery supports for substance use disorders, with an emphasis on recovery high schools. Professor Finch grew up in Kansas City and received a bachelor’s degree in journalism from the University of Kansas. He worked as a sports announcer before moving to Nashville for graduate school. He and his wife, Deb, met as Vanderbilt students, and they have two adult children, Michael and David.

Professor Finch joined the Vanderbilt faculty in 2004 and has been faculty head of North House since 2024. 

The House

Located in the Historic District of The Commons, North House is the closest residence hall to main campus. Approximately 180 residents enjoy modern features in a renovated six-story building. On the first floor, students have access to a fully equipped electronic classroom and 2 lounges. There are 2 lounges and 2 practice rooms on the second floor. The third floor is home to the apartment housing North’s Faculty Head of House in addition to a newly expanded central lobby. The sixth floor houses a central atrium and kitchen for use by all residents. The building is fully air-conditioned and wireless. Corridors, common areas, and student rooms are carpeted.
summary: Located in the Historic District of The Martha Rivers Ingram Commons, North House is the closest residence hall to main campus. Approximately 180 residents enjoy modern features in a renovated six-story building.

name: Stambaugh House
description:
Stambaugh House

Jessica Oster, Faculty Head of Stambaugh House

Jessica Oster (she, her, hers) is an associate professor in the department of Earth and environmental sciences. Her research involves understanding climate change in Earth’s past as it is recorded in the chemistry of mineral deposits from caves.  Jessica investigates questions about rainfall variability and fire activity through caves in California and Wyoming and on the island of Curaçao in the southern Caribbean. Jessica has experience mentoring students from the high school through the graduate level and currently serves as director of graduate studies in her department.

Jessica grew up in Kentucky where she first fell in love with caves. She earned her B.A. at Oberlin College and her PhD from the University of California, Davis. Jessica, her husband Neil Kelley (also on the faculty in Earth and environmental sciences) and their children Juniper and Roscoe are excited to build community in Stambaugh.

The House

Opened in August 2007, Stambaugh House is located on the Commons. Stambaugh provides modern features in a classic building housing 162 first-year students in traditional double rooms. The building is co-residential, with men and women living on separate floors.

The Faculty Head of House lives in an apartment on the first floor, while students live on floors 2-5 of this five story building. Each residential floor has a study area and three shared bathrooms with showers. In addition, there is a lobby, study area, laundry room, seminar room, music room, and communal kitchen on the first floor. The building is fully air conditioned. Corridors and common areas are carpeted. Student rooms have tiled floors.
summary: Stambaugh House is located on the Martha Rivers Ingram Commons. Stambaugh provides modern features in a classic building housing 162 first-year students in traditional double rooms.

name: Sutherland House
description:
Sutherland House

Jesse Spencer-Smith, Faculty Head of Sutherland House

Jesse Spencer-Smith (he, him, his) is the Chief Data Scientist and Interim Director for the Data Science Institute and a Professor of the Practice of Computer Science. Although he is currently in the School of Engineering, he (and the Data Science Institute) will soon become part of the new College of Connected Computing, Vanderbilt's first new College in over 40 years!

As Chief Data Scientist for the DSI, Jesse coordinates the work of the DSI Data Science team and dozens of Vanderbilt undergraduate and graduate students in collaborating with researchers across Vanderbilt on AI and Data Science research engagements. He teaches courses in generative AI at Vanderbilt, and leads workshops on AI for companies, governments, and nonprofits worldwide. He loves speaking with students about AI, careers, and future plans. He and his wife, Caren, and the family geckos, Loki, Ghost, Baby, Dexter and Scout are all happy residents of Sutherland, and welcome visitors for a coffee or tea.

Sutherland is named after Earl W. Sutherland, a Vanderbilt professor of physiology who won the Nobel Prize in 1971. There are seven Nobel laureates affiliated with Vanderbilt, most recently being John Jumper who won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry for his work in protein structure prediction. Prof. Spencer-Smith can't wait to see meet the next Sutherland residents, and to see what THEY do in the future!

The House

Opened in August 2006, Sutherland House is located on the Commons. Sutherland provides modern features in a classic building housing 162 first-year students in traditional double rooms. The building is co-residential, with men and women living on separate floors.

The Faculty Head of House lives in an apartment on the first floor, while students live on floors 2-5 of this five story building. Each residential floor has a study area and three shared bathrooms with showers. In addition, there is a lobby, study area, laundry room, seminar room, music room, and communal kitchen on the first floor. The building is fully air conditioned. Corridors and common areas are carpeted. Student rooms have tiled floors.
summary: Sutherland House is located on the Martha Rivers Ingram Commons. Sutherland provides modern features in a classic building housing 162 first-year students in traditional double rooms with men and women living on separate floors.

name: West House
description:
West House

Huili Hong, Faculty Head of West House

Huili Hong (she, her, hers) is a professor of the practice in teaching and learning. Her research interests include literacy studies focusing on children’s language and literacy processes, multilingual learner education, and teacher preparation. Her teaching, service, and scholarly endeavors emphasize languages and literacies as tools for students’ equitable learning, free expression, imagination, and engagement with the world in critical, constructive, caring, and enjoyable ways. Professor Hong joined the Vanderbilt faculty in 2022, and has been faculty head of West House since 2024. She looks forward to creating a vibrant and inclusive community in West House. She aims to foster students' sense of belonging and connections and build an intercultural and intellectual community.

The House

West House provides modern features in a classic structure. The building was completely renovated in 1986 and received new finishes and furnishing in the summers of 2005 and 2006. West houses approximately 112 first-year men and women on separate floors, mostly in traditional double rooms. A resident adviser lives on each floor.

The Faculty Head of House lives in an apartment on the first floor. Also located on the first floor is a large laundry, two seminar rooms, music practice rooms, a study area, and a full-sized kitchen for the House.

West House was built in 1922.
summary: Located on the Martha Rivers Ingram Commons, West House provides modern features in a classic structure. West houses approximately 112 first-year men and women on separate floors, mostly in traditional double rooms.

name: Blakemore House
description:
Opened in August, 2012, Blakemore House is home to 142 upperclass women and men. Students live on three floors in a mixture of single rooms, double rooms and triple rooms. Women and men live on separate floors. Featuring the campus’s only double rooms with full private bathrooms, Blakemore House is a popular choice for students seeking large rooms, plentiful lounge and study space, and convenient parking.

Blakemore House is sited on a large property that boasts a very large backyard with walking paths, picnic tables, patio furniture, and attractive safety lighting. Inside, students enjoy a fireplace lounge, a large lounge/study area looking out on the back yard, music practice room, and study room.

Blakemore House is located on Vanderbilt Place next to the Vanderbilt University Police Department and less than one block from Dudley Stadium and McGugin Center, the home of Vanderbilt Athletics. It is also close to Centennial Park, Paddock Place shopping, and many West End Avenue restaurants and shops.
summary: Submit a maintenance request by https://apphost2.its.vanderbilt.edu/studentbiz/MaintReq/.

name: Highland Quadrangle
description:
The Premier Place to Live

HQ Mission: To engage all residents through meaningful impactful moments

Learning Outcome: Students living in the Highland Quad will be able to walk away with new or enhanced skills and a better connection to those living in Highland Quad.

Chaffin Place

Chaffin Place is across the street from the Student Recreation Center. Bordrered by 24th and 25th Avenues, it is convenient to most of the athletic facilities on campus.

Each Chaffin apartment consists of two double bedrooms, a living/dining area, a full kitchen with electric range and large refrigerator and bath with tub/shower combination. Each unit is fully carpeted and air-conditioned. In addition, each apartment has its own patio and patio furniture. The dimensions of the usable living and dining room combination is 12′ x 18′. The bedroom dimension is 10′ x12′.

Chaffin Place apartments come in two general types. Some of the apartments (series 100,200, 300 and 800) are townhouses with bedrooms and baths on the second floor. Other apartments (series 400, 500, 600, and 700) have all amenities on one floor, and the apartments are stacked three high (for example, 732 above 722 above 712).

Chaffin houses a total of 192 single undergraduates in 48 apartment units. The 100 series apartments on the 25th Avenue side. The 300 and 800 series apartments are encircled by the remainder of the complex and open onto the Lewis/Morgan courtyard area. A 24 hour information desk is located nearby Lewis House.

Chaffin Place was completed in 1974 and is named in honor of Dean Nora Chaffin.

Lewis House and Morgan House

Lewis House and Morgan House are twin eleven-story buildings that opened in December of 1962. Located on the southern part of campus at 1410 25th Avenue South, they are across the street from the Student Recreation Center and are convenient to most athletic facilities.

Lewis House and Morgan House both house undergraduate students. Each floor has 1 efficiency apartment, 5 one-bedroom apartments and 6 two-bedroom apartments.

A 7 day a week information desk is located in Lewis House, and serves the entire Highland Quadrangle area. The area coordinator for the area is located in Morgan 111. 

Mayfield Lodges

The Mayfield Experience. Ten students, one lodge, one project!  The Mayfield Living Learning Community seeks to empower students with a platform for pursuing their service and learning interests.  The Mayfield’s provide students an opportunity to create a self-generated, self-directed group project with the guidance of a faculty advisor of their choice.  To apply groups must create and present a detailed proposal of their project’s purpose, goals, and timeline.  Following a panel style interview process, groups with well-formed and well-articulated projects may be admitted into the community.  Upon entry into the lodges, program requirements include monthly reports, weekly meetings, lectures with faculty and staff, regular meetings with faculty advisors and participation in educational programs for the campus and community at large.
summary: Highland Quadrangle is an upperclass housing facility made up of Chaffin Place, Lewis House, and Morgan House, and the Mayfiled Lodges.

name: McGill Project
description:
The McGill Project is a Living & Learning community dedicated to free thought, creative expression, and inclusive community. Project members consider themselves part of an ongoing tradition that encompasses a number of innovative and diverse programs and activities. With over 100 residents, McGill is a place where students are pushed to express themselves culturally, socially, politically, and intellectually.

 

Experiencing the Project
Students considering applying to the Project are encouraged to attend one of the community’s McGill Hours, Coffeehouses or other events year round. Prospective Project Members can always just stop by McGill Hall to experience firsthand the Project community. As the Application Process period unfolds, interested students can attend informational meetings to also learn about the Project.

Participant Selection
The Selections Committee, consisting solely of McGill Project members, will review applications, interview applicants, and recommend people for admission to the Project.

Near Spring Break, students accepted for residential membership will receive e-mails prompting them to accept their invitation within a specific time frame. Once they have accepted their invitation, students will not be able to apply to other housing, but will be required to complete the standard Vanderbilt University Housing contract for their living space in McGill.
summary: The McGill Project is a Living & Learning community dedicated to free thought, creative expression, and inclusive community.

name: McTyeire International House
description:
McTyeire International House

What is McTyeire?

The McTyeire International House offers programs to students seeking to improve their fluency in French, German, Japanese, Chinese, Spanish, or Russian as well as a program, offered in English, to students seeking to learn more about international topics. Students are required to speak their target languages or discuss international topics at specific activities, including weekly study breaks and international dinners served Monday through Thursday in McTyeire’s own dining room. McTyeire’s kitchen specializes in international cuisine from around the world.

For more information about the McTyeire International House, please consult the McTyeire International House webpage
summary: The McTyeire International House program promotes the use of foreign languages and the awareness of different cultures. McTyeire is located on the main campus and features 97 single rooms and its own cafeteria.

name: Office of Special Art Initiatives
description: This is the best organization on campus, more info to folow
summary: This office works with all student Performing Arts Groups on campus and acts as the liaison for Vanderbilt University to help bridge the gap between the University and the Nashville community in the arts and service opportunities.

name: Vanderbilt Dance Program
description:
Dance and Dance Fitness classes in Ballet, Jazz, Modern, Tap, Ballroom, Salsa, Hip Hop, World dance forms, Yoga, Pilates, and workout classes. Performance with Vanderbilt Dance Theatre is based on Auditions held in the beginning of the Fall Semester with performance in the Spring. Levels in classes run from beginning to advanced levels in most dance forms. Classes are held in Memorial Gym in 3 dance studios, most classes are held in the evening from 4:30 until 9:30 with a few morning and Saturday Classes.

Classes are geared to Students from age 14 through retirement age with every level and background. Beginning classes are aimed at true beginners no experience needed at all. Advanced classes are true advanced classes andinclude students with many years of experence and training. Some of our students are professional dancers and teachers from other groups in town. No grades or test just the joy of dancing.
summary: Vanderbilt Dance Program offers Dance Classes in all dance forms and levels for students, staff and community. We also produce a professional level dance concert as Vanderbilt Dance Theatre.

name: Project Dialogue
description:
Project Dialogue is a University-wide program that seeks to involve the entire Vanderbilt community in public discourse and reflection connecting classroom learning with larger societal issues. Project Dialogue dinner series give students and faculty an opportunity to engage in conversation on a number of topics around the dinner table over a delicious meal.

Speakers and Artists for Project Dialogue have included: Sandra Bernhard, Naomi Wolf, Cornel West, Arthur Schlesinger, Jr., Oliver Sacks, Danny Seo, Mary Lucking-Reiley, Neil Howe, Scott Turow, Adrienne Outlaw, John Douglas, Gore Vidal, Barbara Ehrenreich, Al Franken, John Ashcroft, Archbishop Desmond Tutu and Elie Wiesel.
summary: Project Dialogue is a University-wide program that seeks to involve the entire Vanderbilt community throughout the academic year in public discourse and reflection connecting classroom learning with larger societal issues.

name: Holocaust Lecture Series
description:
The Holocaust Memorial Lecture Series (HMLS) was established by the University Chaplain, the Rev. Beverly A. Asbury, in 1977 and has become the longest continuous lecture series on the Holocaust at an American university.  Dedicated to reflection on ourselves and our society in the wake of the Holocaust, for over 40 years HLS has brought notable scholars, Survivors and Liberators, and artistic examinations of the Holocaust to Vanderbilt’s campus.

For more information, please visit the HLS website.
summary: Dedicated to reflection on ourselves and our society in the wake of the Holocaust, for over 40 years HMLS has brought notable scholars, Survivors, Rescuers and Liberators, and artistic examinations of the Holocaust to Vanderbilt’s campus.

name: Housing and Residential Experience (HRE)
description: Housing and Residential Experience provides safe, secure, comfortable, accessible and healthy housing for its residents. Programming for residents builds communities in which students’ social needs for belonging, friendship, recognition, respect and dignity are met. These communities foster learning, personal development, academic achievement, and successful transitions for both new students and graduates.
summary: OHARE has responsibility for all 34 of the residence halls on campus, staffing and programming within the residence halls, living learning communities, facilities management, and the Office of Conferences.

organizationType.name: Student Affairs Department, Office, or Program

name: Vanderbilt Production Services
description: The inside provider of Audio Visual solutions in the Sarratt Student Center, the Student Life Center and able to operate across the entirety of the Vanderbilt Campus, we provide expertise and equipment for lighting, audio and video to the Vanderbilt Community.
summary: Provides Audio Visual and event technical production services, equipment and consulting for the Vanderbilt Community

name: Center for Student Wellbeing
description: The mission of the Center for Student Wellbeing is to create a culture that supports the personal development and academic success of students using an integrative, holistic framework.
summary: To serve the Vanderbilt community by coordinating support efforts on campus in order to assist students facing challenges, stressors, and barriers that impede academic and personal success.

organizationType.name: Student Affairs Department, Office, or Program

name: Net Impact - Vanderbilt
description: We connect our student members with opportunities to explore the intersection of business and impact. This means everything from environmental and social sustainability at the corporate level, to workplace diversity and inclusion, to sustainable food supply and social entrepreneurship and innovation, to the role of non-profits and government.
summary: Net Impact is on a mission to inspire and equip international leaders to build a more just and sustainable world.

name: 100% Owen
description: 100% Owen enables the Owen community to engage with the larger Nashville community in meaningful ways beyond financial support. At Owen, many students are new to Nashville and 100% Owen provides a range of diverse and inclusive opportunities for students to easily serve their community. The club also serves to educate students about various types of involvement with philanthropic endeavors to prepare them for lifelong service to others.
summary: 100% Owen seeks to enrich the Owen experience for students, faculty, and staff through the promotion and organization of community service activities and fundraising opportunities in the Nashville Community.

name: Vanderbilt Student Media
description: For Vanderbilt students, the divisions of Vanderbilt Student Communications, Inc. serve as laboratory, classroom, and professional experience in the absence of an academic journalism or broadcasting department. Hundreds of undergraduate students are involved each year as regular staff members or contributors. Each year the divisions of Vanderbilt student media distribute thousands of student-produced stories, air thousands of hours of music, news, sports, interviews and entertainment on radio and television, stream information and audio to the world via numerous internet sites, and cover hundreds of hours of campus activities in service to the university community audience.
summary: Vanderbilt Student Communications

name: Vanderbilt Political Review
description: The Vanderbilt Political Review is Vanderbilt's only nonpartisan political journal. Our mission is to 1) provide a political forum for free expression, a free exchange of ideas, dissemination of news, and an outlet for creative work, 2) to create awareness and encourage discourse on political issues and events, and 3) to provide an opportunity for students to learn and gain competency in mass communications skills.
summary: The Vanderbilt Political Review is Vanderbilt's oldest and largest nonpartisan academic journal, featuring articles and essays on a variety of political topics.

name: Owen Golf Club
description: The Owen Golf Club seeks to grow the game (golf) in underrepresented populations and provide a healthy, fun outlet to the greater Vanderbilt community. The Owen Golf Club seeks to provide an arena for both experienced, non-experienced golfers, and new to the game persons to enjoy the game and its many benefits in life and career. Membership includes, but not limited to: opportunities for lessons, educational programs for under-represented international students in the world of golf, a members-only tournament, and preferred access to the annual spring charity golf tournament.
summary: Owen Graduate School of Management Golf Club

name: Owen Sports Business Association
description:
The Owen Sports Business Association is dedicated to making Owen one of the nation's premier business schools at the intersection of sports and business. With a burgeoning Nashville sports scene, OSBA is uniquely positioned to develop relationships with local franchises and universities to apply classroom learnings to real world, sports-focused business problems.

OSBA joined the nationwide MBA Sports Business Alliance, which is a national consortium of top business schools dedicated to building a cohort of sports-interested MBA students. Through education, recruiting resources, and networking, OSBA will leverage it's membership in the MBA SBA to help aspiring students recruit and land jobs in sports. OSBA leadership secured a spot on the SBA board to further advance this effort.

On top of professional and educational efforts, OSBA will organize fun activities to generate camaraderie in the Owen community, such as field days, watch parties, and outings.
summary: The mission of the Owen Sports Business Association is to educate, inform, and expose Owen students to the business of Sport.

name: Biological Sciences Graduate Student Association
description: The purpose of the Biological Sciences Graduate Student Association is to increase comradery and mentorship between all graduate students in Biological Sciences by holding social and professional development events, aiding in graduate student recruitment, promoting international and service events, inviting renowned speakers to share their work, and helping graduate students through their qualifying exams and other phases of their graduate career.
summary: The purpose of the Biological Sciences Graduate Student Association is to increase comradery between all students in Biological Sciences by holding social events throughout the school year and recruiting graduate students to the department.

name: National Student Speech, Language, & Hearing Association
description: This organization is the Vanderbilt Chapter of the National Student Speech Language and Hearing Association. We strive to enhance our members education professionally, academically, and socially throughout the year by hosting events along with other opportunities. We are made up of graduate students currently studying Speech Language Pathology and Audiology in addition to undergraduate students who are interested in one of these two careers.
summary: Vanderbilt NSSLHA is local chapter of national NSSLHA. It provides a forum for graduate students in human communication sciences to engage in professional development, community service, social events, DEI initiatives, and fundraising efforts.

name: Asian-Pacific American Medical Student Association-Med School
description:
The Asian Pacific American Medical Students Association (APAMSA) serves as a community for students and leaders to engage in health and social issues pertinent to APA communities. APAMSA also provides an important venue for medical students to meet, exchange experiences, and develop personally and professionally through leadership and service.

This organization is only open to current VUSM MD Students.
summary: APAMSA serves serves as a community for students leaders to engage in health and social issues pertinent to APA communities.

name: Careers in Medicine
description:
The Careers in Medicine program at Vanderbilt University School of Medicine supports students as they Explore career options in the health care field, provides structured advising as they Decide among various specialty choices, and paves the path for graduates to Succeed as they begin to embark upon their professional journey. We accomplish these goals by creating the foundation necessary to cultivate leadership and professionalism, by facilitating early and sustained exposure to various career options, and by fostering an atmosphere of dedicated mentorship by faculty and alumni.

**Please note that Anchorlink membership for CiM is reserved for CiM Chairs and Interest Group (IG) leaders. We encourage all VUSM students to join any IG Outlook groups that interest them to stay appraised of events and opportunities!
summary: CiM is a Vanderbilt University School of Medicine student organization dedicated to helping medical students with professional decision making.

name: Wilderness Medical Society
description: Wilderness medicine is the provision of emergency care in a remote setting that requires improvisation, teamwork, and outdoor skills due to limited resources, environmental factors, and the unavailability of nearby medical facilities. This organization provides education on wilderness medicine concepts and skills, in addition to getting students outside in the local environment.
summary: WMS is a group of medical students dedicated to advancing education in wilderness medicine through hands-on experiences of medical workshops, lectures, and outdoor activities.

name: VUSM Student Wellness Committee
description:
“This organization is only open to current VUSM MD Students.”

Among medical students across the country, it is often felt that the best or even the only way to survive and succeed amidst the intense academic pressure is to give up all other activities that compete with academics for time—even those activities of great personal importance. Such decisions made in the interest of short-term survival frequently lead to longer-term sacrifices in healthy living, as habits developed in the formative years of medical school are carried into post-graduate training and, ultimately, one’s profession. Vanderbilt University School of Medicine’s Wellness Program was created to counteract this pervasive culture by celebrating student involvement in those activities important to their physical, social, and spiritual wellness. The Wellness Program is a student-run initiative composed of five committees, each supporting a different area of student well-being—physical, emotional/spiritual, interpersonal, academic/professional, and environmental/community. Throughout the year, each Wellness Committee is responsible for supporting student life through various program events and resources for medical students.
summary: This is a student-run organization aimed at supporting Vanderbilt medical student wellness and to promote physical, mental, emotional, interpersonal, academic, and community health and wellbeing.

name: Global Health Organization - Med School
description:
This organization is only open to current VUSM MD Students.

As the School of Medicine's network for students interested in opportunities and careers in global health, we seek to raise awareness of important issues in medicine across the globe. Our primary initiatives include a fall gathering of students and faculty interested in global health, organizing the annual World Health Week speaker series in the spring, supporting students seeking involvement in global health research projects, and supporting international populations within the greater Nashville area.
summary: The School of Medicine's network for students interested in opportunities and careers in global health.

name: Vanderbilt Speaking Club (Formerly Vanderbilt Toastmasters)
description:
Welcome to Vanderbilt Speaking Club, founded in September 2020! (Previously Vanderbilt Toastmasters) Members deliver speeches and receive constructive feedback on how to become a better, more effective speaker. We provide the Vanderbilt community with the tools and confidence they need to take their communication and leadership abilities to the next level. Please email victoria.p.travassos@vanderbilt.edu if you have any questions or interest in joining our chapter!

Please visit our Instagram page here.
summary: Vanderbilt Speaking Club - become a more persuasive speaker. All are welcome to and no prior experience is necessary!

name: VUSM Music in Medicine
description:
This organization is only open to current VUSM MD Students.

VUSM Music in Medicine is a Vanderbilt University School of Medicine (VUSM) organization dedicated to exploring the intersection of music and medicine. The purpose of the organization is to improve the mental and physical health of people undergoing stress, including patients, patients’ family members and caregivers, medical students, and medical center staff. Through musical performances for individuals or groups, the organization approaches healing holistically, eases suffering, and aids recovery. It encourages student involvement and promotes service opportunities within the Vanderbilt community and Medical Center (VUMC). The group facilitates members’ exploration and expression of music through social events and service, foster community, and provide company, comfort, and joy to patients and others who may need a pick-me-up tune. If interested in getting involved or arranging a performance, please email elizabeth.sun@vanderbilt.edu.

A key component of Music in Medicine is Radial Grooves, VUSM's acclaimed a cappella group. Radial Grooves also holds the honor of being one of its most inclusive student organizations, having welcomed students from the Schools of Medicine, Audiology, Nursing, and Speech Pathology, along with the Vanderbilt Graduate School. We perform unique versions of popular songs for patients at the hospital, nursing facilities, and community events such as the School of Medicine Cadaver Ball! If you interested in joining Radial Grooves, have questions about the group, or would like to request a performance, please email rida.z.bonday@vanderbilt.edu.
summary: Music in Medicine serves as an umbrella organization for music activities by students in the School of Medicine, with a focus on easing suffering and promoting wellness for patients, family members and visitors, medical students, staff, and others.

name: Vanderbilt Latino Medical Student Organization
description:
Our Mission: The Latino Medical Student Association exists to unite and empower current and future physicians through service, mentorship, and education to advocate for the improved health of the Hispanic & Latina/o/x community in the United States.
​
Our Vision: We envision a world in which improved health outcomes for all patients arise as a result of the following:

  • Increased representation and support of healthcare professionals from backgrounds underrepresented among the medical workforce;
  • Increased awareness of critical issues affecting the health of the Hispanic, Latina/o/x, and other marginalized communities; and
  • Increased efforts to reduce disparities in the ways patients of color experience the American healthcare system.

Part of our objectives include:

  • To unify all Latino medical students into one organization
  • To provide a voice for underrepresented medical students
  • To actively promote recruitment and retention of Latino students at all levels
  • To educate medical students on Latino health issues
  • To advocate for the rights of Latinos in health care
  • To provide leadership opportunities for Latinos
  • To promote volunteerism in the Latino community

This organization is only open to current VUSM MD Students.
summary: The Vanderbilt chapter of the national Latino Medical Student Association strives to partner with local organizations to address healthcare needs of the local Latino population and promote Latino culture within the Vanderbilt community

name: Pride in Medicine
description:
Pride In Medicine is devoted to maintaining an active LGBTQ+ community at VUSM, educating VUSM students and faculty on topics in LGBTQ healthcare and culture, and advocating for change benefiting LGBTQ+ individuals both in VUSM and in the broader Nashville community.

Pride In Medicine is a chapter of the Medical Student Pride Alliance.

>> Sign up for our email list here to get regular emails about events and updates <<

MD Admissions Website: https://medschool.vanderbilt.edu/md-admissions/pride-in-medicine/

> For information about LGBTQ-related work at the medical center, a great resource is the Vanderbilt Program in LGBT Health. More information can be found at the program website:

https://www.vumc.org/lgbtq/
summary: Pride in Medicine is a student organization within VUSM committed to creating community for LGBTQ+ medical students and allies and advocating for improving LGBTQ+ health within the medical school, medical center, and broader Nashville community.

name: Maimonides Jewish Student Organization
description:
The Maimonides Society, the Jewish Medical Student Association at Vanderbilt Medical School, is a community of students, faculty and housestaff dedicated to enriching the Jewish experience at Vanderbilt Medical Center for Jewish and non Jewish students. Through speaker programs, social programming, and culturally-centered events, we aim to enhance Jewish community life throughout the medical center.

summary: Jewish Medical Student Association

name: Medical Students for Choice
description: "Medical Students for Choice is dedicated to ensuring that women receive the full range of reproductive healthcare choices. MSFC recognizes that one of the greatest obstacles to safe and legal abortion is the absence of trained providers. As medical students and residents, we work to make reproductive health care, including abortion, a part of standard medical education and residency training."
summary: Vanderbilt University's chapter of Medical Students for Choice.  A national organization dedicated to reproductive rights for all women.

name: Recovering Medical Equipment for Those in Need
description: Vanderbilt’s REMEDY group collects unused medical supplies from operating rooms, labs, and clinics, sending them where they are needed. Ultimately, the mission of REMEDY is two-fold: to provide medical supplies to locations in need while reducing waste from Vanderbilt hospitals.
summary: REMEDY (Recovering Medical Equipment for Those in Need) aims to collect unused medical supplies and send to local or international communities in need

name: Society of Saints Cosmas & Damian
description:
Why is Faith important? Read a former SSCD president's answer here:

Why is Faith Important for Physicians during Discernment and Formation?

The SSCD exists to provide support and community to Christian, particularly Catholic, medical students at Vanderbilt, so that they may grow in their faith throughout the course of their medical training. To accomplish this, we aim to connect students with one another and with Catholic physicians, both locally and nationally, through a strong relationship with the wider Catholic Medical Association.

Through small group discussions, lectures, worship, and informal gatherings, we hope to address the physical, emotional, moral and spiritual challenges of medical practice and strengthen one another through religious formation and community.  

Regular & Ongoing SSCD Events:

SMALL GROUP BOOK DISCUSSIONS

Usually held during lunch hours in January. We gather to discuss the writings of famous Catholic or Christian authors. Past authors have included C.S. Lewis, GK Chesterton, and Flannery O'Connor.  In 2025, we read Story of a Soul by St. Therese of Lisieux.

HIPPOCRATIC OATH BANQUET

One of our biggest events of the year! Generally early on in the academic year, physicians from around Nashville gather for a banquet and a guest lecture. This is a great event for students to get to know Catholic faculty in the Nashville area. The evening gathers us together so we may re-connect with the Catholic community in the city, and together re-dedicate ourselves to the Catholic Hippocratic Oath.

WHITE COAT MASS

Held once a year, this is a Mass celebrated especially for those in health care, and is followed by a reception and a speaker. This event is widely attended by medical students, physicians and healthcare workers from the Catholic Medical Association, and graduate and professional students from other scientific disciplines.

EVENTS WITH THE CATHOLIC MEDICAL ASSOCIATION GUILD

The SSCD regularly has off-campus events with the Nashville Guild of the Catholic Medical Association. In 2023, we gathered twice with the CMA. The first meeting was a debrief session about the COVID-19 pandemic and how it affected our faith and medical practice. The second was a medical student panel about Dr. John Stone's poem, Gaudeamus Igitur, and its inspiration for us as Catholic healthcare professionals. For historical videos, see the CMA Vimeo page! (https://vimeo.com/nashvillecma/videos)

summary:
The purpose of the Society of Saints Cosmas and Damian ("SSCD") is to provide a supportive community where Vanderbilt medical students can share and integrate their Catholic faith with a career in medicine.
(Click "Read More" to learn more about SSCD!)

name: Universities Allied for Essential Medicines - Vanderbilt
description: The mission of UAEM- Vanderbilt is three-fold. We will aim to a) raise awareness of the issues surrounding access to pharmaceutical drugs by increasing our chapter membership and hosting regular on-campus initiatives, b) work directly with the Center for Technology Transfer at Vanderbilt to ensure that Vanderbilt is 100% compliant with the FDA Amendment Act requiring all clinical trial results to be filed on the public registry, and c) influence national drug policy by meeting with US Congressmen and Senators. Throughout this work, we will seek out collaborative efforts with other UAEM Chapters both in North America and abroad as well as engage in direct collaboration with VUMS and Vanderbilt Law. Through all of this, we intend to enhance access to pharmaceuticals and to push Vanderbilt to become a leader in research innovation by focusing on transparency in their research and development.
summary: UAEM believes that we, as students of a research institution, sit in a key position to ensure better access to pharmaceutical drugs for all by influencing research innovation, drug development, & transparency in negotiations both at Vanderbilt and in TN.

name: Vanderbilt Educational Garden Initiative
description:
Through the Vanderbilt Educational Garden Initiative (VEGI), the Vanderbilt School of Medicine student body combats food deserts and their consequent chronic disease epidemic.

A wholesome diet is crucial to physical and mental wellbeing. However, urban planning, economic precarity, and deficient education conspire to block vulnerable Tennesseans from adopting nutritious, plant-forward diets. Furthermore, healthcare professionals may neglect continuing education in America’s food system when pursuing a narrower, medical model of disease.

Thus, we operate a community garden, greenhouse, and composting facility on the grounds of the University Club. The garden serves as a learning laboratory to provide hands-on education in urban farming to Vanderbilt students. The fruits of our labor are distributed to patients attending the Shade Tree Clinic, a free community clinic for uninsured Tennesseans. This lowers behavioral activation energy and removes barriers to the pursuit of a plant-forward diet.

Our club provides three deliverables:

  • Fresh produce to vulnerable Nashvillians
  • Practical education on the food system to Vanderbilt’s student body
  • A community allying medical students, undergraduates, food system nonprofits, and uninsured patients.
summary: Medical School Organization

name: Peabody Professional & Graduate Student Association
description: The PPGSA is comprised of diverse student representatives from all Peabody departments and programs. The goals of the PPGSA are to foster and promote student voice at Peabody; to advise and assist with student support programming; and, to advocate for student needs.
summary: The PPGSA is comprised of diverse student representatives from all Peabody departments and programs. The goals of the PPGSA are to promote student voice at Peabody; to advise and assist with student support programming; and, to advocate for student needs

name: Owen Texas Club
summary: The Texas Club aims to attract strong candidates from Texas, to provide networking opportunities for students who wish to work in Texas, and to continue to build strong relationships with Texan alumni, all within a non-exclusive environment.

name: VPB Homecoming Committee
description: The Vanderbilt Programming Board Homecoming Committee was formalized in the late 90s, early 2000s. It was created to unify all campus Homecoming activities. The Homecoming Committee works tirelessly to provide community-building activities to incite enthusiasm for Vanderbilt athletics and alumni relations. These events include spirit events, service activities (Blood Drive), and the Outstanding Senior Award.
summary: The Homecoming Committee works to provide interesting activities to incite enthusiasm for Vanderbilt athletics and alumni relations. These events include spirit events, service activities, and the Outstanding Senior Award.

name: International Lens Film Series
description:
International Lens (iLens) uses introductions and post-screening discussions as a forum to promote conversations among Vanderbilt's diverse community of students, faculty, and staff. This film series strives to transcend geographic, ethnic, religious, linguistic, and political boundaries, to engage in cross-cultural understanding through cinema.

The series is presented by the Cinema & Media Arts Program in collaboration with the Dean of Students in partnership with academic and other departments, centers, institutes, and programs across the campus. 

For program announcements and notifications of upcoming films, subscribe to the iLensmail LISTSERV.
summary: A Film Series Curated by the Vanderbilt Cinema & Media Arts Program

name: VUSM Council of Class Officers
description:
The Vanderbilt Medical School Association (VMSA) consists of every medical student enrolled in the School of Medicine.  The VMSA is governed by the Council of Class Officers (CCO).

The CCO is a group comprised of class officers from all four years of medical school. These positions include: President (1), Vice President (1), Secretary/Treasurer (1), and Class Chairs (3). As the student government of the Vanderbilt University School of Medicine, the CCO strives to improve medical student life, address student and faculty concerns, and strengthen the medical school community. We meet on a monthly basis with all School of Medicine Deans to discuss pertinent issues concerning our school, from both the administrative and student perspectives.
