name: Energy and Environmental Law Society
description: Vanderbilt University Law School's Energy and Environmental Law Society is a student organization for students who are concerned about energy and environmental issues, and/or interested in careers in energy or environmental law.
summary: Vanderbilt University Law School's Energy and Environmental Law Society is a student organization for students who are concerned about energy and environmental issues, and/or interested in careers in energy or environmental law.

name: Black Law Students Association
description:
The purpose of BLSA shall be to utilize the collective resources of the members, associate members, and to:

a)  Foster the community needs of Black law students;

b)  Articulate and promote the professional needs and goals of Black law students;

c)  Foster and encourage academic success and professional competence;

e) Instill in future Black attorneys a sense of responsibility and commitment to the needs of the Black community;

f) Influence the wider legal community to bring about meaningful change to meet the needs of the Black community; and

g) Do all things necessary and lawful to accomplish these purposes.
summary: The purpose of BLSA is to utilize the collective resources of the members to articulate, promote, and develop the professional, academic, and social needs and goals of Black law students and foster professional competence.

name: Middle Eastern & North African Law Students Association
description: MENALSA aims to situate the Vanderbilt Legal Community in a position that fosters informed and meaningful dialogue about the Middle East & North Africa. Members enjoy educational events relating to MENA legal systems and policies, as well as professional and social events.
summary: MENALSA aims to situate the Vanderbilt Legal Community in a position that fosters informed and meaningful dialogue about the Middle East & North Africa.  MENALSA members will enjoy educational and cultural events relating to the MENA region.

name: IEEE Vanderbilt
description:
IEEE Vanderbilt’s purpose is to provide an organization around which Vanderbilt students can share in their common interests for electrical engineering, computer engineering, and computer science. This will be achieved through club meetings, speakers, social events, and interactions with the greater IEEE community.

 

GroupMe: https://groupme.com/join_group/102954362/j3ylLz8S
summary: IEEE Vanderbilt provides an organization around which Vanderbilt students can share in their common interests for electrical engineering, computer engineering, and computer science in relation with the national IEEE organization.

name: Owen Japan Business Club
description: The mission of the Owen Japan Business Club is to introduce Japan’s business and culture, support Owen to increase its presence in Japan and assist prospective students. Events include Global Food Festival and Japan trip.
summary:
Owen Japan Business Club's mission is to add value to Vanderbilt University Owen Graduate School of Management by 
・ Introducing Japan's business and culture
・ Supporting our school to market Owen in Japan
・ Assisting prospective students

name: Owen Student Government Association
description: OSGA is the primary communication conduit between OGSM administration and students. Furthermore, OSGA helps to shape current and future policy matters and strengthen OGSM’s sense of community. The Owen community is defined as full-time students, student spouses, alumni, faculty, staff, administrators, and corporations with whom Owen has a relationship.
summary: The Owen Student Government Association (OSGA) is the student government for Masters of Business Administration, Masters of Accounting, and Masters of Finance students at the Owen Graduate School of Management (OGSM) at Vanderbilt University.

name: Vanderbilt Army ROTC
description:
The Go Gold Battalion has a proud unit history, beginning at Vanderbilt in 1947 with a small Medical Corps unit for medical school students.  It has since expanded into a program to train, develop, and commission officers of all branches into the United States Army.

Army ROTC (Reserve Officers' Training Corps) is a challenging series of college electives which teach leadership and management skills. The program prepares both men and women for responsibilities as Second Lieutenants in the United States Army, Army Reserve, or Army National Guard upon graduation.

As part of the Gold Go Battalion, cadets have a normal college experience as well as participate in ROTC classes, leadership labs, field training, and physical training. Other training activities include: Airborne School, Air Assault School, Mountain Warfare School, Northern Warfare School, CTLT, along with various internship opportunities.  Go Gold cadets also have fully-funded opportunities to travel abroad during the summer through the study of strategic languages.

Army ROTC is one of the smartest college courses you can take. It offers opportunities and challenges that can put you on the fast track to success in life. You'll develop the confidence, self-esteem, motivation and leadership skills you'll need regardless of your career plans. The qualities that ROTC training instills will be vital to a productive and rewarding future.

For more information, please visit our website: https://www.vanderbilt.edu/army/
summary: Army ROTC is a Cadet-run organization charged with training future Officers of the United States Army.

name: Vanderbilt University American Nuclear Society Student Section
summary: Our mission is to advance the understanding and application of nuclear science and technology by providing students with educational, professional, and leadership opportunities.

name: Mock Trial Board at Vanderbilt Law School
description: The Vanderbilt Mock Trial Board aims to assist students in developing public speaking skills, proper courtroom decorum, and a basic knowledge of the federal rules of evidence through the operation of all competitive trial advocacy tournaments at Vanderbilt Law School. During the fall semester, the Mock Trial Board holds several lectures teaching the 1L class how to behave in court, how to write and present the parts of a trial, and how to argue objections. The Board then operates a tournament for all members of the 1L class who wish to participate in this first competitive experience of their law school career. In the spring semester, the Board sponsors two teams of three to travel and compete in various nation-wide competitions, such as the Texas Young Lawyers Association regional mock trial tournament. In the spring, the Board also operates an intramural competition for all interested 2Ls, as a review of trial advocacy skills and the federal rules of evidence.
summary: The Vanderbilt Mock Trial Board aims to assist students in developing public speaking skills, proper courtroom decorum, and a basic knowledge of the federal rules of evidence through intramural tournaments and traveling team competitions.

name: If/When/How: Lawyering for Reproductive Justice
description:
If/When/How: Lawyering for Reproductive Justice, formerly Law Students for Reproductive Justice, supports law students and legal professionals aiming to increase dialogue, awareness, and activism to protect and expand reproductive rights as basic civil and human rights.

If/When/How is a national non-profit network of law students, professors, and legal professionals committed to fostering the next wave of legal experts for the reproductive justice movement. Mobilizing and mentoring new lawyers and scholars is a long-term strategy that will build capacity, vision, and leadership for a more successful reproductive justice movement. Law functions as both a catalyst to prompt the expansion of rights and a tool with which to hold governments and communities accountable for the delivery of liberty, equality, dignity, and fairness promised by those rights.

Reproductive justice will exist when all people can exercise the rights and access the resources they need to thrive and to decide if, when, and how to create and sustain a family with dignity, free from discrimination, coercion, or violence. We dedicate ourselves to ensuring access to medically accurate, age-appropriate, culturally competent sex and sexuality education, as well as comprehensive, quality reproductive healthcare, including prenatal care, postnatal care, contraception, abortion services, and alternative reproductive technologies for all people, free from coercion, discrimination, and violence.

Reproductive justice recognizes the ways race, class, sex, age, sexual orientation, gender expression, immigration status, and ability impact access, agency, and autonomy in shaping one’s reproductive destiny. By applying an intersectional analysis, Reproductive justice advocates recognize how each individual is uniquely affected by barriers to information, resources, health care, and social supports at different stages throughout their reproductive lives.

Today, a growing number of law schools offer comprehensive courses in reproductive rights, law, and justice, but opportunities for professional training remain scarce. As individuals, each of us faces difficulties obtaining the education and training we need to become strong, successful advocates for reproductive justice. Together, we can change this situation and become a powerful, educated force that defends and expands reproductive rights in the United States and around the world.
summary: If/When/How: Lawyering for Reproductive Justice, formerly Law Students for Reproductive Justice, supports law students and legal professionals aiming to increase dialogue, awareness, and activism to protect and expand reproductive rights.

name: Greater China Business Club
description: Greater China Business Club (GCBC) was created to promote cross-cultural communications and explore shared business opportunities between Owen and Greater China. GCBC provides insight into one of the fastest growing economies in the world, and helps Owen students better understand the culture, customs, and business practices in China through special events, speaker series, and seminars. By leveraging alumni resources and other resources, GCBC will assist students pursuing career opportunities in China. Additionally, GCBC will facilitate the integration of students from Greater China into the U.S. Culture, enriching their life and learning experience.
summary: The mission of the Greater China Business Club (GCBC) is to promote cross-cultural communications and explore shared business opportunities between Owen and Greater China.

name: Vanderbilt Undergraduate Consulting Club
description: We are a dynamic and ambitious group of undergraduate students passionate about the field of consulting. Our club aims to provide members with valuable opportunities to develop consulting skills, network with industry professionals, and gain real-world consulting experience. Our mission is to foster a community of aspiring consultants by creating a supportive environment where members can learn, grow, and excel in the consulting industry. We strive to offer a platform for students to gain exposure to different facets of consulting, including strategy, operations, management, and more.
summary: We're a dynamic, ambitious group of undergrads passionate about consulting, offering opportunities to hone skills, network with pros, and gain real-world experience.

name: Vanderbilt Habitat for Humanity
description: Vanderbilt Habitat for Humanity is a service organization dedicated to issues around homelessness and poverty-housing. From monthly service projects to weekend builds and spring break trips, there is an opportunity for everyone to get involved!
summary: Habitat for Humanity is an on-campus service organization that believes in empowering the people of greater Nashville and the world by helping build homes that will be a foundation for a family to build their futures.

name: Philippine Intercultural Student Organization
description: PISO serves Vanderbilt University and the greater Nashville community by engaging in a multifaceted approach in empowering students and community members — both of Filipino/Filipinx descent or with earnest interest in Filipino/Filipinx culture. PISO realizes this mission by creating a culturally collaborative environment, fostering cross-cultural metacognition, emphasizing diversity and inclusivity, and reflecting on the history of the Philippines. PISO, moreover, provides productive resources and opportunities that aim to accelerate the success of its members and the representation of the larger Filipino/Filipinx community in society.
summary: PISO serves Vanderbilt University and the greater Nashville community by engaging in a multifaceted approach in empowering students and community members — both of Filipino descent or with earnest interest in Filipino culture.

name: Students Transitioning Relaxed and Natural Developing Sisterhood
description: The purpose of STRANDS is to give Black women on Vanderbilt's campus a voice. STRANDS is more than just a group of women meeting to discuss the latest trends in hair care, we teach the importance of a healthy mind, body, and soul. Our purpose is to provide Vanderbilt students the opportunity to celebrate black womanhood and discuss issues directly relevant to young black women today. We want to promote awareness of the diversity of the black female experience as well as develop a sense of community amongst black women in Nashville. A major part of our organization is to provide mentorship to young black women (undergraduates and local teens) in Nashville in order to help build them up as individuals as well as creating a network which local black women can depend upon.
summary: STRANDS is an organization dedicated to uplifting and empowering of Black women on Vanderbilt's campus.

name: Vanderbilt College Democrats
description: College Democrats is a student organization dedicated to establishing a progressive voice on campus and increasing civic engagement among students at Vanderbilt. We seek to promote the Democratic party platform and assist local and national politicians aligned with Democratic principles. We also aim to provide professional development and social connection to our members.
summary: College Democrats is a student group dedicated to establishing a liberal voice on campus and increasing student involvement in politics. We seek to promote the Democratic party platform and assist local and national politicians aligned with our ideology.

name: Eta Sigma Phi: Psi Chapter
description: The purposes of the Society, in the words of the Constitution, are "to develop and promote interest in classical study among the students of colleges and universities; to promote closer fraternal relationship among students who are interested in classical study, including inter-campus relationship; to engage generally in an effort to stimulate interest in classical study, and in the history, art, and literature of ancient Greece and Rome."
summary: The Psi Chapter of Eta Sigma Phi is Vanderbilt University's honorary collegiate society for students of Latin and/or Greek. A student must meet the qualification of an attained grade of not less than “B+” in at least 3 courses in Latin and/or Greek.

name: Camp Kesem at Vanderbilt
description:
Our mission is two-fold:

  • To provide children affected by a  parent's cancer with a supportive, lifelong camp community that recognizes and understands their unique needs
  • To empower college students to make a difference and build invaluable leadership skills by developing and managing every aspect of their Camp Kesem chapter
summary: Camp Kesem provides year-round support for kids through and beyond their parents' cancer.  Camp Kesem is our week-long student run summer camp is a chance for children ages 6-18 to let loose and to connect with other kids who share their experience.

name: Biomedical Engineering Graduate Student Association
description:
The Biomedical Engineering Graduate Student Association (BME GSA) shall:

  1. Serve as a forum for the discussion of matters pertaining to the welfare of biomedical engineering graduate students at Vanderbilt
  2. Serve as a liaison between BME graduate students, BME faculty and staff, the Graduate Student Council (GSC), and the University.
  3. Contribute to the recognition and integration of biomedical engineering graduate students at the University
  4. Select biomedical engineering graduate students to serve on committees for which graduate representation is necessary
  5. Foster unity among biomedical engineering graduate students at Vanderbilt by sponsoring social activities and other events
  6. Strive to continuously improve the standards of the academic experience of Vanderbilt's biomedical engineering graduate students
  7. BMA GSA understands and is committed to fulfilling its responsibilities of abiding by Vanderbilt University policies.
summary: BME GSA serves to foster unity among biomedical engineering graduate students, faculty, and undergraduate students.

name: Alpha Kappa Psi, Omega Nu Chapter
description:
​Founded in 2006, the Omega Nu chapter of AKPsi is the oldest and largest professional co-ed business fraternity at Vanderbilt University. Comprised of a diverse group of individuals, professionals, and campus leaders, we are dedicated to fostering a supportive, professional, and encouraging environment for young business leaders on our campus.

 

Our membership is defined by students with diverse backgrounds, professional interests, disciplines, and networks. Our brothers hold leadership positions in other selective organizations, have created successful campus startups, go on to work at top companies, and are united by their high level of motivation and shared passion for success in business.

 

We are in active cooperation with employers, the Vanderbilt administration, and faculty in order to provide our brothers with the resources necessary to succeed. As a brotherhood, we look for new members that share our goal for excellence and contribute to our community.
summary: AKPsi is Vanderbilt's oldest and largest professional co-ed business fraternity. Comprised of a diverse group of students and professionals, we are dedicated to fostering a supportive, professional environment for young business leaders on campus.

name: Owen Venture and Entrepreneurship Club
description:
The OVEC welcomes all future funders and founders, as well as anyone with an entrepreneurial soul. The purpose of our club is to give students exposure to all things entrepreneurial, including fundraising. We not only support those who are starting their own businesses, want to work at startups, or hope to be venture capitalists in the future, but also anyone who wants to explore what it means to think as an entrepreneur even at a large company. In collaboration with the Center for Entrepreneurship, we foster relationships between students and the Nashville community, and offer networking opportunities with both founders and VCs in the area.  

Our hope is that all students feel they have the resources and support to think creatively and innovate, no matter what their career aspirations are.  

In order to achieve this mission, we operate with the following club values:

  • We believe that no idea is too big, too small, or too crazy. This club is a safe space for dreamers of all kinds. 
  • We believe that Entrepreneurship is a valuable, respected, and challenging MBA career path.
  • We believe that you do not need to have your million dollar idea today to be an entrepreneur, and that entrepreneurship looks very different from one person to another.  
  • We believe in giving back to the community, and getting involved in service opportunities for future entrepreneurs. 
  • We believe in collaboration with peers, professors, and other people in the Owen family. Nothing gets done in a silo. 
  • We believe in entrepreneurial education. You're born an entrepreneur, but we can help guide you towards being a highly successful one.
summary: The Owen VEC promotes and supports entrepreneurial and venture capital activities within the Owen Graduate School of Management, at Vanderbilt University, and in the Nashville Community as a whole.

name: Women's Club Soccer
description:
If you are interested in trying out, please fill out this Google Form: 

https://docs.google.com/forms/d/1QC4teqxepVwuXtXUskMJQoSNdVTAT7z2s7iMUaDby18/edit

Tryouts will be at the beginning of the semester, so make sure you bring your soccer stuff to college with you! If you happen to miss tryouts, feel free to contact any of exec and we can make arrangements.
summary: The Vanderbilt Women's Club Soccer team is a great opportunity for Vanderbilt women who desire to continue playing soccer at a competitive level. The team has both a fall and spring season with weekly practices, and games and tournaments held on weekends

name: Eastern European and Central Asian Club
description:
What is ECC?

The Vanderbilt Eastern European and Central Asian Club exists to celebrate diverse cultures from regions spanning half the globe. It serves as a necessary outlet for students from Eastern Europe and Central Asia to express their distinct cultures and traditions, all while serving as a space for students to learn more about the region and its people, right on Vanderbilt’s campus. 

 

As the region reels from various conflicts — even amongst regional neighbors — the Vanderbilt Eastern European and Central Asian Club strives to be a forum for productive conversations surrounding such conflicts. We work to serve as a critical bridge that forges unity between community members whose governments may be at odds with one another.

What is the geography of the region?

Eastern Europe and Central Asia include multiple regions with shared cultural, historical, and geographical elements.

Post-Soviet States: Belarus, Moldova, Russia, Ukraine

Southeastern and Central Europe: Albania, Bosnia and Herzegovina, Bulgaria, Cyprus, Croatia, Czech Republic, Hungary, Kosovo, Montenegro, North Macedonia, Poland, Romania, Serbia, Slovakia, Slovenia

Baltic States: Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania

Caucasus states: Armenia, Azerbaijan, Georgia

Central Asia: Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, Turkmenistan, Uzbekistan
summary: The Eastern European and Central Asian Club exists to celebrate diverse cultures from regions that span half the globe. It serves as an outlet for students from Eastern Europe and Central Asia to express and educate on their distinct cultures/traditions.

name: VMS Dance
description: This organization is only open to current VUSM MD students. VMS dance provides a creative space for students to express themselves through the performing arts. This student-organized group welcomes all dance forms ranging from classical to modern to cultural. Each work pulls from individual talents and experiences easily found within Vanderbilt School of Medicine’s diverse student body. Toward this goal, the club recruits student instructors and choreographers to offer free dance classes. Classes in a variety of styles will be offered on a regular basis to allow members to experience different dance forms and maintain wellness. When possible, the club will offer specialty and cultural dance classes taught by professional dance instructors from local studios. The club dances have been featured in the Cadaver Ball. Upcoming performances are pending. VMS Dance also performs and organize workshops at local clinics and schools to offer performances for patients and underserved communities.
summary: VMS Dance promotes wellness, creative expression, and community service. It is an organization composed of medical students and faculty. The club organizes performances and classes for the student body as well as the community.

name: Zeta Tau Alpha
description:
Founded in 1898 at Longwood University, Zeta Tau Alpha brings a rich tradition of sisterhood and community involvement to Vanderbilt University. The goal of Zeta Tau Alpha is to intensify friendships, participate in community and national philanthropic events, and to build a nobler and purer womanhood in the world. Zeta Tau Alpha exists to make a difference in the lives of its members by developing the potential of each individual through innovative programming. Our organization emphasizes leadership development, service to others, academic achievement, and continued personal growth for women. With a commitment to friendship and the future based on the sisterhood, values and traditions of the past, Zeta Tau Alpha welcomes women wanting to perpetuate the ties of friendship and grow in serving others.

 

Our national philanthropy is breast cancer education and awareness. Zeta Tau Alpha has partnerships with the NFL, Bright Pink and American Cancer Society to promote education for the fight against breast cancer. In the act of serving others, sisters work with Sisters By Choice to provide underrepresented communities with diagnostic services, attend Tennessee Titans home games to pass out shower cards and information, and attend Race for the Cure to support and greet Survivors. With notable alumna such as Erin Andrews, Zeta Tau Alpha truly lives by its motto, “Seek the noblest.”

 

Zetas at Vanderbilt are active in other organizations such as Cheerleading, Dance, Field Hockey, Camp Kesem, VUDM, The Hustler, Vida, VUcept, VIVA and many, many more organizations. Zeta provides women with the opportunity to enrich and ennoble their lives through service and social activities.
summary: The Lambda Iota Chapter of Zeta Tau Alpha offers sisterhood, service, and leadership opportunities to our outstanding members. ZTA is the third largest Panhellenic sorority in the nation! Check out our Instagram page @vandyzeta to learn more!

name: Methodist Student Association
description:
The Methodist Student Association of the Vanderbilt Divinity School exists to provide opportunities for all interested students with an emphasis on the theologies and narratives born out of the Methodist and Wesleyan traditions. We work to facilitate interaction, provide avenues for mutual support and spiritual formation, connect to the structures and organizations of Methodist and Wesleyan Churches, promote social activism and justice in the Vanderbilt and Nashville communities, and coordinate events for fellowship and fun. While membership is designated specifically for students of the Vanderbilt Divinity School and Graduate Department of Religion, participation in MSA events, trainings, and activities are open to any and all interested students within the Vanderbilt community at large.

 

 All are welcome here, regardless of faith tradition, affiliation, or lack thereof.

 

The MSA is committed as an affirming and reconciling organization, to maintaining boundaries to promote the growth, formation, wellbeing, and physical safety of its affiliates and members.
summary: The MSA of the Divinity School seeks to provide community and programming for all interested students, regardless of faith, affiliation, or lack thereof, with an emphasis on the theologies and narratives born out of the Methodist and Wesleyan traditions.

name: Vanderbilt University Marching Band
description:
The Vanderbilt University Band program is one of the largest and most diverse student activities at Vanderbilt University. The University Band program includes five performing ensembles with members hailing from all undergraduate and graduate academic schools at Vanderbilt.

Our premier ensemble, the "Spirit of Gold," is a performing unit adding music and pageantry to the excitement of Commodore Football.

  • MEMBERSHIP is open to all Vanderbilt students as well as students from other Nashville-area colleges, including Belmont, Lipscomb, Trevecca, and Fisk Universities.
  • AUDITIONS are held at BAND CAMP for all sections.
  • SCHOLARSHIPS are awarded to all "Spirit of Gold" members who successfully complete the season.
  • COST: There are no required costs for uniforms, instruments, music or travel. "Spirit of Gold" Members are responsible for providing their own black MARCHING shoes.
  • REHEARSALS are Tuesdays and Thursdays 4:30-6:30 p.m. and Fridays 5:30-7:30 p.m-- No Extra Rehearsals!

A 50-member Basketball Band helps create the legendary "Memorial Magic" in Memorial Gym for both Men's and Women's Basketball games. Membership in the "Spirit of Gold" Marching Band is prerequisite to play in the Basketball Band and students receive additional scholarships for participation.

Vanderbilt University Bands are in the center of campus life....concerts, social gatherings, university activities, & athletic events! Whether it is the Marching Band performing during Halftime at a Commodore Football Game; the Basketball Band performing from the floor of Memorial Gymnasium; the Symphonic Wind Ensemble presenting a concert of Feature works; the Jazz band swinging on the Riverboat for Big band Ball; or the Community Band's Concert in the Park: The Vanderbilt University Band program offers a wealth of opportunities both to the performer and the audience.
summary: The Spirit of Gold a Marching Band that performs at all home football games, most home basketball games, and select away events. Members receive a scholarship at the end of the season and membership is open to all Vanderbilt students.

name: Student Executive Council (McGill)
description: The McGill Project is a Living & Learning community dedicated to free thought, creative expression, and inclusive community. Project members consider themselves part of an ongoing tradition that encompasses a number of innovative and diverse programs and activities. With over 100 residents, McGill is a place where students are pushed to express themselves culturally, socially, politically, and intellectually.
summary: The McGill Project is a Living & Learning community dedicated to free thought, creative expression, and inclusive community.

name: Vanderbilt Women's Club Volleyball
description:
2025-2026 VANDERBILT CLUB VOLLEYBALL

The women's club volleyball team is interested in students who want to continue their love of the sport in a competitive and fun environment. The team hosts a 12-team tournament each semester and also travels to regional tournaments.  

 

For more information contact:

  • Ellie Cox - Co-President - ellie.c.cox@vanderbilt.edu
  • Izzy Talopau - Co-President - isabella.j.talopau@vanderbilt.edu
summary: The women's club volleyball team is interested in students who want to continue their love of the sport in a competitive and fun environment. Please see try out and practice information on our Anchor Link profile or Instagram.

name: Vanderbilt Undergraduate Research Journal
description: The purpose of the VURJ is to showcase the excellent research and investigative work done by Vanderbilt undergraduate students. The primary objective of the journal is to publish original studies from across all disciplines (natural sciences, social sciences, political science and economics, education and human development, humanities, engineering, and mathematics and physics, but the VURJ will also publish literature reviews and other forms of rigorous research as well. Check out our website for the latest issue at vurj.vanderbilt.edu.
summary: The Vanderbilt Undergraduate Research Journal welcomes submissions from all four undergraduate schools. VURJ conforms to the highest standards of ethics and integrity in the review process to bring forth quality, well-developed thought to the forefront.

name: Commencement
summary: Commencement managed by University Signature Events team

name: Phi Mu Alpha Sinfonia - Pi Delta Chapter
description: The Object of this Fraternity shall be for the development of the best and truest fraternal spirit; the mutual welfare and brotherhood of musical students; the advancement of music in America and a loyalty to the Alma Mater.
summary: The Object of this Fraternity shall be for the development of the best and truest fraternal spirit; the mutual welfare and brotherhood of musical students; the advancement of music in America and a loyalty to the Alma Mater.

name: Students Consulting for Nonprofit Organizations
description:
Vanderbilt Students Consulting for Nonprofit Organizations (SCNO) is Vanderbilt's student-led nonprofit consulting group. We're a service organization that works on challenging problems with Nashville-based nonprofits and social enterprises each year to help them achieve their missions and goals. Since 2013, we've served over 40 organizations in our community while providing Vandy students the opportunity to gain experience in the social impact space and develop invaluable professional skills.

 

Our projects have included, but are not limited to, market mapping and industry research, organizational restructuring, inventory optimization, marketing strategies, and fundraising model development. As an organization, we value the multidimensionality of diversity, which we recognize as integral to the success of our project teams and to our own student community. Members of SCNO come from a variety of backgrounds and years (first-years to seniors), from political science to biochemistry and computer science to language studies.

 

Vanderbilt SCNO alumni continue to drive impact at a variety of organizations, including nonprofits, socially-focused start-ups, management consulting firms, and industry-specific companies. Please visit our website at www.vanderbiltSCNO.com to learn more about our organization.
summary: Vanderbilt SCNO coordinates project teams of Vanderbilt students to jumpstart social impact through pro-bono consulting engagements with nonprofit and social enterprise clients in the Nashville community.

name: Dores Design
description:
Dores Design is a free graphic design service for students, organizations, faculty, and departments marketing on Vanderbilt's campus. We help you create posters, fliers, social media materials, stickers, t-shirts and more! Please fill out the project request form if you would like a design made. Please request your design three to five weeks prior to your event. If you are requesting a design that needs printing, please request five weeks in advance.

Please follow us on Instagram (@doresdesignteam) to check out our designers' amazing work!
summary: Dores Design is a FREE design service for all students and student organizations at Vanderbilt University. We help your marketing pop by creating logos, posters, flyers, stickers, t-shirt designs, and more! Fill out a request form to work with us today.

name: Resident Advisers
description: The Resident Advisor (RA) is a paraprofessional student staff member for the Office of Housing and Residential Experience. The RA assumes responsibility for fostering an environment that supports the academic and personal development of students. The RA works closely with the Area Coordinator (AC) and Head Residents (HR) in order to maintain a level of consistency between the area and department of Residential Experience in regards to community standards. The position requires dedication, flexibility, commitment and enthusiasm as the staff will actively respond to the changing needs of the residential community.
summary:
A Resource Page for Resident Advisors
Click here to file an incident report

name: Blair School of Music
description: Blair School of Music serves as the focal point within Vanderbilt University for the study of music as a human endeavor and as a performing art. Music offers to all persons a medium for the expression of the human spirit. Accordingly, the Blair School of Music addresses music through a broad array of academic, pedagogical, and performing activities. Consistent with the mission of Vanderbilt University, the school maintains and promotes the highest standards in the pursuit of scholarly and creative work, in the delivery of instruction, and in the promotion of professional and public service.
summary: The Blair School of Music serves as the focal point within Vanderbilt University for the study of music as a human endeavor and as a performing art.

name: Special Event Registration Committee
description: The Special Event Registration process is designed to support outdoor events, large-scale events, and events with alcohol. The Special Event Registration Committee is charged with approving such events on campus. The committee consists of representative(s) from Reservations and Events, Traffic and Parking, Vanderbilt University Police Department, Plant Operations, Health and Wellness, Risk Management and a designee of the Dean of Students. The committee meets Tuesdays, Sarratt 208, 2-3pm.
summary: Special Event Registration Committee approval.

name: The Mayfield Experience
description:
The Mayfield Experience: Ten students, one lodge, one project!  The Mayfield Experience seeks to empower students with a platform for pursuing their service and learning interests.  The Mayfield’s provide students an opportunity to create a self-generated, self-directed group project with the guidance of faculty and staff advisers.  To apply, groups must create and present a detailed proposal of their project’s purpose, goals, and timeline.  Following a panel style interview process, groups with well-formed and well-articulated projects may be admitted into the community.  Upon entry into the Mayfield Experience, program requirements include monthly reports, weekly meetings, lectures with faculty and staff, regular meetings with faculty advisors and participation in educational programs for the campus and community at large.

The Mayfield Place is the physical site for the Mayfield Experience. It sits on the southern fringe of the Highland Quadrangle community. The semi-circle shaped complex is located near the Student Recreation Center and most althletic facilites.

The Mayfield Place offers a unique living arrangement designed for 200 undergraduates living in 20 lodges with 10 residents each. Each two-story lodge has 10 single bedrooms, a common area, two baths, a microwave, a 21 inch stove, a sink, and a full-size refrigerator. All are fully carpeted and air-conditioned. There are five bedrooms and one bath on each floor, and the floors are connected by a wooden staircase. The living room area is 12′ x 16′ and the kitchen is 4′ x 16′. The dimensions of the bedrooms are 8′ x 12′.

Mayfield Place was built in 1977 and named for Dr. George Mayfield.
summary: The Mayfield Experience seeks to empower students with a platform for pursuing their service and learning interests. Students are afforded an opportunity to create a self-directed group project with the guidance of faculty and staff advisers.

name: Residential Experience Programming
description:
Residential Experience strives to create a community environment in which students can participate in a wide variety of educational activities and events.  We offer a diverse group of programs that will enrich our residents and compliment their academic experience.  Our programs are designed to help students:

 Find their place and be exposed to a culturally diverse community

  • Develop life skills
  • Enhance their understanding and appreciation of the arts
  • Develop socially, academically, intellectually, vocationally, physically, emotionally, spiritually, and civically

 At Vanderbilt, education is not just about the classroom experience; it is also about the learning that happens outside the classroom.  We challenge the students to take part in their community by building new friendships and managing those relationships, interacting with faculty and staff, and exposing themselves to new and different perspectives.  Our programs are designed to emphasize the four major areas of acculturation at Vanderbilt: Academic, Social, Values, and Cognitive.  We believe these areas encompass the learning outcomes needed for our students to be highly successful, global citizens.

  

Our Programs:

Commodore Cruises

Commodore Cruises are opportunities for Vanderbilt students to experience Nashville with dinner at a Nashville-centric restaurant followed by an enjoyable night beyond Vanderbilt’s campus. The events range from seeing a show at the Schermerhorn Symphony Center, to a musical at the Tennessee Performing Arts Center, to a game the Bridgestone Arena.  The biggest highlight of the cruises is that they are all FREE to the students!

 Mega Stress Fest

Mega Stress Fest is our yearly stress-reliever for the student body before fall finals.  It’s a great opportunity to take a study break and enjoy a festive yet calming atmosphere filled with free food, fun and relaxation!!!  Students have the opportunity to sign up for free massages, paraffin hand wax treatments, facials, henna art tattoos, Wii games, arts and crafts and more! Students also get an array of tasty treats and warm drink options.  Traditionally held the first Friday in December in the Student Life Center, this program is co-sponsored with VPB’s VenUe.
summary: Residential Experience strives to create a community environment in which students can participate in a wide variety of educational activities and events.  We offer a diverse group of programs including Commodore Cruises & Mega Stress Fest!

organizationType.name: Student Affairs Department, Office, or Program

name: Student Organizations, Leadership, and Service
description:
Student Organizations, Leadership, and Service strives to encourage co-curricular learning and personal development through intentional student engagement. Student Organizations and Anchor Link connects the campus community by highlighting opportunities for meaningful involvement and providing tools to help manage and track the co-curricular student experience. We support the mission-driven work of student organizations and seek to equip student leaders with vital skills and resources in order to make their organizations more effective, inclusive, impactful, and sustainable.  

In order to support and centralize student engagement across campus, our office will:

  • Oversee the registration process for Vanderbilt’s 500+ registered student organizations
  • Assist student organizations with accessing and managing their finances
  • Support the Activity Fee (AcFee) allocation process for student organizations
  • Educate student leaders about Vanderbilt University policies and support compliance efforts
  • Provide online resources to student organization leaders and advisors
  • Offer opportunities for training and leadership development through the START student leader workshops
  • Directly advise Vanderbilt Student Government and the Black Student Association,
  • Provide advisory support to various student organizations
  • Conduct campus-wide elections such as Vanderbilt Student Government, Outstanding Senior, and Young Alumni Leader

Visit the Anchor Link resources website for
summary: Student Organizations, Leadership, and Service (SOLS) connects the campus community by highlighting opportunities for meaningful involvement.

organizationType.name: Student Affairs Department, Office, or Program

name: Design For America - Vanderbilt
description: Design For America is a network of student-led organizations seeking to create social change through interdisciplinary design. To affect social change, the organizations seek to understand the challenges of our communities, create solutions based on human-centered insights, and then partner with organizations to execute these ideas. Design for America believes in the power of students and the local community to affect real change in our world using their boundless energy, social intelligence, and fresh perspectives.
summary: Design For America is a network of student-led organizations seeking to create social change through interdisciplinary design.

name: Shade Tree Clinic Fundraising Committee
description:
The Shade Tree Fundraising Committee is a Vanderbilt Medical School student organization responsible for planning and promoting the annual events that fundraise and raise awareness for the Shade Tree Clinic. The Shade Tree Clinic is our student-run, non-profit clinic that serves the community by providing free medical care to underserved and uninsured Nashville citizens. These fundraising efforts serve as the primary sources of income that comprise the clinic’s annual budget. This committee is composed of Vanderbilt Medical School students and Vanderbilt Nursing students, and is advised by VUSM faculty. This year the committee will work to build upon the success of previous Shade Tree fundraising events (including the Trot and the Benefit), which annually raise over $200,000 for the clinic.

This organization is only open to current VUSM MD students.
summary: The STCFC is a Vanderbilt medical school organization that organizes the fundraising events for Shade Tree Clinic, our student run clinic. This includes planning and orchestrating the annual Benefit Dinner and Shade Tree Trot 5K race.

name: Vanderbilt Campus Dining
description:
Mission
 
Vanderbilt Campus Dining has a passion for providing excellent food at the highest levels of quality, value, convenience, and service. We are builders of community and do so in an innovative, creative, proactive, supportive, welcoming and financially self-supporting way.
 
Company Overview

We take pride in our commitment to providing value through the highest quality, best-tasting, most nutritious and diverse menus possible. We embrace our role as builders of community. Food brings people together, creates a sense of place and a feeling of kinship. Food sustains not only our bodies, but also our communities and environment. Vanderbilt Campus Dining is highly decorated, winner of many prestigious awards in the food service industry, including Best On-Site Food Service Operation in the Nation, Best Food Service Operator, the prestigous Ivy Award, and many more. Our team also consists of several award-winning, certified chefs. 

Our Meal Plans are designed specifically for the Vanderbilt student.
summary:
Vanderbilt Campus Dining is a non-profit, award winning service at Vanderbilt University.

description:
Helping Hands for Refugees is a student-run service organization advised by the Office of Active Citizenship and Service.

 

We strive to improve the well-being of refugees and immigrants through a framework of community connectedness by:

 

1.  Coordinating the matching of students with community agencies, refugee families, and individuals.

name: Transfer Student Orientation
description:
We are very pleased to have the opportunity to extend a warm welcome to
you as you begin your journey at such a dynamic and vibrant university!
You have chosen a wonderful institution to call home for the upcoming
years and we hope you are excited as we are for your arrival come
August. Whether it is musing with Vanderbilt Off Broadway, our premier
Broadway performing group, or writing for one of our seven publications
on campus, opportunities at Vanderbilt are endless as there are more
than 300 clubs and organizations with which you can get involved! We are
excited to include you in the many traditions Vanderbilt offers its students.


From Founders Walk to Rites of Spring, Vanderbilt strives to incorporate
a balance between academically rigorous classes and socially enriching
experiences. Undergraduates at Vanderbilt are ambitious, engaged,
passionate students committed to both scholarship and social exchange. We
couldn’t be more excited than for you to become a part of this unique community!
Again welcome; we cannot wait to meet you this fall

shortName: MTNA/TMTA/NAMTA Collegiate Blair Chapter at Vanderbilt
description:
MTNA collegiate chapters work to enhance students' classroom studies by providing educational, musical, social and professional experiences. The goal is to acquaint students with professional opportunities and options while developing professional leadership skills. The organization should be actively involved in campus, community and national arenas.

 

To acquire a collegiate membership, follow this link! https://www.mtna.org/MTNA/JoinAndRenew/Membership/Collegiate_Membership.aspx

name: Vanderbilt Brewing and Fermentation Science Club
description:
Vanderbilt Brewing and Fermentation Science Club is a student-run organization that promotes appreciation for the science, art, culture, and history of fermented products through meetings, events, tastings, and brewing. Past events have included beer and kombucha brewing, pickling (pickles, kimchi, etc.), hot sauce fermentation, sourdough baking, and cheese production. 

This organization is only open to current VUSM MD Students.
summary: Vanderbilt Brewing and Fermentation Science Club is student run organization that promotes appreciation for the science, art, culture, and history of beer and other fermented products through meetings, events, tastings, and brewing.

name: Engineers Without Borders
description:
Established in 2007, EWB-VU is an organization that performs service with an engineering focus and currently has 30-50 active student members. The club meets weekly and hosts events that are tailored to engineering students, but are open to all. The organization focuses on assessing and implementing engineering projects in developing communities in other countries, and, each year, 4-6 students and 2 professional engineers travel abroad to work on these projects.

Our past international project was building a latrine in a village in Llanchama, Peru. We are currently finishing up international projects in Paxoj and Txemuj, Guatemala. In the past we developed a septic system for an elementary school, and we are now finishing up drainage systems and retaining walls to support their school. EWB-VU is also working in Sabana Larga, Nicaragua to provide a village of ~200 with a fully functioning water system, source to tap. Along with working on international projects, EWB volunteers in the Nashville community and collaborates on projects with service organizations . More recently, EWB-VU has worked with the Susan Gray School near Vanderbilt on designing a green roof on their campus and constructing planting tables; now, the team has transitioned to designing a sensor network to help us make informed decisions in managing campus' stormwaters.
summary: EWB-Vanderbilt supports community-driven development programs worldwide by collaborating with local partners to design and implement sustainable engineering projects, while creating transformative experiences and responsible leaders.

name: Youth Encouragement Services  - Vanderbilt
description:
Y.E.S. - Vanderbilt serves to promote and further the goals of the Nashville organization, Youth Encouragement Services on Vanderbilt's campus. Y.E.S. - Vanderbilt is a student run organization that provides volunteers to two Y.E.S. centers in Nashville. These centers strive "to enrich the lives of children in inner city Nashville by encouraging them to reach their full potential. Through daily programs such as tutoring, summer camp, a basketball league, job skills training, and other recreational and cultural activities, low-income children are given a positive alternative to the streets and opportunities for a bright future.”

 

Y.E.S. - Vanderbilt will further the organization’s mission by providing dedicated, dependable volunteers to mentor underprivileged inner city youth.  Y.E.S. Vanderbilt will assign individual members to a day of the week (Monday through Friday) on which he or she will volunteer as a part of a group of 2-4 students. Y.E.S. - Vanderbilt currently serves the Lindsey Center. Furthermore, Y.E.S. Vanderbilt exists to build the relationship between the children at Y.E.S and the Vanderbilt community by hosting events on campus or at various locations in Nashville throughout the school year.
summary: Y.E.S. Vanderbilt serves to promote and further the goals of the Nashville organization, Youth Encouragement Services, on Vanderbilt's campus. Y.E.S. Vanderbilt will further the organization’s mission by providing dedicated, dependable volunteers.

name: The Vanderbilt Hustler/vanderbilthustler.com
description: The Vanderbilt Hustler is Vanderbilt's official student newspaper, serving the Vanderbilt community with daily publications on vanderbilthustler.com about news and events affecting the Vanderbilt community. Hustler content is produced by undergraduate students. The Hustler is a division of Vanderbilt Student Communications (VSC).
summary: Vanderbilt's official student newspaper and online media outlet. The Vanderbilt Hustler publishes daily during the school year on vanderbilthustler.com.

name: The Vanderbilt Review Literary Journal
description:
The Vanderbilt Review is Vanderbilt’s official undergraduate literary and arts journal. The Review includes refereed work in fiction, poetry, drama, art, and photography from students, alumni, and University affiliates.

The Vanderbilt Review was first published in April 1985 as a consolidation of three previous showcases for student work: The Poetry Review, The Photography Review, and Scrivener. The journal has received prestigious awards for its professional work and its format. In past years, it has featured interviews and work from writers such as Robert Penn Warren, Roy Blount, Jr., and Bobbie Ann Mason.

The Vanderbilt Review is a digital publication. We're excited about showcasing the creative expression of poets, writers, and artists in new ways and expanding access to The Review to alumni and appreciators across the country!

Roughly 25 students make up The Review‘s editorial board. More than 100 students, alumni, and University affiliates submit work for consideration of publication. The Review solicits assistance from professors in the English and Art Departments as judges for its annual awards.  The Vanderbilt Review staff works in the Student Media Newsroom in Suite 130 of Sarratt Student Center.
summary: Vanderbilt's official undergraduate-run literary and arts journal, published in print and online, and featuring poetry, fiction, creative nonfiction, and visual art.

summary: Student/Staff committee for emergency relief.

name: Vanderbilt University Motorsports
description:
Vanderbilt University Motorsports participates in the Formula SAE collegiate design competition. This is an international engineering competition sponsored by the Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE) that presents students with the challenge of designing and building a small, open-wheel race car. The prototyped car is evaluated for its feasibility to be sold as a weekend autocross vehicle. During the school year, students design and fabricate their car before bringing it to compete against other universities from around the world. At competition, students are judged in the areas of design, start-up cost, business case, and racing performance. In competition, adeptness in the areas of design, fabrication, teamwork, leadership, and marketing are vital to a team’s success. Ultimately, participation in Formula SAE gives students an edge upon graduating by exposing them to these skills that are unattainable in the classroom. The program has gained significant reputation, and many employers focus primarily on recruiting SAE competition graduates for both business and technical internships.

 

Our shop is located in Featheringill Hall 131. Shop hours are about 1-8:00 Monday-Thursday and 1-5:00 on Friday.
summary: Vanderbilt University Motorsports participates in the international Formula SAE collegiate design competition organized by the Society of Automotive Engineers in which students design, fabricate, test, and market a Formula type race car.

name: Tau Beta Pi
description:
Tau Beta Pi is universally recognized as the premier engineering honor society.  It is the oldest, largest, and most prestigious, and it brings together all engineering fields.

*Please Note: Membership in Tau Beta Pi is via invite only during your junior or senior year of enrollment in the Vanderbilt School of Engineering based on your academic standing within VUSE. If you are eligible for membership in the Tennessee Beta chapter of TBP, you will be contacted by a TBP officer who will inform you on how to proceed with the membership process.

The Tennessee Beta chapter of Tau Beta Pi at Vanderbilt University provides the opportunity for the top students in engineering to collaborate, socialize, serve the community, discuss problems, and form friendships. 

Within the busy schedules of engineering students at Vanderbilt, it can be difficult to find other students with your same drive to succeed, interests in engineering, past collegiate success, and moral integrity. Tau Beta Pi provides an avenue through which these top students can collaborate and work together, while also integrating them into the national Tau Beta Pi network so as to aid in career advancement through interactions with alumni.  Tau Beta Pi is a vast national honor society, providing opportunities for networking with many famous and successful alumni.

It is not uncommon in engineering job interviews for the interviewer to ask whether you are in Tau Beta Pi, often because the interviewer himself/herself is an alumni. Your membership can create instant connections that can smooth your interviewing and job seeking processes. 

Tau Beta Pi is the only engineering honor society representing the entire engineering profession. It is the nation's second-oldest honor society, founded at Lehigh University in 1885 both to mark those who have conferred honor upon their Alma Mater by distinguished scholarship and exemplary character as students in engineering or by their achievements as alumni in the field of engineering, and to foster a spirit of liberal culture in engineering colleges. 
There are now collegiate chapters at 252 US colleges and universities (of which 245 are active), 42 active alumnus chapters in 16 districts across the country, and a total initiated membership of 564,000.

For more information, visit us at tbp.vanderbilt.edu
summary:
Integrity and Excellence in Engineering 
Tau Beta Pi is universally recognized as the premier engineering honor society.  It is the oldest, largest, and most prestigious, and it brings together all engineering disciplines.

name: Vanderbilt AIAA Student Chapter
description:
The Vanderbilt AIAA student chapter is a volunteer student organization that
promotes aerospace engineering activities at Vanderbilt. It also conducts aerospace
educational outreach to Middle Tennessee middle and high schools. The Aerospace
activities apply directly to the profession of Aerospace Engineering (AE) and its
practice. It is most relevant to upper-class undergraduate and graduate engineering
students with an aptitude for AE and to upper-class undergraduate students from
A&S and Education who may be interested in school science teaching. AIAA
members interested in pursuing an aerospace engineering career actively participate in the NASA Student Launch Competition, through the engineering schools’ senior design project, which is sponsored by the Vanderbilt Aerospace Design Laboratory (www.vanderbilt.edu/usli ).


Constitutional Articles:


1. The AIAA must have a mechanical engineering professor who serves as the
faculty advisor. In addition, the organisation must also have a financial advisor
pursuant to Vanderbilt policy.


2. AIAA membership can have both undergraduate and graduate students
interested in aerospace engineering. The President, Vice President, Secretary,
and Treasurer of the AIAA must be recommended by the AIAA
faculty advisor.


3. AIAA members must actively seek to promote Aerospace and STEM
education and outreach in the Nashville Area.


4. The largest AIAA membership that can compete and be active in the NASA SL
competition is 15-18 members. Preference for Student Launch Competition
participation is given to VUSE Seniors with good academic standing, through the VUSE senior design program, and to graduate students with specific skillsets.

5. Students who are selected to compete in the NASA SL competition must demonstrate qualities that will contribute to success at the competition. Such qualities may include prior rocketry experience, interest in aerospace engineering or science teaching as a career, a good academic standing and a very strong work ethic in a field that is of use to the team (particularly educational outreach or engineering).

6. The NASA SL Competition also serves as the senior design project for the
VUSE Curriculum.


7. Any amendments to this Constitution can be made by a vote of the members
and approved by the faculty advisor.
summary: The Vanderbilt AIAA student chapter is a volunteer student organization that promotes aerospace engineering activities at Vanderbilt. It also conducts aerospace educational outreach to Middle Tennessee middle and high schools.

name: WRVU Nashville
description:
WRVU is a student-run, free-format college radio station focused on teaching students the ins and outs of radio broadcasting and providing Vanderbilt University and the surrounding community with the best in new, non-mainstream music. We currently stream online at wrvu.org, through our iPhone and Android apps, over iHeartRadio and Live 365, and on 90.3 FM HD-3.

Reach out to the Station Manager at wrvugm@gmail.com & the Training Director at wrvutd@gmail.com for info on joining!
summary: WRVU is a free-format college radio station committed to providing Vanderbilt University and the surrounding community with the best in new, non-mainstream music.

name: Susan Gray-Murray House Fellowship Program
description: The Susan Gray School-Murray House Fellowship Program pairs first-year residents of Murray House on Commons with both typically developing students and students with special needs at the Susan Gray School on campus. Past residents of Murray House continue to visit their buddies throughout their four years at Vanderbilt. Before going into the school, each student volunteer gets TN background checks in addition to completing the online minors modules. The program also hosts events throughout the year for holidays, along with a spring play day. Murray House Fellows volunteer at these special events in addition to visiting and playing with their preschool buddy each week.
summary: The Susan Gray-Murray House Fellowship Program is a volunteer program based out of Murray House on Commons. Past and current Murray House residents volunteer to be paired up with a preschool buddy from the Susan Gray School to visit each week.

Each year, people throughout Tennessee and the Southeast choose Vanderbilt for their health care needs, not only because of its excellence in medical science, but also because the faculty and staff are dedicated to treating patients with dignity and compassion. Vanderbilt's mission is to advance health and wellness through preeminent programs in patient care, education, and research.

name: College of Arts & Science Pre-Major Academic Advising  Resources Center
description:
Click Here to Schedule an Appointment

Director's Message

I and the other advisers at CASPAR are prepared and eager to provide advice, support, and perspective to all pre-major students in the College of Arts and Science as they make the transition from high school to Vanderbilt and then select a major. We will meet with these students on a regular basis in order to learn more about their specific goals, particular interests, and concrete plans for advancing those interests and meeting those goals. Our own goal is to be prompt in response to student queries, accurate in the answers we provide, open to exploring possibilities, and supportive of individual needs and plans. In the belief that we are helping to prepare self-actualized individuals, we will always encourage students to “think for themselves,” that is to take our and others’ advice and suggestions into account while arriving at their own conclusions and making their own decisions about being a successful student at Vanderbilt.

Student Responsibilities

We can help you best if you
- Arrive on time to your advising appointments
- Prepare for your appointments by consulting

  • your academic record to date
  • On the Road with AXLE
  • the Schedule of Courses
  • the Undergraduate Catalog and
  • the academic calendar for relevant deadlines

- Initiate and participate actively in conversations with your adviser
- Set academic/career goals and reassess them as you move through your undergraduate course of study
- Are open about your particular circumstances so that your adviser can provide specific guidance

Adviser Responsibilities

We help you to
- Select appropriate courses and develop a workable schedule
- Review course placement at Vanderbilt in light of your prior academic record and placement scores 
- Formulate an academic plan with respect to your interests, demonstrated strengths, and long-term goals
- Locate and understand AXLE requirements
- Interpret institutional policies and procedures
- Identify campus resources and use them effectively
- Nurture an active curiosity about the intellectual possibilities afforded by a research institution 
- Understand the structure of the university and its intellectual function

College of Arts and Science Pre-major Academic Advising Resources (CASPAR) Policies

Confidentiality – The members of the CASPAR office maintain the strictest confidentiality regarding student academic records and adhere to the guidelines of the Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA).

Cooperation – Advisers and the students whom they advise share responsibilities for making sure that students develop and follow a course of study that honors their abilities and interests, and that will help them meet their life goals. Advisers do not make decisions for students and will not tell them “what to do.” Advisers do, however, offer informed advice about academic decisions, big and small; provide relevant information as needed; and support students in the decisions they make.
summary: CASPAR is the College of Arts & Science Pre-Major Academic Advising  Resources Center that aids all pre-major students in the College of Arts & Science. Click "Read More" to learn more or to schedule an appointment.
