There are no tryouts to join the club.
summary: The Vanderbilt Club Golf program seeks to promote the game of golf by introducing it to beginners while giving experienced players the opportunity to compete on a local and national level.  We compete through the NCCGA and have weekly practices.

name: Vanderbilt Black Seminarians
description:
The purpose of Black Seminarians is to support Black students, faculty, and staff
within the Divinity School, the Graduate Department of Religion, and the Department of Religious Studies. We also intend to support and offer assistance to members of the
African Diaspora outside of Vanderbilt University.  

The philosophy of the Black Seminarians is to offer Black students the academic guidance, spiritual support, and social outlets necessary to excel and act as catalysts for social justice in a predominately White educational institution. We are committed to supporting Black faculty and staff in institutional concerns where the voices of African descent are non-existent or ignored.

Membership in Black Seminarians is open to any and all students, faculty, and staff at Vanderbilt University.
summary: The purpose of Black Seminarians shall be to support and offer assistance to all Black students, faculty, and staff within the Divinity School, the Graduate Department of Religion, the Department of Religious Studies.

name: VU SPIE Student Chapter
description: SPIE Student Chapters are intended to connect and engage students in more diverse career development opportunities. SPIE believes the future belongs to optics and photonics, and the future of these technologies is in the hands of our students. Student Chapters, therefore, have access to sponsored activities, reduced conferences registration fees, SPIE publications, travel grants, and the SPIE visiting lecture program to engage students and develop career opportunities.
summary: SPIE, the international society for optics and photonics, was founded in 1955 to advance light-based technologies through interdisciplinary information exchange, continuing education, publications, patent precedent, and career and professional growth.

name: Vanderbilt Awards For Leadership Excellence
description:
About the Vanderbilt Awards for Leadership Excellence

The mission of The Vanderbilt Awards for Leadership Excellence is to celebrate the work of student leaders and organizations that strengthens and fosters a diverse and transformative Vanderbilt community. These awards also recognize efforts that encourage members of this community to engage in a life-long pursuit of excellence in personal and social development. 

The individual awards honor students who have demonstrated leadership in areas ranging from academic achievement to community service efforts. The individual award winners will be notified prior to the Vanderbilt Awards for Leadership Excellence ceremony. All award recipients will be announced through the award ceremony program.

For the first time in 2016, these awards highlighted the work of student organizations on campus. We will continue to recognize these groups at Vanderbilt for the following: Best Service Program, Best Intercultural Awareness Program, Best Collaborative Program, Best Arts Program, Best Interfaith Program, Best Health & Wellness Program, and Best Community Development Program. These award winners will also be announced during the award ceremony.

The Vanderbilt Awards for Leadership Excellence Ceremony

Every spring, an award ceremony is held to celebrate award recipients and their work on behalf of the campus community. Currently this event is open to all Vanderbilt students, faculty, and staff members.  Whether you are an award recipient, a student organization member showing support, or just a spectator, we invite you to be a part of this memorable experience.
summary: The mission of The Vanderbilt Awards for Leadership Excellence (VALE) is to celebrate the work of student leaders and organizations that strengthens and fosters a diverse and transformative Vanderbilt community.

organizationType.name: Student Affairs Department, Office, or Program

name: Latinx Seminarians
description: The purpose of our group is to dialogue and advocate with Administration to recruit a plurality of Latinx identifying persons.  To create a community embodying the diverse Latinx cultures.  To engage and work with non-Latinx communities in Nashville.  To harness and uphold as valuable the identities of our varied embodied realities.
summary: To harness and encourage the plurality and diversity of Latinx identifying persons and their experiences.

name: Vanderbilt University Libraries
description:
Vanderbilt's Heard Libraries is a preeminent, academic research library system that acquires, preserves and makes accessible the world's information and engenders a community of collaboration, adaptability and innovation to address deep inquiry and challenges facing all of humanity. The Heard Libraries seek to create a welcoming, comfortable, and safe environment for our users. The library is a place for learning and reflection, and our staff supports these activities by providing helpful, responsive, and knowledgeable service.

 

Contact: Melissa Mallon
summary: The Jean and Alexander Heard Libraries will be a leader, partner and ally in propelling knowledge to address the challenges and opportunities of a rapidly changing world.

name: Vanderbilt Figure Skating Club
description:
Vanderbilt Figure Skating Club provides an opportunity for skaters of all levels from beginner to expert to learn, practice, and improve their figure skating skills in a fun and recreational environment. For beginners, we provide a chance to learn from figure skaters with skating expertise through professional coaching during our weekly Friday 3-4 pm practices. For experienced skaters, join our Intercollegiate Figure Skating Team to compete in Team Maneuvers, Free Skate, Free Dance, and Pattern Dance!

We practice weekly, and we host a skate night and exhibition each semester.

You will be added to our email list when you join.
summary: The Vanderbilt Figure Skating Club provides an opportunity for skaters of every level to learn, practice, and improve their skating skills in a recreational environment.

name: Gender and Sexuality Studies
description: Gender and Sexaulity Studies is an interdisciplinary program that encourages the development of critical perspectives in both intellectual and activist contexts. Gender and Sexaulity Studies teaches students to re-examine traditional beliefs and to recognize that gender, race, class, ethnicity, age, sexuality, and nationality are crucial aspects of identity and can be understood primarily in their intersections. The program offers a Major, a Minor, an Honors track, and a Graduate Certificate.
summary: Gender and Sexuality Studies is an interdisciplinary program that encourages the development of critical perspectives in both intellectual and activist contexts.

name: Undergraduate Political Science Association
description:
The purposes and objectives of the UPSA are

  1. To promote a better understanding of political science;
  2. To foster a relationship between faculty and students by providing a channel of communication between political science students, political science professors, and political science departmental groups;
  3. To represent the undergraduate political science students before other departmental groups;
  4. To sponsor projects and events which may be of specific interest to political science students; and
  5. To motivate and inspire undergraduates to engage political science in meaningful ways and pursue careers and other opportunities in the field of political science.
summary: The Vanderbilt Undergraduate Political Science Association's mission is twofold. First, the group works to facilitate interactions between faculty and students. Second, the group works to enhance political science students' understanding of the field.

name: Owen Tech Club
description: Our mission is to educate and provide resources to students interested in the technology industry. The Tech Club will leverage the local tech community, students and alums with experience in tech, and the greater Vanderbilt community to provide our members with educational and networking opportunities. The Tech Club seeks to help its members attain their career goals as well as help establish Owen as a leader in tech recruiting.
summary: The purpose of the club is to educate and provide resources to students regarding careers in technology industry. The club would provide students with several platforms to network with Owen alumni and technology companies.

name: Project Safe
description:
Project Safe Center for Sexual Misconduct Prevention and Response

The Project Safe Center is here for anyone affected by sexual assault, sexual harassment, dating violence, stalking, or domestic violence. We offer awareness-raising, prevention, and culture change programming to Vanderbilt students, faculty, and staff.

We are located on main campus in the Cumberland House on West Side Row.  Our physical address is 2304H Vanderbilt Place, Nashville, TN, 37235.  Our 24-hour hotline is available by calling (615) 322-SAFE (7233) or you contact our office line 615-875-0660 Monday through Friday, 8:00 AM to 5:00 PM.

Talk To an Advocate

Project Safe is committed to preventing all types of intimate partner violence (sexual assault, dating and domestic violence, stalking, sexual exploitation, and more), helping those affected by these forms of sexual misconduct, answering questions, and more.

24-Hour Crisis Line: 615-322-SAFE (7233)

In an Emergency

The following services are available 24 hours a day, 7 days a week:

  • Project Safe Support Line: 615-322-SAFE (7233)
  • Vanderbilt University Police: 911 or 615-421-1911
  • Vanderbilt University Medical Center Emergency Services: 615-322-0160
  • Sexual Assault Center hotline: 1-800-879-1999

Drop-ins welcome Monday – Friday, 8 a.m. – 5 p.m.  Appointments with a Victim Resource Specialist may also be scheduled via email to projectsafe@vanderbilt.edu or by calling our office line 615-875-0660.

Phone: 615-322-SAFE (7233)
summary: The Project Safe Center works to prevent and reduce sexual misconduct and interpersonal violence (sexual assault, sexual harassment, stalking, dating/domestic violence) in the Vanderbilt community through prevention programming and advocacy services.

organizationType.name: Student Affairs Department, Office, or Program

name: Vanderbilt University Women in Science and Engineering
description:
VU-WiSE aims to develop a community of women in science, engineering and mathematics at Vanderbilt University and their supporters. We work to support the personal and professional development of these women at all levels of their careers through networking, mentoring, seminars, outreach, and other events focused on professional development. We also aim to inspire the next generation of women scientists by engaging with girls in the greater Nashville community. We are comprised of undergrads, graduate students, postdocs, faculty, and staff!

Leadership

President: Destiny Harrison

Vice President: Ella Wavering

Secretary/Communications Chair: Uvarani Rajamani

Treasurer/Peer Accountability Officer: Destiny Harrison

Social Chair: Kelsey Nocilla

Mentorship Chairs: Carissa Kim & Gigi Hunn

Outreach Chairs: Eunice Hahm & Kathleen Olsen

Faculty Advisor:  Dr. Senta V Greene
summary: VU-WiSE is a community and support network of women in science, engineering and mathematics at Vanderbilt and their supporters.

name: Lights on the Lawn
description:
Lights on the Lawn is a student-run philanthropy organization comprised of IFC fraternity members committed to supporting the Mary Parrish Center (MPC), a transitional housing nonprofit for survivors of sexual and domestic abuse. The MPC helps survivors get back on their feet after living through detrimental relationships. The organization achieves this goal primarily through the planning and execution of an annual on-campus concert, from which all profits are donated to the Mary Parrish Center.

 
Past artists have included Quinn XCII, Diplo, Louis the Child, the Chainsmokers, Two Friends, Gryffin, and many more!
summary: Planning and executing the annual Lights on the Lawn Philanthropy Concert

name: Vanderbilt Journal of Entertainment & Technology Law
description:
The Vanderbilt Journal of Entertainment & Technology Law (JETLaw) is an official, highly ranked, student-run publication of Vanderbilt Law School focusing on technology, intellectual property, and entertainment law. JETLaw publishes four issues annually, including a special issue comprised of papers presented at its annual symposium, hosted on the law school campus in Nashville, TN.

JETLaw serves as a unique and inclusive forum for cutting-edge legal scholarship dedicated to the expanding nexus of entertainment, technology, and law in an increasingly connected world. Recognizing that this fusion of entertainment and technology poses novel legal issues in many fields of law, JETLaw welcomes scholarly submissions from the perspectives of copyright, administrative, sports, art, science, technology, and entertainment law, among others.

In addition to the printed journal, JETLaw seeks to connect interested communities through its blog, which incorporates easily accessible legal analysis of current events, cases, and issues related to the journal’s scope.

JETLaw was founded in 1998 as the Vanderbilt Journal of Entertainment Law and Practice and changed its name in 2005.
summary: The Vanderbilt Journal of Entertainment & Technology Law (JETLaw) is an official, student-edited publication of Vanderbilt Law School focusing on technology, intellectual property, and entertainment law.

name: Vanderbilt Journal of Transnational Law
description: Established in 1967, the Vanderbilt Journal of Transnational Law is one of the world’s most prominent and widely cited legal journals devoted to current and emerging issues of comparative and international law. Published by a student board, each of the five annual issues contains articles written by prominent international scholars and legal practitioners.
summary: Vanderbilt Journal of Transnational Law

name: Vanderbilt Lakshya
description:
Vanderbilt Lakshya is the Bollywood fusion dance team at Vanderbilt University that embraces cultural diversity through performance. Lakshya provides students with an opportunity to celebrate and learn about South Asian culture and heritage, blending various dance styles such as Hip-Hop, Bhangra, Kuthu, Indian Classical, and contemporary. Our team name, meaning "Goal" or "Aim," reflects our mission to expand our presence on campus and excel in the National Intercollegiate Bollywood Fusion circuit.

We are proud to have placed multiple times in the circuit, a testament to our dedication and skill. Beyond our achievements, Lakshya is known for its family-like team atmosphere, fostering a supportive and inclusive environment where every member can thrive.
summary: Vanderbilt Lakshya is the premier Bollywood Fusion dance team at Vanderbilt University that performs in the National Intercollegiate Dance Circuit by showcasing styles like hiphop, bollywood, contemporary, bhangra, and more.

name: University Staff Advisory Council
description: The University Staff Advisory Council (USAC) represents all staff members in Vanderbilt University (non-union) positions and is made up of more than 90 elected individuals, who represent the more than 3,000 full- and part-time exempt and non-exempt university staff members. USAC is an advisory group to the university’s administration, including Chancellor Nicholas S. Zeppos, on issues that are important to staff, such as policies, benefits and practices.
summary: The University Staff Advisory Council (USAC) represents all staff members in Vanderbilt University (non-union) positions. USAC is an advisory group to the university’s administration on issues that are important to staff.

name: Vanderbilt Gamecraft
description: Vanderbilt Gamecraft's mission is to foster social play of tabletop games at Vanderbilt University, as well as supporting students who wants to develop their tabletop experience. Vanderbilt Gamecraft runs weekly board game play events on campus to gather people together and teach them new games. It also provides a place for tabletop role-playing games.
summary:
An organization dedicated to fostering creativity and recreation through organization of tabletop games.

Meeting at Buttrick 205 every Friday at 6:00pm

name: National Society of Collegiate Scholars
description:
Email: nscs.vanderbilt@gmail.com

Facebook: National Society of Collegiate Scholars at Vanderbilt

Instagram: nscs.vanderbilt

 

The National Society of Collegiate Scholars (NSCS) is an honors organization that recognizes and elevates high achievers. NSCS provides career and graduate school connections, leadership and service opportunities and gives out a million dollars in scholarships annually. NSCS members are deeply committed to scholarship, leadership and service and as a result, are impacting their campus and local communities every day.

NSCS is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization founded in 1994 on the campus of The George Washington University in Washington, D.C. NSCS is a member of the Association of College Honor Societies and is a recognized organization at over 330 campuses across the country.
summary: NSCS is an honors organization that recognizes and elevates high achievers. NSCS provides career and graduate school connections, leadership and service opportunities and gives out nearly a million dollars in scholarships.

name: Vanderbilt Finance Club
description: The Vanderbilt Finance Club is the university’s largest and leading organization for students interested in finance. We provide undergraduates with opportunities to explore a wide range of careers and pathways in the financial industry, while fostering meaningful relationships with peers and upperclassmen mentors who offer guidance and support throughout their professional journeys.
summary: Vanderbilt's Premier Undergraduate Organization for Securing a Job in Finance

name: MOSAIC
description:
MOSAIC (Medley Of Students And Ideas Connecting) exists as an extension of the Office of Undergraduate Admissions to connect prospective students to the Vanderbilt student perspective. Through various programming, such as MOSAIC Weekend, members will aid in the recruitment of students who will contribute to Vanderbilt’s value of developing and maintaining a campus community of ethnic and cultural diversity.

 

Vanderbilt celebrates a rich diversity of thought and identity. We believe a residential community provides the opportunity for a well-rounded academic and social experience. Our students, faculty, staff, programming, and community initiatives all demonstrate Vanderbilt's commitment to diversity. We strive each year to recruit a highly qualified first-year class from a variety of populations.
summary: Medley Of Students And Ideas Connecting (MOSAIC) Recruitment Committee exists as an extension of Undergraduate Admissions to connect prospective students to the Vanderbilt student perspective, as well as to provide community-building programming.

name: Women's Club Water Polo
description:
Women's Club Water polo invites students and faculty of all skill levels to try the sport of water polo. Our goal is to make water polo accessible and fun while building community. Please reach out if you are interested!

Join our GroupMe for more info on practices and tournaments: https://tinyurl.com/Vandy-Wapo
summary: Women's water polo is a mix of undergraduate, graduate, and professional students with or without a background in water polo. We are a growing team always looking for new members! Join our GroupMe for more info: https://tinyurl.com/Vandy-Wapo

name: Nilaja Amari African Dance Troupe
description: Nilaja Amari is Vanderbilt’s premier African dance group made up of a diverse group of students who are passionate about African culture and dance. Before the founding of Nilaja Amari, there were few opportunities for students to engage with African dance and culture. Through dance, Nilaja Amari strives to expose more of the Vanderbilt and Nashville communities to African culture and celebrate the diversity in the many styles from several different countries. By showcasing our efforts, we hope that the Vanderbilt and Nashville communities will also continue to grow in appreciation for African dance.
summary: Nilaja Amari is Vanderbilt’s premier African dance group of diverse students passionate about African culture and dance. Through dance, Nilaja Amari strives to expose more communities to the culture and celebrate diversity.

name: Sankofa: Fellows in African American & Diaspora Studies
description:
Sankofa in the Akan language of Ghana translates in English to "reach back and get it" or "we must go back and reclaim our past so we can move forward". It is often associated with the proverb, "Se wo were fi na wosankofa a yenkyi," which translates "It is not wrong to go back for that which you have forgotten. It symbolizes one taking from the past what is good and bringing it into the present in order to make positive progress through the benevolent use of knowledge. In this vein, we as an organization strive to discuss and tackle issues afflicting our lives in the present by understanding the varying structures of the past.

The purpose of Sankofa is to: 1) provide a space of no judgment for agency and voice for intellectuals within African-American & Diaspora Studies and community-at-large; 2) promote pride and awareness of the vast Black Diaspora through multiple entities, not only to its members but to the entire Vanderbilt community; and 3) support and promote the scholarship of African American & Diaspora Studies Department.
summary: Among our many goals, we in Sankofa strive to ultimately provide a space of no judgement for agency and voice for intellectuals within African-American & Diaspora Studies and the community-at-large.

name: Wesley Fellowship
description:
Vandy Wesley offers a break from this daily grind, a free meal, and a place to deeply connect with others as we explore our faith together. 

At Wesley we desire to create a community that is intentionally diverse, radically welcoming, practices ways to connect with God and each other, and invites everyone to live our their unique gifts and strengths.

Vandy Wesley welcomes and celebrates people of all races, religions, economic statuses, sexual orientations, ethnicity, gender identities, gender expressions, and mental or physical ability.

We believe that the decision to follow Jesus is a life long journey with many twists and turns.  We value asking questions of Scripture, reflection on our experiences, courageous and vulnerable conversations, and strive to be open to different perspectives and beliefs.
summary: We are a Methodist-based campus ministry that welcomes students from all backgrounds. We encourage open dialogue and critical thinking about our personal faith journeys. We gather weekly for dinner and student-led discussions, as well as, special events.

name: Susan Gray School
description:
The Susan Gray School, originally named the Peabody Experimental School, opened in 1968 as an on-campus research-oriented school devoted to educational research involving young children with developmental disabilities and children whose future development was at risk because of conditions such as poverty. In 1986 the School was renamed the Susan Gray School in recognition of Dr. Susan Gray (1913-1992), a Peabody faculty member and national authority on the early education of "disadvantaged" children.

The pioneering work of Dr. Gray was recognized nationally and Sargent Shriver, founding director of the Peace Corps and architect of the Head Start program, credited her with the inspiration for founding Head Start: "Dr. Gray's pioneering experiments showed us what could be accomplished and how to go about it. Through Head Start and through the even more massive programs for child development which have flowed from the Head Start experiment, Susan Gray has made an enduring contribution to the nation's welfare."

For nearly four decades, the Susan Gray School has served young children with developmental disabilities and young children who are at risk for developmental delay. The school has the distinction of being the first nationally recognized early intervention program to include typically developing children in educational settings with children with disabilities.

Today, the school's four-fold mission to service, training, research, and demonstration continues. Susan Gray School is approved through the Tennessee Department of Education and is licensed through the Tennessee Department of Intellectual Developmental Disabilities.
summary: Susan Gray School-Peabody College- Vanderbilt University

name: Vanderbilt Philippine Dance Theatre
description:
Vanderbilt Philippine Dance Theatre (VPDT) is a performing arts group dedicated to traditional Filipino dance. Currently, VPDT choreographs, teaches, and performs two traditional dances. 

 

Tinikling, the dance of fast footwork, comes with its own percussion as dancers' feet weave between two bamboo sticks that beat out the quick pace of the music. 

Sayaw sa Bangko, the bench dance, brings drama to new heights as dancers twirl, jump, and lift each other in increasingly dangerous feats on benches stacked higher and higher.

 

VPDT serves as an important bridge between the Philippine Intercultural Student Organization (PISO) and the Vanderbilt Performing Arts Council (VPAC). Performances occur throughout the year, but audition scheduling and most practices center around the Lunar New Year Festival each February.
summary: An organization dedicated to traditional Filipino dances — Tinikling and Sayaw sa Bangko.

name: Trauma Survivors Network
summary: The Trauma Survivors Network is to provide counseling and peer mentors to those who have been affected physically and mentally by traumatic events

name: Vanderbilt Law Review
description: The Vanderbilt Law Review is a legal academic journal that publishes six issues a year (January, March, April, May, October, and November) containing professor-written articles and student-written notes.
summary: The Vanderbilt Law Review is a legal academic journal that publishes six issues a year (January, March, April, May, October, and November) containing professor-written articles and student-written notes.

name: School of Medicine Health Sciences Education - Office of Programs & Activities
summary: Vanderbilt University School of Medicine, Office of Programs and Activities, Health Sciences Education

name: Vanderbilt Association for Behavior Analysis
description:
VABA assists students and faculty interested in behavior analysis with accessing and affecting the larger behavior analytic community through sponsorship of well-known and contemporary speakers in the field, and by providing opportunities to prepare for and attend behavior analytic meetings and conferences.  In addition, these opportunities are enhanced by the dissemination of new research-based technology, additional information important to application, and with interest specific opportunities for collaboration and brainstorming on behavior analytic research.  VABA also provides skill-building opportunities to the larger community we serve by offering workshops, continuing education, and user-friendly materials directed at enhancing quality of life for individuals with developmental disabilities and mental illness, as well as those who serve supportive roles for these individuals.  By engaging in these services and supporting students in pursuit of careers in behavior analytic research and practice, we hope to advance the overall performance of our members as such that they are able to benefit the Department of Special Education, Vanderbilt University, the field of behavior analysis, and the greater community as a whole. 

 

Graduate students enrolled in the Special Education Applied Behavior Analysis Specialization Program are automatically members of VABA and may attend events at no cost.

 

Community members seeking CEUs may do so in one of two ways:
1. They may pay $10/CEU/event at the door.
2. They may pay $20 for VABA Membership and earn CEUs from any eligible event at no additional cost (membership cycle runs from July to June of the following year).

 

Community members NOT seeking CEUs may attend events for free.
summary: Vanderbilt Association for Behavior Analysis

name: Vanderbilt LifeFlight
summary: Vanderbilt LifeFlight is the critical care air medical transport service of Vanderbilt University Medical Center and the Monroe Carell Jr. Children's Hospital at Vanderbilt since 1984.

name: Trap and Skeet Shooting Club
description: The purpose of the Vanderbilt Skeet Shooting Team (VST) is to promote the importance of handling firearms safely; to educate competitors about the sport of shooting trap, skeet, or clay; to encourage healthy competition through teamwork and responsibility; to create strong connections between students and the skeet shooting community on both a local as well as national scale. All of these goals are accomplished through various extra curricular activities, community service opportunities, and participation in shooting competitions throughout the school year.
summary: The Trap and Skeet Shooting Club encourages and develops a safe, competitive environment for hunting, target shooting, and other kindred sports.

name: Department of Special Education
description:
Ranked as a top-tier special education program in the nation by U.S. News & World Report for more than a decade, the department has long been a leader both in research and practice. The department ranks first in scholarly production among other departments at research universities in the United States, according to a new report by Academic Analytics.

With a reputation for teaching, conducting groundbreaking research, establishing innovative practices, and developing widely-used methods, we provide practitioners, researchers, parents, and policy-makers with information to act on behalf of children and youth with disabilities.

The mission of the Department of Special Education is to improve the lives of children and youth with disabilities through (a) the preparation of exceptionally competent teachers and researchers, (b) conducting research that informs and improves educational programs and behavioral interventions, and (c) leadership in professional associations and advocacy for persons with disabilities.
summary: Ranked as a top-tier special education program in the nation by U.S. News & World Report for more than a decade, the department has long been a leader both in research and practice.

name: Upper-Division Residential Colleges
description: Upper-division residential colleges are home to sophomore, junior, and senior students. This residential option expands on the experience provided during students' first year on The Ingram Commons. As of fall 2022, there are five upper-division colleges: E. Bronson Ingram, Moore, Nicholas S. Zeppos, Rothschild, and Warren colleges.
summary: Upper-division residential colleges are home to sophomore, junior, and senior students. This residential option expands on the experience provided during students' first year on The Ingram Commons.

name: Project C.U.R.E.
description:
We are dedicated to connecting the Vanderbilt community to the non-profit, Project C.U.R.E., which delivers donated medical supplies from hospitals and clinics in the U.S. to resource-starved hospitals and clinics in over 130 developing countries. We also hope to help the Vanderbilt community understand the value and necessity of their global health service.

 

Join our GroupMe! https://groupme.com/join_group/80703187/nve0deVH
summary: We are dedicated to connecting the Vanderbilt community to the non-profit, Project C.U.R.E., which delivers donated medical supplies from hospitals and clinics in the U.S. to resource-starved hospitals and clinics in over 130 developing countries.

name: POIESIS
description: POIESIS is a Ministry of the Arts, that exists to provide space for student creativity. POIESIS seeks to allow students the opportunity for artistic growth, expression, and incorporation within their degree program at Vanderbilt Divinity School. POIESIS is an intersectional space that encourages creativity and making in the divinity context - our coming together around the arts for personal expression of our identities and theologies as well as for social change.
summary: Ministry of the Arts

name: Club Sports
description:
Vanderbilt Club Sports is a comprehensive 34 team program that operates within the Vanderbilt Recreation & Wellness Center (the Rec). The Club Sports program adheres to the Rec's mission and creates an inclusive environment for its members. Club sports are both recreational and competitive and open to all Vanderbilt students, faculty, and staff. Members can develop individual skills, compete at the collegiate level, or learn a new sport. Participation in the Club Sports program provides many benefits including the opportunity to be part of a community, develop a social network, and take part in personal and professional growth. A successful club sport is led by dependable student officers who motivate their members, meet administrative deadlines, are financially responsible, and promote teamwork.

 

Sports Clubs

  • Baseball
  • Brazilian Jiu Jitsu
  • Climbing
  • Cricket
  • Fencing
  • Field Hockey (Women)
  • Golf (Men)
  • Gymnastics
  • Ice Hockey
  • Kung Fu
  • Lacrosse (Men)
  • Lacrosse (Women)
  • Trap and Skeet
  • Rowing
  • Rugby (Men)
  • Running
  • Sailing
  • Soccer (Men)
  • Soccer (Women)
  • Speed/Figure Skating
  • Spike Ball
  • Squash (Co-ed)
  • Swim 
  • Table Tennis
  • Tennis
  • Ultimate Frisbee (Men)
  • Ultimate Frisbee (Women)
  • Volleyball (Men)
  • Volleyball (Women)
  • Water Polo (Men)
  • Water Polo (Women)
  • Taekwondo

Student Leadership Opportunities 

Through practices, competition, travel, socials, community service and fundraisers, club members establish friendships and skills that develop them physically, socially and intellectually. Participation in the Club Sports program provides many benefits including the opportunity to be part of a community, develop a social network, and take part in personal and professional growth. A successful club sport is led by dependable student officers who motivate their members, meet administrative deadlines, are financially responsible, and promote teamwork.

To be a recognized club sport through the Rec, the student organization must be established, maintained and developed by volunteer student leaders.  Leadership opportunities are available for club members through officer positions.  Club officers and contributing members are responsible for writing, editing and upholding their constitution and bylaws.  Other responsibilities include determining membership requirements and dues structure, establishing a consensus surrounding level and amount of activity, managing club budgets and fundraising.  

At minimum, clubs have a typical structure of a President, Vice President, Treasurer, and Secretary. Other recognized positions may be created within the Club operations. Clubs may also consider a travel officer, social/fundraising officer, practice/game officer, etc., depending on the activity level of the Club.

Eligibility 

All undergraduate and graduate students officially enrolled at Vanderbilt University are eligible to join a club. There may be certain restrictions from competition by league or association regulations.

Renewing Your Club

Existing sport clubs are required to re-register through Anchorlink annually to maintain official status. Typically, the renewal process is at the end of the Spring semester through Anchorlink. 

In addition to adhering to the Club Sports program’s policies and procedures, it is required that each club maintain a minimum of 10 active members on their annual roster.  Club activity occurs during the fall and spring semesters, though a handful of clubs practice during the summer and winter sessions.  Individual club seasons can vary based on the sport and the governing body.  Clubs that are only active during one semester, are still required to attend training and meet administrative deadlines during their off season semester in order to maintain recognition within the Club Sports program. Sanctions are applied when a club or club member violates University policies, department expectations or program requirements. 

Registering A New Club

If you are interested in applying to the Club Sports program, please contact the Rec Competitive Sports professional staff to review the application process, recognition requirements and timeline also available on the Rec website in the club handbook.
summary: Designed to serve individual interests and foster a team atmosphere, our Club Sports Program is a place for you to continue playing a favorite sport or learn to play a new one. Come make some new friends and stay active!

name: Indigenous Scholars Organization
description: The Indigenous Scholars Organization (ISO) aspires to serve a critical role in promoting the cultural knowledge and community involvement of its members in a collaborative environment, as well as educating the broader Vanderbilt and Nashville community about Indigenous cultures.  By fostering an atmosphere that promotes social engagement and group support, we hope to cultivate a close-knit community with a collective vision at the institutional level, to increase Indigenous presence at Vanderbilt University. Additionally, we aim to serve the surrounding Indigenous communities and those without origins of Indigeneity to the Americas, in a State without formal American Indian/Alaska Native representation.  ISO embraces all Indigenous students, regardless of tribal enrollment, status, or blood quantum. This includes students from Native American, Alaska Native, First Nation, Pacific Islander and other Indigenous backgrounds.
summary: The Indigenous Scholar Organization (ISO) serves a critical role in promoting the cultural knowledge and community involvement of its members in a collaborative environment, as well as educating our community about Indigenous cultures.

name: The Office of Al's Pub
description: Al's Pub is a twice monthly happy hour organization which exists to facilitate community and fellowship between students, faculty, and staff at Vanderbilt Divinity School.
summary: Divinity School Organization that facilitates community and fellowship through happy hours and event co-sponsorships.

name: Vanderbilt Alliance on Disability and Condition
description:
Welcome!

The Vanderbilt Alliance on Disability and Condition was founded after we noticed that there was no form of community on-campus for students with disabilities and other medical conditions. Whether you are impaired with a cognitive, physical, mental, or social condition; whether you have a visible or invisible condition; and whether you personally play host or simply care for the cause; VADC is the premier outlet for community and discussion on all things disability as faced by the students at Vanderbilt.

VADC is oriented towards two primary causes: (1) engaging the Vanderbilt community at-large in disability-forward discussion through interactions with other student organizations and (2) organizing VADC-run events to bring students together in disability-related programming through general body meetings (GBMs). GBMs may vary from a relevant movie showing followed by a discussion to forums pertaining to Student Access Services and accommodations at Vanderbilt.

Check out our upcoming GBMs or affiliated events. We hope to see you soon!
summary: The Vanderbilt Alliance on Disability and Condition (VADC) is the premier student organization for those interested in discussion and advocacy pertaining to the disabilities and conditions affecting the student body and our greater communities.

name: Department of Health Policy
description:
Welcome to Health Policy

The Department of Health Policy was established in 2013 and includes faculty formerly in the Department of Preventive Medicine. Faculty in the department have a variety of interests including health care financing, health insurance coverage, aging and long-term care, provider payment, health services research, pharmacoepidemiology, cancer epidemiology, and infectious diseases. The Department has a history of strong collaborative relationships with the Tennessee Department of Health and the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention and receives funding from numerous federal agencies and foundations.

Contact hpevents@vanderbilt.edu to sign up for the Department of Health Policy's newsletter and receive information about upcoming campus speakers and events.
summary: To inform health policy and to improve health and health care through research, education and service.

name: Cork & Tap Society at Vanderbilt University Law School
description: Our mission is to explore the legal intricacies surrounding the production and distribution of alcoholic beverages. By taking advantage of Nashville’s unique proximity to many of the region’s alcohol producers, our goal is to take a deeper look at the implications of trademark, property, environmental, and regulatory legal issues, among others, on this constantly evolving industry.
summary: The purpose of the Cork and Tap Society is to provide interested students an organization in which to explore the various legal issues relating to the alcoholic beverage industry, in both domestic and international settings.

name: Association of Women Surgeons
description: The Association of Women Surgeons is dedicated to the mission of inspiring and supporting women in all stages of medical training and to inspire, encourage, and enable female surgeons to achieve their personal and professional goals. The AWS Vanderbilt Chapter will facilitate the success of aspiring women surgeons by 1) highlighting practicing role models at Vanderbilt and within the community, 2) providing networking and mentorship opportunities with surgeons and surgical residents, and 3) providing a forum for open discussion and collaboration on strategies related to professional development and the challenges woman surgeons face.
summary: The Association of Women Surgeons is dedicated to the mission of inspiring and supporting women in all stages of medical training and to inspire, encourage, and enable female surgeons to achieve their personal and profession.(Medical School Organization)

name: Vanderbilt UNICEF
description: Vanderbilt UNICEF seeks to promote UNICEF USA’s mission of educating, advocating, and fundraising on behalf of UNICEF. It seeks to enrich Vanderbilt’s culture by providing students with the opportunity to learn more about world events and then act to try to help the world’s children.
summary: Our mission is to support UNICEF USA in its fight to ensure every child's right to be happy, healthy, and safe. Help us in our fundraising, awareness, and advocacy efforts on the organization's behalf!

name: Italian Club
summary: The Italian Club is open to all students, regardless of their background or proficiency in Italian. Whether you're of Italian descent, studying the language, or fascinated by Italian culture, you'll find a warm and inclusive community here.

name: Vitality Dance Company
description: Vitality Dance Company finds its purpose in providing the Vanderbilt student community with the opportunity to choreograph, perform, and take dance classes in a variety of styles. With a focus on technique and artistry, Vitality allows dancers from a variety of backgrounds to explore the large breadth of expression that dance has to offer. We encourage all members to develop their artistry by choreographing and/or performing in impactful, unique, and inspired pieces. Vitality produces a polished showcase every semester that aims to tell an overarching story and move audience members. In addition, members have the opportunity to engage with the Vanderbilt and Nashville community through open workshops and philanthropy events.
summary: Vitality Dance Company finds its purpose in providing the Vanderbilt undergraduate student community with the opportunity to choreograph, perform, and take dance classes in a variety of dance styles.

name: Vanderbilt Divinity School
summary: The Divinity School is committed to the faith that brought the church into being, and it believes that one comes more authentically to grasp that faith by a critical and open examination of the Hebraic and Christian traditions.

name: Peabody Q. Ed.
description: An organization providing event programming and community space for LGBTQIA+ graduate and professional students and their allies at Peabody College at Vanderbilt University.
summary: Through intentional, community-building outreach, advocacy, social events, and education, Peabody Q.Ed. (Queering Education and Development) will make Peabody a safer, more inclusive environment for LGBTQI+ students and their allies.

name: Pharmacology Graduate Student Association
description:
The Pharmacology Graduate Student Association (PhGSA) of Vanderbilt University is a representative body of all students in the Pharmacology Department at Vanderbilt University as well as other Vanderbilt students interested in the discipline. The PhGSA strives to facilitate an understanding and appreciation of the pharmacological sciences in both the campus and civic communities through:

  • Academic events to increase awareness of leading pharmacological trends and career opportunities in the sciences,
  • Community outreach to develop a better understanding of current health issues as well as cutting-edge therapies to educate the populace as well as increase the visibility of the important work done in the Department of Pharmacology here at Vanderbilt, and,
  • Social activities to help connect and support the students, faculty, staff, and community supporters of the pharmacological sciences.

The Pharmacology Graduate Student Association strives to promote pharmacological graduate student integration within the university community.
summary:
PhGSA strives to facilitate an understanding and appreciation of the
pharmacological sciences in both the campus and civic communities.

name: Society for the Advancement of Chicanos/Hispanics and Native Americans in Science
description:
Mission:

To empower and support underrepresented students in STEM by creating and promoting opportunities for professional and personal growth, community building, while integrating identities and cultures.
summary: SACNAS serves to increase the number of Chicanos/Hispanics and Native Americans in science by supporting them throughout their college, advanced education, and professional careers.

name: Latin American and Caribbean Student Association
description:
We are the first school-wide organization that aims to bring together people from the geographical and historical areas of Mexico, Central America, the Caribbean Islands, and South America, as well as those who are interested or passionate about the region. We are a pluralistic and inclusive organization seeking to build community and cultural awareness among Vanderbilt graduate students and Nashville at large.

We want to serve as a gathering point for people of Latin America and the Caribbean, to be a support for international students from the region who are already in Nashville or are coming next year, and to make ourselves present on the Vanderbilt campus and in Nashville in diverse ways.

We have a lot to offer and a lot to share among ourselves!!

Our main goals are:

  1. To be a gathering point for Latin American and Caribbean students and all other students that identify themselves as Latin American or Caribbean, or feel a strong connection, interest, or passion for Latin America and the Caribbean regardless of citizenship.
  2. Promote the diversity of Latin American and Caribbean cultures through events and other activities, such as holidays of the region or the countries of the region.
  3. Showcase aspects of Latin American and Caribbean cultures at different events (e.g., World on Wednesdays, invited speakers, etc.).
  4. Welcome, assist, and guide Latin American and Caribbean students upon arrival to Vanderbilt.
  5. Offer support to Vanderbilt students interested in learning about, working on, or visiting Latin America and the Caribbean.
  6. Establish links with Latin American-, Caribbean-, Hispanic-, and Latino-oriented (or interested) organizations on campus such as Vanderbilt Association of Hispanic Students, La Alianza at Vanderbilt Law School, AMIGOS, Center for Latin American Studies (CLAS), the Humphrey Fellowship Program, Lambda Theta Alpha Latin Sorority, Latin@/American@ Seminarians (LAS), the Vanderbilt chapter of the national Latino Medical Student Association, and the Owen Latin Business Association, and collaborate in issues of common interest.
  7. Collaborate with organizations that interact with Latin American and Caribbean students, or whose focus is Latin America and the Caribbean, such as ISSS and CLAS, GSC, among others, in issues of common interest.
  8. Lend support to Latin American, Caribbean, and Latino communities in Nashville and surrounding areas.
summary: LACS is a pluralistic and inclusive organization seeking to build cultural awareness among Vanderbilt graduate students. Our community serves as a gathering point for graduate students from Latin America and the Caribbean region at Vanderbilt.

name: Brazilian Student Association (BRASA) at Vanderbilt University
summary: BRASA's goal is to promote opportunities for Brazilians studying outside of Brazil. Our mission is to connect Brazilian students studying abroad, empower the Brazilian community at Vanderbilt, and support professional development at Vanderbilt.

name: Hidden Dores
description: Hidden Dores strives to reduce the barriers surrounding the experiences of marginalized communities, particularly people of color, across the Vanderbilt community by advocating for social and administrative change and challenging conceptions of race, identity, and equality.
summary: Hidden Dores is an ongoing student led campaign aiming to illuminate and improve the experiences of marginalized students on Vanderbilt's campus.

name: Black Cultural Center
description:
The Center was established in 1984, in honor of Bishop Joseph Johnson, the first African American to attend and graduate from Vanderbilt University.   

The mission of the Bishop Joseph Johnson Black Cultural Center is to enhance the student experience at Vanderbilt by providing programming and resources that educate about Black history, highlight Black accomplishments, and showcase Black culture, while promoting belonging and creating community for all.

The BCC’s programs and partnerships, serve to promote respect, understanding, tolerance and a greater appreciation for community and belonging.

What goes on at the BCC?


The BCC sponsors speakers’ series and panel discussions, BBC Book Club discussions, and poetry readings.  You can come to the BCC to see a play, witness a musical performance, or take part in one of a number of annual events and celebrations, as such Dia de la Raza, pre-Kwanzaa; Walter Murray Lecture (co-sponsored with The Ingram Commons) and Black History and Women’s History Month events.

What goes on at the BCC is a celebration, open to the campus community, of Black culture, accomplishments, and contributions of those at Vanderbilt and beyond.

Why should I visit the BCC?

You should stop by to gain an understanding of and deeper appreciation for the contributions of people of African descent.  We invite you to visit the BCC—to see our exhibit of African American and African art; to feast on Soul Food during our fall semester Welcome Back Luncheon; to meet the BCC staff and make new friends among the students, faculty, staff and alumni who frequent the BCC.  Don’t just be a visitor, come back often—to see a visiting art exhibit, or hear a stimulating panel discussion; and make new friends!

Who utilizes the BCC?

The BCC is open to any and all VU students, faculty and staff! Vanderbilt faculty hold academic classes in the BCC Seminar room, and the BCC auditorium hosts numerous scholarly presentations for Vanderbilt departments, schools and colleges.   We also host events for community partners such as the YMCA, Big Brothers, Big Sisters, and Organized Neighbors of Edgehill.

Some of the famous speakers who have visited the BCC include Angela Davis, Julian Bond, the late Negro Leagues Legend, Buck O’Neil, Afeni Shakur (mother of the late Tupac Shakur), and Dr. Eugene Richardson of the Tuskegee Airmen.

BCC facilities—auditorium, seminar room, computer lab and lounge—are open to the entire university, and are heavily utilized by students, staff, and faculty.
summary: The Bishop Joseph Johnson Black Cultural Center (BCC) is a gathering place; a home away from home for students who study in the BCC; gather there for meetings and learn about African and African American culture through its programs.

organizationType.name: Student Affairs Department, Office, or Program

name: American Chemical Society - Vanderbilt Chapter
description: The purpose of Vanderbilt ACS is to foster a community for those passionate about chemistry's possibilities and to provide resources for the professional, academic, and exploratory guidance for those in the field of chemistry or related fields.
summary: The purpose of Vanderbilt ACS is to foster a community for those passionate about chemistry's possibilities and to provide resources for the professional, academic, and exploratory guidance for those in the field of chemistry or related fields.

name: Vanderbilt University School of Medicine
description:
About the School

 

The Vanderbilt University School of Medicine administers degree programs that provide students with the knowledge, skills, and attitudes they will need to practice safe, effective, ethical, evidence-based, and patient-centered health care in the 21st century, and to contribute to the knowledge base supporting it.

 

Mission

The vision of Vanderbilt University School of Medicine is to shape a future in which all persons reach their full health potential.

The core values of Vanderbilt University School of Medicine are integrity, inclusion, humility, mutual respect, and excellence.

The mission of Vanderbilt University School of Medicine is to catalyze the advancement of impactful discovery, servant leadership, and lifelong learning.

In order to carry out this mission, we make these strategic commitments:

  • To nurture the growth of clinicians, scientists, and educators who will serve and lead their local, national, and global communities.
  • To create, implement, and disseminate new knowledge that expands understanding of health, disease, and healthcare systems.
  • To teach, learn, and provide compassionate, personalized caring of the highest quality for every patient who seeks our service and to strive to achieve health equity in the populations we serve.
  • To embrace a culture of lifelong learning, critical thinking, and innovation so that we will continuously improve in all we do.
  • To build a diverse community of faculty, staff, and students that expands the richness of our learning environment and enhances excellence in all of our endeavors.
summary: Vanderbilt University School of Medicine trains leaders in medicine by providing the best faculty and physicians in the best learning environment for the best students in the world.

name: Project Sunshine
description:
Project Sunshine supports patients and their families in pediatric hospitals. We volunteer at Monroe Carell Jr. Children's Hospital at Vanderbilt several times a week in addition to our weekly club activities.

**All members who wish to volunteer at the hospital must be fully trained. Training dates are after the first GBM each month. GBMs are every Tuesday.

 

You must email a screenshot of your Protection of Minors certificate to vanderbilt@projectsunshine.org in order to be considered for AnchorLink membership. Our email listserv comes directly from our AnchorLink roster.
summary: A volunteer organization that supports patients and their families at Vanderbilt Children's Hospital through our hospital visits and weekly club activities.

name: Vanderbilt Department of Art
summary: An important goal of the Art Department is to help students become lifelong interpreters of the rich visual environment in our culture as well as proponents of the creative approach in their selected professions, whatever they might turn out to be.

name: VandyHacks
description: VandyHacks is an organization dedicated to fostering hacker culture and building a computer science community at Vanderbilt University. We accomplish this by hosting an annual hackathon - a 36 hour software development marathon during which attendees think of an idea and build it - alongside regular hack nights and study nights. The VandyHacks board consists of organizers who spend months planning these events in several different areas: Content, Design, Development, Hacker Experience, Marketing, Operations, and Sponsorship.
summary: VandyHacks is an organization dedicated to fostering hacker culture and building a computer science community at Vanderbilt University. We accomplish this by hosting an annual hackathon as well as regular hack nights and study nights throughout the year.

name: Vanderbilt University Amateur Radio Club
description: We are a small org on campus dedicated to deepening our knowledge surrounding RF engineering and wireless networking while at the same time interacting with the rest of the amatuer radio community in the Nashville area.
summary: Student, faculty, and alumnus hub for amateur radio enthusiasts. Our goal is to both assist new students in becoming certified as ham radio operators and to provide a friendly community for VU-affiliated licensed and aspiring "hams."

name: Vanderbilt Historical Review
description:
Vanderbilt Historical Review is an undergraduate journal of history. The publication provides a forum for original historical research and emphasizes the importance of studying the past. This organization helps students hone their skills in historical research, editing, and publishing. 

To learn more visit our website: www.vanderbilthistoricalreview.com
summary: The Vanderbilt Historical Review is an undergraduate journal of history. The publication provides a forum for original historical research and emphasizes the importance of studying the past.

name: Pi Kappa Alpha
description: Sigma Chapter of The Pi Kappa Alpha Fraternity
summary: Greek Organization

name: Owen Out & Allied
description: • Our mission is to provide an open and supportive educational, social, and professional environment for the LGBTQ+ community at Owen. Specifically, Out & Allied strives to strengthen allyship, promote an inclusive and diverse culture, and further the professional goals of our members.
summary: Our mission is to provide an open and supportive environment for the LGBTQ+ community at Owen. Out & Allied strives to strengthen allyship, promote an inclusive and diverse culture, and further the professional goals of our members.

name: Pakistani Students Association
description:
The Pakistan Students Association (PSA) at Vanderbilt University aims to promote a better understanding and appreciation of Pakistani culture within the Vanderbilt community. The organization aims to achieve this by hosting a variety of events that showcase different aspects of Pakistani heritage and identity, including cultural showcases, social gatherings, and educational seminars. Through these events, the PSA strives to educate the Vanderbilt community about the rich and diverse culture of Pakistan, breaking down stereotypes and promoting harmony.

The PSA is driven by the belief that a greater appreciation of different cultures and backgrounds contributes to the intellectual diversity of the Vanderbilt community. By creating an atmosphere of acceptance and inclusivity, the PSA hopes to foster a community that celebrates diversity and encourages open-mindedness and understanding. Through its efforts, the PSA aims to play a role in creating a vibrant and intellectually stimulating community at Vanderbilt University.
summary: PSA is a student-run organization that provides a platform for Vanderbilt students to come together and learn about Pakistani culture, and create an atmosphere of tolerance and acceptance.

name: VUSM Students for a National Health Program Club
summary: The VUSM Students for a National Health Program Chapter seeks to educate medical students about the benefits of a single-payer health care system as outlined by the Physicians’ for a National Health Program.

name: AmbassaDores: Dore for a Day
description:
‘Dore for a Day is an organization which seeks to introduce prospective students to Vanderbilt University from a uniquely student perspective. Working in conjunction with the Office of Undergraduate Admissions, this organization helps recruit a broad range of prospective students through one-on-one visits to campus, where a host takes a visiting student to lunch, class, and on a brief tour of campus. ‘Dore for a Day members may also participate in other targeted programs throughout the year. ‘Dore for a Day members strive for the following:

  1. To provide prospective students with an inside look at Vanderbilt’s unique undergraduate student community.
  2. To foster a spirit of community and dedication to Vanderbilt within the ‘Dore for a Day organization.
  3. To connect personally with each and every personal visitor to campus.
  4. To embody and represent to the best of our ability the entire campus of 6,885 students.
summary: 'Dore for a Day is an organization which introduces prospective students to Vanderbilt from a uniquely student perspective. Members host prospective students in a more personalized  campus visit, including taking students to lunch, class & around campus.

name: Owen Music & Entertainment Club
description: The Owen Music & Entertainment Club (OMEC) connects students passionate about the music and entertainment industries with career-building opportunities in Nashville and beyond. OMEC supports those pursuing, transitioning into, or exiting careers in music by showcasing the industry's viability, fostering strong professional networks, and leveraging Nashville’s unique position as a global music hub. Through events, panels, and industry engagement, OMEC helps students explore diverse roles and pathways within music, from entrepreneurship and executive leadership to talent management and beyond.
summary: Owen Music & Entertainment Club brings Owen students together who share a passion for the vibrant music industry in Tennessee and beyond.

name: Vanderbilt IT
summary: Vanderbilt University Information Technology is a human-centric organization that advances our university by delivering innovative solutions and frictionless experiences through collaboration.

name: Vanderbilt Neuromuscular Physiology Lab
description: We use non-invasive techniques to study skeletal muscle and brown adipose tissue energetics and blood flow in vivo. In particular we are interested in the basic mechanisms that control tissue and organ energetics and blood flow and how they are impaired in chronic disease.
summary: The  Neuromuscular Physiology Lab at Vanderbilt University

name: Vanderbilt K-Pop
description: Vanderbilt K-Pop is a student organization that creates space for anyone who is interested in K-Pop to engage and share the culture! Our sub-group -- VIVID -- is a dance crew that performs K-Pop dance covers at our annual showcases, guest performances, and through video projects.
summary: K-Pop strives to enhance Vanderbilt’s exposure to popular South Korean pop culture through dance performances, club bondings, and other organization events.

name: Revitalizing & Empowering Vanderbilt's African Man Population
description: REVAMP is a student lead initiative that strives to establish and foster a sense of unity, strength, and love among Black men and to direct it towards uplifting the community at large. It is our goal to support the academic, social, intellectual and spiritual growth of Black undergraduate and graduate students as well as alumni, at Vanderbilt University by maintaining a community of men who will continuously support and encourage one another.
summary: REVAMP looks to promote the personal growth, maturity, and civic responsibility of Black men at Vanderbilt University and its surrounding communities.

name: Phi Alpha Theta - Alpha Beta Iota Chapter
description: We are a professional society whose mission is to promote the study of history through the encouragement of research, good teaching, publication and the exchange of learning and ideas among historians. We seek to bring students and teachers together for intellectual and social exchanges, which promote and assist historical research and publication by our members in a variety of ways.
summary: Phi Alpha Theta recognizes outstanding students of history with membership in its national honors society, while also organizing programming and outreach to promote the study of history. Students must be deemed eligible to join by the History Department.

name: Life's a Stitch
description:
Whether you're a seasoned knitter/crocheter or a newbie, this club offers a fun expression outlet which provides us with a pause in our crazy schedules to just sit back and relax with friends. This organization is a FUN ~service~ club where the meetings are a place to talk and laugh and share a common hobby. Our goal is for each member to be able to finish a project (blanket, scarf, hat, etc.) which at the end of the year can be donated to charity!

Meetings are weekly and supplies are provided for beginners. If you already knit or crochet please come along and bring your Works-In-Progress!
summary: Whether you're a seasoned knitter/crocheter or a newbie, this club offers a fun outlet which provides us with a pause in our crazy schedules to just sit back and relax with friends while creating amazing knit/crochet projects!

name: Vandy Actuarial
description:
The Vandy Actuarial is dedicated to educating students about the actuarial profession and promoting awareness of its diverse career opportunities. Our mission is to support students interested in actuarial science by providing resources, guidance, and industry exposure. Throughout the academic year, we organize informational sessions on actuarial exams, career paths, and skill development. We also host company presentations and networking events with industry professionals, giving students valuable insights into various practice areas such as life, health, property and casualty, and retirement.

GroupMe: https://groupme.com/join_group/105280938/equUgQ1C
summary: Educate students about actuarial science and expand awareness of the profession. We host informational sessions and company presentations to help members explore career paths, connect with industry professionals, and prepare for internships and exams.

name: Harmonies for the Elderly
description:
Harmonies for the Elderly, founded in 2010, is an organization of students of all ages who play music at nursing homes and hospitals. We incorporate aspects of music therapy while interacting with Alzheimer's and dementia patients. To date, we have performed over 114 concerts at various nursing homes and have improved the happiness and health of hundreds of people. We encourage musical participation from our friends, family, and the general community. Music has the extraordinary power to empower and comfort those most in need. 

 

S2025 Sign-Up Form here!

Join our Groupme here!

Follow our Instagram here!
summary: Harmonies for the Elderly strives to empower patients struggling with Alzheimer's and other cognitive aging disorders through interactive music performances.

name: University Concert Band
description:
Mission Statement: UCB provides an all-inclusive venue for non-music majors to participate in a band ensemble, foster their talents, and cultivate a sense of community within and beyond the Vanderbilt campus through music. 

 

We rehearse weekly on Mondays from 7-9pm in the Music Rehearsal Hall and perform in one concert per semester for the Vanderbilt community. We are primarily led by faculty members, but we also feature a few Blair students each year to conduct a piece in our concerts. UCB also has a chamber program which allows our band members more opportunities to perform on campus and represent UCB in various performing arts showcases. In the spring, UCB hosts Chamberfest to feature all of the chamber groups on campus. To build community within the band, we host various social events throughout the semester.

 

If you are interested in getting more information about our organization, feel free to contact us at concertband@vanderbilt.edu! Our Instagram @vandyucb also provides updates. We are looking forward to welcoming you to our band family!
summary: The University Concert Band (UCB) is an ensemble for non-music major woodwind, brass, and percussion musicians to host and develop their musical talents in a concert band setting with an emphasis placed on building community through music.

name: VandyWrites
description: VandyWrites is a community for students to read, discuss, and develop their writing. It is a safe and inclusive environment that encourages free-thought among its members. We host weekly drop-in "writing hours" for members to exchange ideas, receive feedback, and collaborate with fellow students in an encouraging environment. Some of our common monthly events include holiday writing games, a live writing showcase at the end of the semester, our cocktail party co-hosted with the English Department, and occasional guest lectures from distinguished authors, playwrights, screenwriters, and/or literary agents.
summary: As a student-led organization, VandyWrites is a writing community in which all Vanderbilt students can share ideas and improve as writers through crafting workshops, writing sessions, and guest lecturers.

name: Office of Student Accountability, Community Standards, & Academic Integrity
description:
Mission Statement

The Office of Student Accountability, Community Standards, & Academic Integrity promotes good citizenship within the Vanderbilt University community through education. Students are expected to respect themselves and others, to act responsibly, and to be accountable for their actions. The student accountability system addresses student violations of University policy through fair, consistent, and confidential procedures.
summary: The Office of Student Accountability, Community Standards, & Academic Integrity promotes good citizenship within the Vanderbilt University community through education.

organizationType.name: Student Affairs Department, Office, or Program

name: Meditation and Mindfulness Club
description: Organization that meets weekly to host meditation sessions as well as a post meditation thoughtful discussion. Goal is to continue to broaden services provided (such as yoga, tai chi, or other neglected forms of meditation) and integrate into Vanderbilt, working with heads of houses for mindfulness nights and any other opportunities that crop up.
summary: Our mission is to introduce mindfulness to students and support them in deepening their practice through a loving and inclusive community centered around mindfulness.

name: Vanderbilt Spikeball Club
description: Supported by the Vanderbilt Rec Center, the Spikeball club aims to give students the ability to learn, practice, and play the sport of roundnet. Recognizing both the competitive and casual aspects of the sport, the Vanderbilt Spikeball Club will host and attend tournaments while also providing a casual outlet to play a new game! No experience is necessary. Everyone interested in learning about this new sport is welcome!
summary: Vanderbilt Spikeball Club, supported by the Vanderbilt Rec Center, provides students the ability to play the sport of roundnet (Spikeball) with others in the Nashville community.

name: Challah for Hunger at Vanderbilt University
description: Come bake challah once a month, sell to the Vanderbilt community, and raise money for local and national hunger-fighting organizations!
summary: Baking challah to raise money for local and national hunger-fighting organizations

name: Biochemistry Postdoctoral Researcher and Student Association
description: The Biochemistry Postdoctoral Researcher and Student Association (BPSA) strives to create an environment where both graduate students and post-doctoral fellows have a base of support within the department.
summary: The Biochemistry Postdoctoral Researcher and Student Association (BPSA) strives to create an environment where both graduate students and post-doctoral fellows have a base of support within the department. We hope to accomplish this by enhancing the acad

name: Owen South Asian Business Club
description:
The South Asian Business Club is a great platform for collaboration among students, faculty and alumni who share a deep interest in the rich cultural heritage of South Asia. The objective of the SABC is to not only expose its members to the variety of cultures in the region but also to assist them in their professional development.

Professional Development: SABC aims to provide mentorship, networking, and recruiting support to its members and the Owen community. We will also arrange for guest speakers from various industries to talk about their success and struggles to help its members get a broader understanding of the business world.

Community Engagement: We will engage with other organizations in Owen and Vanderbilt to foster learning and collaboration on various business and social events and promote diversity and a sense of oneness.

Social Development: SABC stands to create awareness of Indian culture in the Owen Community and organize social events throughout the year recognizing Indian holidays and celebrating festivals.
summary: OSABC's mission is to encourage professional development, community engagement, and social development among prospective students, current students, faculty, and alumni who share an interest in topics related to the sub-continent region of South Asia.

name: Presidents' Roundtable
description: • The mission of the Presidents' Roundtable is to provide a space for campus leaders to dialogue about current issues, produce strategies for partnerships across organizations, and establish a vision for the academic school year in conjunction with Vanderbilt Student Government.
summary: The purpose of the Presidents’ Roundtable is to provide a platform for student organization presidents to network, collaborate, and help identify priorities for the Vanderbilt student community.

name: Undergraduate Business Minor
description:
Launched in the fall of 2017, the Undergraduate Business Minor is an opportunity for students to pursue a program that is “Uniquely Vanderbilt.” This trans-institutional program, with elective course options from each of the four undergraduate schools and fundamentals courses taught by the Owen Graduate School of Management, is designed to complement the strong liberal arts education.

The minor, in the spirit of the Academic Strategic Plan, offers students a wide variety of courses.

The structure of the minor is based in five Fundamentals courses led by Owen Graduate School of Management faculty. These courses include Organizational Behavior, Essentials of Financial Reporting, Principles of Finance, Managing Operations, and Principles of Marketing. These are half semester courses designed to provide students with a solid foundation of general business disciplines.

To address diverse student interests, students may select three electives from among more than 70 courses available through the four undergraduate schools.

Seven optional Business Pathways, or informal groupings of courses, exist should a student want to focus in a particular area within the minor. Students must pick at least two of their electives from one or more pathways. The pathways include:

•    Entrepreneurship
•    Ethics
•    Finance and Accounting
•    Marketing
•    Operations
•    Organizational Effectiveness
•    Strategy

Students may also count one elective from a unique group of “Business in Society” courses. These elective courses connect the minor to the liberal curriculum.

Students may enroll in two Fundamentals courses per module, allowing students to complete up to four Fundamentals courses in a semester.
summary: Launched in the fall of 2017, the Undergraduate Business Minor is an opportunity for students to pursue a program that is “Uniquely Vanderbilt.”

name: VUSM Student Technology Committee
description:
The Vanderbilt University School of Medicine (VUSM) Student Technology Committee (STC) is committed to bringing the student voice to clinical and curricular technologies. Our group unites the medical student perspective with technology acumen and excitement for improving the environment in which we learn.

As the official liaisons between medical students, school administration, and information technology staff, our primary role is to help guide the discussion and implementation of technologies in the academic and clinical environment. Examples of our work include introducing iPads into the gross anatomy lab, providing feedback on the VSTAR redesign, developing relationships with the engineering department, and maintaining the Technology Oar: a guide that helps medical students navigate the electronic health record. 

This organization is only open to current VUSM MD Students.
summary: The VUSM Student Technology Committee (VSTC) is composed of medical students with interests in technology. We serve as liaisons between students and administrators, foster interest in technology, and guide implementation of clinical and curricular tools.

name: Maymester Abroad
summary: This is an organization for those who are interested in traveling during the month of May.  Maymester is an opportunity to learn, travel and earn credit.  Join and visit this site often to keep up to date for the latest Maymester information.

name: Vanderbilt Green Fund
description:
OFFICIAL WEBSITE

The Vanderbilt Green Fund is a $150,000 annual fund set aside for sustainability projects proposed by students or faculty.  Members of SPEAR and VSG established the Green Fund in the spring of 2011. The Green Fund has grown since then and is now a subsection of SPEAR. We are looking for innovative and educational ideas that improve sustainability on campus. Take a look at our past projects!

 

Sign up for the Feb. 4th (3-5 PM) Green-a-thon! Learn more here.


Submit a Green Fund proposal by February 28th, 2022, 11:59 PM!

Proposal Overview/Guide (incl. proposal requirements)
Proposal Submit Form: proposal submission link

 

Read and learn more about the Vanderbilt Green Fund:

https://www.vanderbilt.edu/sustainability/what-we-do/vanderbilt-green-fund/
